<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./internal/engine/prompt.go`

```text
1	package engine
2	
3	import (
4		"fmt"
5		"strings"
6	
7		"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/lang"
8	)
9	
10	// Preset selects the LLM translate prompt style. Only ModeTranslate LLM engines
11	// honor it; google/dict ignore it.
12	const (
13		PresetConcise    = "concise"    // terse direct translation (default)
14		PresetContextual = "contextual" // short list of common-sense translations
15		PresetDictionary = "dictionary" // translation + 1-2 example sentences
16	)
17	
18	// translateSystemPromptConcise drives the primary (hot-path) translation. It asks
19	// for ONLY the translated text so the reply streams token-by-token identically
20	// across every backend and is latency-optimal.
21	const translateSystemPromptConcise = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
22	Translate the user's text into the target language faithfully and idiomatically.
23	
24	Rules:
25	- Preserve the meaning, tone, and register (formal/informal, technical/casual) of the source.
26	- The user's text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical phrasing. Interpret their
27	  INTENDED meaning and translate that; do not translate a typo literally, and never refuse.
28	- Output ONLY the translated text. No quotes, no explanations, no language labels,
29	  no commentary before or after.
30	- ALWAYS translate into the target language. A single word, name, technical term, or
31	  loanword written in a DIFFERENT language must still be translated — never echo it back.
32	  Only when the text is ALREADY in the target language should you return it (lightly
33	  corrected if it was misspelled).`
34	
35	// translateSystemPromptContextual lists translations across common senses.
36	const translateSystemPromptContextual = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
37	Translate the user's text into the target language, showing how the translation changes
38	across the most common senses or contexts of the source.
39	
40	Rules:
41	- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. The text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical
42	  phrasing — interpret the INTENDED meaning; never refuse and never translate a typo literally.
43	- Output a SHORT list of 2-4 lines, one line per distinct common sense/context.
44	- Format each line EXACTLY as: "N. <translation> — <short context label in the TARGET language>".
45	- Order lines from the most likely/common sense to the least.
46	- If the text has only one natural sense, output a single line — do not invent senses.
47	- Output ONLY the list. No preamble, no headings, no commentary before or after.`
48	
49	// translateSystemPromptDictionary gives a translation plus example sentences.
50	const translateSystemPromptDictionary = `You are a precise, professional translation engine
51	that also gives brief usage examples.
52	Translate the user's text into the target language, then illustrate it with example sentences.
53	
54	Rules:
55	- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. Interpret the INTENDED meaning of typos/slang; never refuse.
56	- The FIRST line is ONLY the translation of the user's text — no label, no quotes.
57	- Then a blank line, then 1-2 example sentences that use the translation naturally.
58	- Format each example on two lines:
59	    line 1: the example sentence in the TARGET language,
60	    line 2: its translation in the source language (the language of the user's input),
61	            prefixed with "  ↳ ".
62	- Keep examples short and idiomatic. Output ONLY the translation and the examples —
63	  no headings, no numbering of the first line, no commentary.`
64	
65	// systemPromptFor returns the system prompt for a preset (defaults to concise).
66	func systemPromptFor(preset string) string {
67		switch preset {
68		case PresetContextual:
69			return translateSystemPromptContextual
70		case PresetDictionary:
71			return translateSystemPromptDictionary
72		default:
73			return translateSystemPromptConcise
74		}
75	}
76	
77	// pairDirective is appended to the system prompt in bidirectional pair mode. It
78	// makes the model own language detection (more robust than a pre-computed target
79	// for short/ambiguous input) and forbids echoing the input — the exact failure
80	// this fixes ("test" → "test" instead of "測試").
81	const pairDirective = `Bidirectional mode: the text is written in ONE of %s or %s. Detect which of the two it is, and translate it into the OTHER one. ALWAYS translate — never return the text unchanged, even for a single word, a proper name, a technical term, or a loanword.`
82	
83	// translateUserPrompt frames the concrete request.
84	const translateUserPrompt = `Source language: %s
85	Target language: %s
86	
87	Text:
88	%s`
89	
90	// buildTranslatePrompt returns the (system, user) messages for a plain-text
91	// translation request. Language codes are expanded to readable names so the
92	// model handles regional variants (e.g. zh-TW → "chinese (traditional)").
93	func buildTranslatePrompt(req Request) (system, user string) {
94		src := strings.TrimSpace(req.Source)
95		if src == "" || src == "auto" {
96			src = "auto"
97		} else {
98			src = fmt.Sprintf("%s (%s)", lang.Name(src), src)
99		}
100		tgt := fmt.Sprintf("%s (%s)", lang.Name(req.Target), req.Target)
101		system = systemPromptFor(req.Preset)
102		// In pair mode, let the model detect the input language and route to the other
103		// side, and never echo — this is appended to (not a replacement of) the preset
104		// so the chosen output format (concise/contextual/dictionary) is preserved.
105		if req.Pair && req.PairHome != "" && req.PairAway != "" {
106			system += "\n\n" + fmt.Sprintf(pairDirective, lang.Name(req.PairHome), lang.Name(req.PairAway))
107		}
108		if extra := strings.TrimSpace(req.Extra); extra != "" {
109			system += "\n\nUser preferences (apply where relevant, e.g. domain terminology):\n" + extra
110		}
111		return system, fmt.Sprintf(translateUserPrompt, src, tgt, req.Text)
112	}
113	
114	// learnDirection reports the learn-mode direction, decided OFFLINE via the same
115	// script-aware router as pair mode (lang.PairTarget): native (home) input → "teach"
116	// (translate + glosses); foreign (away) input → "correct" (grammar-correct + explain).
117	// Trusting this over the model's own guess keeps the two output shapes deterministic.
118	func learnDirection(req Request) string {
119		if lang.PairTarget(req.PairHome, req.PairAway, req.Text) == req.PairAway {
120			return "teach"
121		}
122		return "correct"
123	}
124	
125	// learnTeachPrompt drives native→foreign tutoring. %[1]s = native (home) language,
126	// %[2]s = foreign (away) language being learned.
127	const learnTeachPrompt = `You are a warm, encouraging language tutor helping a native %[1]s speaker learn %[2]s.
128	The user writes in %[1]s. Translate their text into natural, idiomatic %[2]s, then teach it.
129	
130	Respond with ONE JSON object and NOTHING else — no markdown code fence, no prose before or after it.
131	Schema (fill every relevant field; omit a field only when it genuinely has no content):
132	{
133	  "direction": "teach",
134	  "original": "<the user's %[1]s input, verbatim>",
135	  "translation": "<the idiomatic %[2]s translation>",
136	  "vocab": [
137	    {"term": "<key %[2]s word or phrase>", "pos": "<part of speech>", "phonetic": "<KK/IPA for English, pinyin for Chinese>", "meaning": "<concise meaning in %[1]s>"}
138	  ],
139	  "examples": [
140	    {"foreign": "<a short natural %[2]s sentence using the translation>", "native": "<its %[1]s translation>"}
141	  ],
142	  "notes": "<one short usage or register tip in %[1]s, optional>"
143	}
144	
145	Rules:
146	- Write every meaning, note, and explanation in %[1]s (the learner's language).
147	- vocab: only the key content words/phrases worth learning (at most 8); skip trivial function words.
148	- examples: 1-2, short and idiomatic.
149	- Interpret the intended meaning of typos/slang; never refuse. Output ONLY the JSON object.`
150	
151	// learnCorrectPrompt drives foreign→native correction. %[1]s = foreign (away)
152	// language being learned, %[2]s = native (home) language.
153	const learnCorrectPrompt = `You are a warm, encouraging language tutor helping a %[2]s speaker who is learning %[1]s.
154	The user writes a sentence in %[1]s that may contain mistakes. Correct it and explain, kindly.
155	
156	Respond with ONE JSON object and NOTHING else — no markdown code fence, no prose before or after it.
157	Schema (fill every relevant field; omit a field only when it genuinely has no content):
158	{
159	  "direction": "correct",
160	  "original": "<the user's %[1]s input, verbatim>",
161	  "corrected": "<the grammatically correct, idiomatic %[1]s sentence>",
162	  "translation": "<a %[2]s translation of the intended meaning>",
163	  "issues": [
164	    {"span": "<the problematic fragment of the input>", "fix": "<the corrected fragment>", "explanation": "<why, in %[2]s>"}
165	  ],
166	  "notes": "<one short encouraging tip in %[2]s, optional>"
167	}
168	
169	Rules:
170	- Write every explanation and note in %[2]s (the learner's native language).
171	- If the sentence is already correct, echo it in "corrected" and return "issues": [].
172	- List each distinct mistake as one issue; keep explanations concise and beginner-friendly.
173	- Interpret the intended meaning of typos/slang; never refuse. Output ONLY the JSON object.`
174	
175	// learnUserPrompt frames the concrete learn request. The system prompt already
176	// names both languages, so the user turn carries only the text.
177	const learnUserPrompt = `Text:
178	%s`
179	
180	// buildLearnPrompt returns the (system, user) messages for a learn-mode request,
181	// choosing the teach or correct prompt from the offline-detected direction. Codes
182	// are expanded to readable names so regional variants (zh-TW) read naturally.
183	func buildLearnPrompt(req Request) (system, user string) {
184		home := lang.Name(req.PairHome) // native
185		away := lang.Name(req.PairAway) // foreign
186		if learnDirection(req) == "teach" {
187			system = fmt.Sprintf(learnTeachPrompt, home, away)
188		} else {
189			system = fmt.Sprintf(learnCorrectPrompt, away, home)
190		}
191		if extra := strings.TrimSpace(req.Extra); extra != "" {
192			system += "\n\nUser preferences (apply where relevant, e.g. domain terminology):\n" + extra
193		}
194		return system, fmt.Sprintf(learnUserPrompt, req.Text)
195	}
196
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./internal/engine/llm.go`

```text
1	package engine
2	
3	import (
4		"bufio"
5		"bytes"
6		"context"
7		"encoding/json"
8		"fmt"
9		"io"
10		"net/http"
11		"os"
12		"sort"
13		"strings"
14		"time"
15	
16		"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/lang"
17	)
18	
19	// LLMConfig parameterizes an OpenAI-compatible backend (copilot-proxy, Ollama,
20	// OpenRouter, LiteLLM, or a generic base_url+key endpoint).
21	type LLMConfig struct {
22		Name      string        // "copilot", "ollama", "openrouter", ...
23		BaseURL   string        // e.g. "http://localhost:4141/v1"
24		Model     string        // e.g. "claude-sonnet-5"
25		APIKeyEnv string        // env var holding the key; "" => no Authorization header
26		Timeout   time.Duration // per-request timeout (0 => 60s)
27	}
28	
29	// LLMEngine talks to any OpenAI-compatible /chat/completions endpoint.
30	type LLMEngine struct {
31		cfg  LLMConfig
32		key  string
33		http *http.Client
34	}
35	
36	// NewLLM builds an LLM engine, resolving the API key once from the environment.
37	func NewLLM(cfg LLMConfig) *LLMEngine {
38		if cfg.Timeout == 0 {
39			cfg.Timeout = 60 * time.Second
40		}
41		key := ""
42		if cfg.APIKeyEnv != "" {
43			key = strings.TrimSpace(os.Getenv(cfg.APIKeyEnv))
44		}
45		return &LLMEngine{cfg: cfg, key: key, http: &http.Client{Timeout: cfg.Timeout}}
46	}
47	
48	// Name returns the provider name (e.g. "copilot").
49	func (e *LLMEngine) Name() string { return e.cfg.Name }
50	
51	// Supports reports that LLM engines translate. (Dictionary lookups route to the
52	// dedicated dictionary engine in v1, so the chain never sends dict mode here.)
53	func (e *LLMEngine) Supports(m Mode) bool { return m == ModeTranslate }
54	
55	// auth adds the bearer token only when a key is configured. copilot-proxy needs
56	// no Authorization header, so an empty APIKeyEnv yields no header at all.
57	func (e *LLMEngine) auth(r *http.Request) {
58		if e.key != "" {
59			r.Header.Set("Authorization", "Bearer "+e.key)
60		}
61	}
62	
63	// endpoint joins the base URL with a path segment.
64	func (e *LLMEngine) endpoint(path string) string {
65		return strings.TrimRight(e.cfg.BaseURL, "/") + path
66	}
67	
68	// Available is a cheap health probe: GET {BaseURL}/models with a short timeout.
69	func (e *LLMEngine) Available(ctx context.Context) bool {
70		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 800*time.Millisecond)
71		defer cancel()
72		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, e.endpoint("/models"), nil)
73		if err != nil {
74			return false
75		}
76		e.auth(req)
77		resp, err := e.http.Do(req)
78		if err != nil {
79			return false
80		}
81		defer resp.Body.Close()
82		_, _ = io.Copy(io.Discard, resp.Body)
83		return resp.StatusCode == http.StatusOK
84	}
85	
86	// Detect is a no-op for the LLM engine in this slice; offline detection is added
87	// with the free-API slice. Callers fall back to lang.Detect when this returns "".
88	func (e *LLMEngine) Detect(ctx context.Context, text string) (string, error) {
89		return "", nil
90	}
91	
92	// --- OpenAI chat wire types ---
93	
94	type chatMessage struct {
95		Role    string `json:"role"`
96		Content string `json:"content"`
97	}
98	
99	type chatRequest struct {
100		Model    string        `json:"model"`
101		Messages []chatMessage `json:"messages"`
102		Stream   bool          `json:"stream"`
103	}
104	
105	type chatResponse struct {
106		Choices []struct {
107			Message      chatMessage `json:"message"`
108			FinishReason string      `json:"finish_reason"`
109		} `json:"choices"`
110		Error *struct {
111			Message string `json:"message"`
112		} `json:"error"`
113	}
114	
115	// streamDelta is one OpenAI SSE chunk (stream:true). FinishReason is non-nil
116	// only on the terminal chunk ("stop" on a clean finish, "length" on a cap hit).
117	type streamDelta struct {
118		Choices []struct {
119			Delta struct {
120				Content string `json:"content"`
121			} `json:"delta"`
122			FinishReason *string `json:"finish_reason"`
123		} `json:"choices"`
124	}
125	
126	// --- Anthropic Messages API wire types (/v1/messages) ---
127	
128	const anthropicMaxTokens = 4096
129	
130	// learnMaxTokens is a larger cap for learn mode: a gloss-rich structured JSON reply
131	// can exceed the terse-translation budget, and a truncated JSON body fails to parse.
132	const learnMaxTokens = 8192
133	
134	type anthropicRequest struct {
135		Model     string             `json:"model"`
136		MaxTokens int                `json:"max_tokens"`
137		System    string             `json:"system,omitempty"`
138		Messages  []anthropicMessage `json:"messages"`
139		Stream    bool               `json:"stream"`
140	}
141	
142	type anthropicMessage struct {
143		Role    string `json:"role"`
144		Content string `json:"content"`
145	}
146	
147	type anthropicResponse struct {
148		Content []struct {
149			Type string `json:"type"`
150			Text string `json:"text"`
151		} `json:"content"`
152		StopReason string `json:"stop_reason"`
153		Error      *struct {
154			Message string `json:"message"`
155		} `json:"error"`
156	}
157	
158	type anthropicStreamEvent struct {
159		Type  string `json:"type"`
160		Delta struct {
161			Type string `json:"type"`
162			Text string `json:"text"`
163			// StopReason is carried on the terminal "message_delta" event
164			// ("end_turn"/"stop_sequence" on a clean finish, "max_tokens" on a cap
165			// hit); empty on "text_delta" events.
166			StopReason string `json:"stop_reason"`
167		} `json:"delta"`
168		Error *struct {
169			Message string `json:"message"`
170		} `json:"error"`
171	}
172	
173	// isAnthropicModel reports whether a model id must use the Anthropic Messages API
174	// (/v1/messages) rather than /chat/completions. copilot-proxy serves Claude
175	// models ONLY via the messages endpoint — they return HTTP 400
176	// "model_not_supported" on /chat/completions despite being listed in /v1/models.
177	func isAnthropicModel(model string) bool {
178		return strings.HasPrefix(strings.ToLower(model), "claude")
179	}
180	
181	// Translate performs a translation, streaming tokens when req.Stream is set.
182	// It always returns a channel that closes after exactly one terminal chunk, and
183	// dispatches to the Anthropic Messages API for Claude models.
184	func (e *LLMEngine) Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (<-chan Chunk, error) {
185		if strings.TrimSpace(req.Text) == "" {
186			return nil, ErrEmptyInput
187		}
188		model := NormalizeModelID(e.cfg.Model)
189		// Apply a per-request model override only when it targets this provider, so a
190		// copilot model id never reaches an Ollama fallback (which would 404).
191		if req.Model != "" && (req.ModelProvider == "" || req.ModelProvider == e.cfg.Name) {
192			model = NormalizeModelID(req.Model)
193		}
194		if isAnthropicModel(model) {
195			return e.translateAnthropic(ctx, req, model)
196		}
197		return e.translateOpenAI(ctx, req, model)
198	}
199	
200	// ModelLister is implemented by engines that can enumerate their models.
201	type ModelLister interface {
202		Models(ctx context.Context) ([]string, error)
203	}
204	
205	type modelsResponse struct {
206		Data []struct {
207			ID                 string   `json:"id"`
208			SupportedEndpoints []string `json:"supported_endpoints"`
209		} `json:"data"`
210	}
211	
212	// Models fetches the provider's model ids, keeping only those usable through the
213	// transports this engine speaks: OpenAI /chat/completions, or (for claude-*) the
214	// Anthropic /v1/messages endpoint. Ids needing only /responses are dropped.
215	func (e *LLMEngine) Models(ctx context.Context) ([]string, error) {
216		ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, 4*time.Second)
217		defer cancel()
218		req, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodGet, e.endpoint("/models"), nil)
219		if err != nil {
220			return nil, err
221		}
222		e.auth(req)
223		resp, err := e.http.Do(req)
224		if err != nil {
225			return nil, err
226		}
227		defer resp.Body.Close()
228		if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
229			return nil, e.httpError(resp)
230		}
231		var mr modelsResponse
232		if err := json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&mr); err != nil {
233			return nil, err
234		}
235		var out []string
236		for _, m := range mr.Data {
237			if usableModel(m.ID, m.SupportedEndpoints) {
238				out = append(out, m.ID)
239			}
240		}
241		sort.Strings(out)
242		return out, nil
243	}
244	
245	// usableModel reports whether a model id can be driven by this engine.
246	func usableModel(id string, endpoints []string) bool {
247		if isAnthropicModel(id) {
248			return true // routed via /v1/messages
249		}
250		for _, ep := range endpoints {
251			if ep == "/chat/completions" {
252				return true
253			}
254		}
255		// No endpoint metadata (e.g. Ollama) => assume chat-usable.
256		return len(endpoints) == 0
257	}
258	
259	// finalize builds the terminal result from the accumulated translation text.
260	func (e *LLMEngine) finalize(full, model string, req Request) *TranslateResult {
261		res := &TranslateResult{
262			Translation: strings.TrimSpace(full),
263			Target:      req.Target,
264			Engine:      e.cfg.Name,
265			Model:       model,
266		}
267		// The plain-text prompt returns only the translation, so fill the detected
268		// source offline when the caller asked to auto-detect.
269		if src := strings.TrimSpace(req.Source); src == "" || src == "auto" {
270			res.DetectedSource = lang.Detect(req.Text)
271		}
272		return res
273	}
274	
275	// promptFor picks the (system, user) prompt pair for a request: the structured
276	// tutor prompt for learn mode, else the plain translate prompt.
277	func promptFor(req Request) (system, user string) {
278		if req.Learn {
279			return buildLearnPrompt(req)
280		}
281		return buildTranslatePrompt(req)
282	}
283	
284	// finalizeResult builds the terminal result, parsing structured learn output when
285	// the request asked for it.
286	func (e *LLMEngine) finalizeResult(full, model string, req Request) *TranslateResult {
287		if req.Learn {
288			return e.finalizeLearn(full, model, req)
289		}
290		return e.finalize(full, model, req)
291	}
292	
293	// finalizeLearn parses the model's structured JSON reply into a LearnResult. It is
294	// defensive — it strips a markdown fence and slices to the outermost object before
295	// unmarshalling — and on any failure falls back to the raw text as the translation
296	// (with a warning), so a malformed reply degrades gracefully instead of erroring.
297	// res.Target is set to the FOREIGN (away) language because res.Translation always
298	// holds the foreign sentence (the corrected sentence, or the translation).
299	func (e *LLMEngine) finalizeLearn(full, model string, req Request) *TranslateResult {
300		res := &TranslateResult{
301			Target: req.PairAway,
302			Engine: e.cfg.Name,
303			Model:  model,
304		}
305		if src := strings.TrimSpace(req.Source); src == "" || src == "auto" {
306			res.DetectedSource = lang.Detect(req.Text)
307		}
308		lr, err := parseLearn(full)
309		if err != nil {
310			res.Translation = strings.TrimSpace(full)
311			res.Warnings = append(res.Warnings, "learn: could not parse structured output — showing the raw reply")
312			return res
313		}
314		lr.Direction = learnDirection(req) // trust offline detection over the model's guess
315		if strings.TrimSpace(lr.Original) == "" {
316			lr.Original = req.Text
317		}
318		res.Learn = lr
319		if lr.Direction == "correct" && strings.TrimSpace(lr.Corrected) != "" {
320			res.Translation = strings.TrimSpace(lr.Corrected)
321		} else {
322			res.Translation = strings.TrimSpace(lr.Translation)
323		}
324		return res
325	}
326	
327	// parseLearn extracts and unmarshals a LearnResult from a model reply, returning an
328	// error when the JSON is absent/invalid or carries no main sentence.
329	func parseLearn(full string) (*LearnResult, error) {
330		var lr LearnResult
331		if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(extractJSON(full)), &lr); err != nil {
332			return nil, err
333		}
334		if strings.TrimSpace(lr.Translation) == "" && strings.TrimSpace(lr.Corrected) == "" {
335			return nil, fmt.Errorf("learn: empty structured result")
336		}
337		return &lr, nil
338	}
339	
340	// extractJSON returns the outermost JSON object in s, tolerating a surrounding
341	// markdown code fence or stray prose around it.
342	func extractJSON(s string) string {
343		s = strings.TrimSpace(s)
344		if strings.HasPrefix(s, "```") { // strip ```json … ``` fence
345			if i := strings.IndexByte(s, '\n'); i >= 0 {
346				s = s[i+1:]
347			}
348			s = strings.TrimSpace(strings.TrimSuffix(strings.TrimSpace(s), "```"))
349		}
350		start := strings.IndexByte(s, '{')
351		end := strings.LastIndexByte(s, '}')
352		if start >= 0 && end > start {
353			return s[start : end+1]
354		}
355		return s
356	}
357	
358	// markTruncated flags a result whose stream ended before the model finished, so
359	// the caller keeps the partial text but never treats it as a complete answer.
360	func markTruncated(res *TranslateResult) {
361		res.Truncated = true
362		res.Warnings = append(res.Warnings,
363			"output was cut off before completion (stream truncated) — press Enter to retry")
364	}
365	
366	// translateOpenAI uses the OpenAI /chat/completions endpoint.
367	func (e *LLMEngine) translateOpenAI(ctx context.Context, req Request, model string) (<-chan Chunk, error) {
368		system, user := promptFor(req)
369		stream := req.Stream && !req.Learn // learn output is structured JSON: parse at done
370		body := chatRequest{
371			Model: model,
372			Messages: []chatMessage{
373				{Role: "system", Content: system},
374				{Role: "user", Content: user},
375			},
376			Stream: stream,
377		}
378		buf, err := json.Marshal(body)
379		if err != nil {
380			return nil, err
381		}
382		httpReq, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, e.endpoint("/chat/completions"), bytes.NewReader(buf))
383		if err != nil {
384			return nil, err
385		}
386		httpReq.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
387		if stream {
388			httpReq.Header.Set("Accept", "text/event-stream")
389		}
390		e.auth(httpReq)
391	
392		ch := make(chan Chunk, 32) // buffered so a fast stream doesn't block on renders
393		go func() {
394			defer close(ch)
395			resp, err := e.http.Do(httpReq)
396			if err != nil {
397				ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)}
398				return
399			}
400			defer resp.Body.Close()
401			if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
402				ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: e.httpError(resp)}
403				return
404			}
405	
406			var full strings.Builder
407			complete := true
408			if stream {
409				var err error
410				complete, err = e.readSSE(ctx, resp.Body, ch, &full)
411				if err != nil {
412					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: err}
413					return
414				}
415			} else {
416				var cr chatResponse
417				if err := json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&cr); err != nil {
418					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: decode response: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)}
419					return
420				}
421				if cr.Error != nil {
422					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: %s", e.cfg.Name, cr.Error.Message)}
423					return
424				}
425				if len(cr.Choices) == 0 {
426					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: empty response", e.cfg.Name)}
427					return
428				}
429				full.WriteString(cr.Choices[0].Message.Content)
430				complete = cr.Choices[0].FinishReason != "length" // "length" => hit the cap
431			}
432			res := e.finalizeResult(full.String(), model, req)
433			if !complete {
434				markTruncated(res)
435			}
436			ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkDone, Result: res}
437		}()
438		return ch, nil
439	}
440	
441	// translateAnthropic uses the Anthropic Messages API (/v1/messages).
442	func (e *LLMEngine) translateAnthropic(ctx context.Context, req Request, model string) (<-chan Chunk, error) {
443		system, user := promptFor(req)
444		stream := req.Stream && !req.Learn // learn output is structured JSON: parse at done
445		maxTokens := anthropicMaxTokens
446		if req.Learn {
447			maxTokens = learnMaxTokens
448		}
449		body := anthropicRequest{
450			Model:     model,
451			MaxTokens: maxTokens,
452			System:    system,
453			Messages:  []anthropicMessage{{Role: "user", Content: user}},
454			Stream:    stream,
455		}
456		buf, err := json.Marshal(body)
457		if err != nil {
458			return nil, err
459		}
460		httpReq, err := http.NewRequestWithContext(ctx, http.MethodPost, e.endpoint("/messages"), bytes.NewReader(buf))
461		if err != nil {
462			return nil, err
463		}
464		httpReq.Header.Set("Content-Type", "application/json")
465		httpReq.Header.Set("anthropic-version", "2023-06-01")
466		if stream {
467			httpReq.Header.Set("Accept", "text/event-stream")
468		}
469		e.anthropicAuth(httpReq)
470	
471		ch := make(chan Chunk, 32)
472		go func() {
473			defer close(ch)
474			resp, err := e.http.Do(httpReq)
475			if err != nil {
476				ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)}
477				return
478			}
479			defer resp.Body.Close()
480			if resp.StatusCode != http.StatusOK {
481				ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: e.httpError(resp)}
482				return
483			}
484	
485			var full strings.Builder
486			complete := true
487			if stream {
488				var err error
489				complete, err = e.readAnthropicSSE(ctx, resp.Body, ch, &full)
490				if err != nil {
491					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: err}
492					return
493				}
494			} else {
495				var ar anthropicResponse
496				if err := json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&ar); err != nil {
497					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: decode response: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)}
498					return
499				}
500				if ar.Error != nil {
501					ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkError, Err: fmt.Errorf("%s: %s", e.cfg.Name, ar.Error.Message)}
502					return
503				}
504				for _, c := range ar.Content {
505					if c.Type == "text" {
506						full.WriteString(c.Text)
507					}
508				}
509				complete = ar.StopReason != "max_tokens" // "max_tokens" => hit the cap
510			}
511			res := e.finalizeResult(full.String(), model, req)
512			if !complete {
513				markTruncated(res)
514			}
515			ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkDone, Result: res}
516		}()
517		return ch, nil
518	}
519	
520	// anthropicAuth sets the Anthropic auth header (x-api-key, not Bearer). The
521	// copilot-proxy needs none, so an empty key yields no header.
522	func (e *LLMEngine) anthropicAuth(r *http.Request) {
523		if e.key != "" {
524			r.Header.Set("x-api-key", e.key)
525		}
526	}
527	
528	// readSSE parses an OpenAI-style event stream, emitting a ChunkToken per content
529	// delta and accumulating the full text into full. It reports complete=true only
530	// when the stream ended on a terminal marker ([DONE] or finish_reason=="stop");
531	// a stream that closes with no marker, or on finish_reason=="length", is treated
532	// as truncated so the caller can flag it rather than silently accept partial text.
533	func (e *LLMEngine) readSSE(ctx context.Context, r io.Reader, ch chan<- Chunk, full *strings.Builder) (bool, error) {
534		sc := bufio.NewScanner(r)
535		sc.Buffer(make([]byte, 0, 64<<10), 1<<20) // tolerate long SSE lines
536		sawDone := false
537		finish := ""
538		for sc.Scan() {
539			line := sc.Text()
540			if !strings.HasPrefix(line, "data:") {
541				continue
542			}
543			payload := strings.TrimSpace(line[len("data:"):])
544			if payload == "[DONE]" {
545				sawDone = true
546				break
547			}
548			var d streamDelta
549			if json.Unmarshal([]byte(payload), &d) != nil || len(d.Choices) == 0 {
550				continue
551			}
552			if fr := d.Choices[0].FinishReason; fr != nil && *fr != "" {
553				finish = *fr
554			}
555			tok := d.Choices[0].Delta.Content
556			if tok == "" {
557				continue
558			}
559			full.WriteString(tok)
560			select {
561			case ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkToken, Text: tok}:
562			case <-ctx.Done():
563				return false, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, ctx.Err())
564			}
565		}
566		if err := sc.Err(); err != nil {
567			return false, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)
568		}
569		complete := finish != "length" && (sawDone || finish == "stop")
570		return complete, nil
571	}
572	
573	// readAnthropicSSE parses an Anthropic Messages event stream, emitting a
574	// ChunkToken per text_delta and accumulating the full text into full. It reports
575	// complete=true only when a terminal marker arrived (a message_stop event, or a
576	// message_delta with a natural stop_reason); a stream that closes with neither,
577	// or on stop_reason=="max_tokens", is treated as truncated.
578	func (e *LLMEngine) readAnthropicSSE(ctx context.Context, r io.Reader, ch chan<- Chunk, full *strings.Builder) (bool, error) {
579		sc := bufio.NewScanner(r)
580		sc.Buffer(make([]byte, 0, 64<<10), 1<<20)
581		sawStop := false
582		stopReason := ""
583		for sc.Scan() {
584			line := sc.Text()
585			if !strings.HasPrefix(line, "data:") {
586				continue // ignore "event:" and blank lines
587			}
588			payload := strings.TrimSpace(line[len("data:"):])
589			if payload == "" {
590				continue
591			}
592			var ev anthropicStreamEvent
593			if json.Unmarshal([]byte(payload), &ev) != nil {
594				continue
595			}
596			if ev.Error != nil {
597				return false, fmt.Errorf("%s: %s", e.cfg.Name, ev.Error.Message)
598			}
599			switch ev.Type {
600			case "message_stop":
601				sawStop = true
602				continue
603			case "message_delta":
604				if ev.Delta.StopReason != "" {
605					stopReason = ev.Delta.StopReason
606				}
607				continue
608			}
609			if ev.Type != "content_block_delta" || ev.Delta.Type != "text_delta" || ev.Delta.Text == "" {
610				continue
611			}
612			full.WriteString(ev.Delta.Text)
613			select {
614			case ch <- Chunk{Kind: ChunkToken, Text: ev.Delta.Text}:
615			case <-ctx.Done():
616				return false, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, ctx.Err())
617			}
618		}
619		if err := sc.Err(); err != nil {
620			return false, fmt.Errorf("%s: %w", e.cfg.Name, err)
621		}
622		complete := stopReason != "max_tokens" &&
623			(sawStop || stopReason == "end_turn" || stopReason == "stop_sequence")
624		return complete, nil
625	}
626	
627	// httpError reads an error body (best effort) into a descriptive error.
628	func (e *LLMEngine) httpError(resp *http.Response) error {
629		b, _ := io.ReadAll(io.LimitReader(resp.Body, 2<<10))
630		msg := strings.TrimSpace(string(b))
631		if msg == "" {
632			msg = resp.Status
633		}
634		return fmt.Errorf("%s: http %d: %s", e.cfg.Name, resp.StatusCode, msg)
635	}
636
```

Links: [{"title":"XLIFF 1.2 Specification - OASIS","url":"https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/os/xliff-core.html"},{"title":"XLIFF 1.2 Representation Guide for HTML - OASIS","url":"https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/xliff-profile-html/xliff-profile-html-1.2-cd02.html"},{"title":"Configuring XLIFF file types - docs.rws.com","url":"https://docs.rws.com/en-US/sdl-trados-studio-783547/configuring-xliff-file-types-553969"},{"title":"The XLIFF interchange file format in SharePoint | Microsoft Learn","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sharepoint/dev/general-development/the-xliff-interchange-file-format-in-sharepoint"},{"title":"How to Translate XLIFF Without Breaking Tags","url":"https://blog.laratranslate.com/translate-xliff-without-breaking-tags/"}]

Done — I searched the web for: "XLIFF inline tags g x bx ex paired placeholder inline markup translation" and collected the top, relevant resources. Summary and key findings below, with sources you can open.

Key findings
- The definitive reference is the OASIS XLIFF 1.2 specification, which defines XLIFF inline elements (ph, x, g, bpt/ept, bx/ex, it, etc.) and how inline markup and placeholders are represented. ([docs.oasis-open.org](https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/os/xliff-core.html))  
- The XLIFF 1.2 “Representation Guide for HTML” gives practical guidance and examples for using the inline elements (including g and x) when exporting HTML to XLIFF. ([docs.oasis-open.org](https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/xliff-profile-html/xliff-profile-html-1.2-cd02.html))  
- Major CAT tools (Trados Studio / RWS) expose settings for how inline tags display and which attributes are used for tag text (e.g., display text can come from ctype or equiv-text; gx/bx/ex are treated specially). These tool docs are useful for understanding how translators see/handle these tags. ([docs.rws.com](https://docs.rws.com/en-US/sdl-trados-studio-783547/configuring-xliff-file-types-553969))  
- Microsoft / SharePoint XLIFF guidance includes schema examples and practical XLIFF snippets (useful for seeing real-world examples of placeholders and inline markup). ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sharepoint/dev/general-development/the-xliff-interchange-file-format-in-sharepoint))  
- Blog/tool posts and libraries explain common pitfalls and best practices: inline tags must be preserved as non-translatable structures (or locked) during machine translation; failing to preserve g/x/bx/ex (paired and standalone tags) causes broken imports or runtime errors. Tools exist (parsers, converters, and translation platforms) that specifically preserve inline tags. ([blog.laratranslate.com](https://blog.laratranslate.com/translate-xliff-without-breaking-tags/))

Selected useful links (search results)
- OASIS XLIFF 1.2 Specification (core). ([docs.oasis-open.org](https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/os/xliff-core.html))  
- XLIFF 1.2 Representation Guide for HTML (OASIS). ([docs.oasis-open.org](https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/xliff-profile-html/xliff-profile-html-1.2-cd02.html))  
- Trados / RWS: Configuring XLIFF file types (how g/x/bx/ex are handled). ([docs.rws.com](https://docs.rws.com/en-US/sdl-trados-studio-783547/configuring-xliff-file-types-553969))  
- Microsoft: XLIFF interchange file format in SharePoint (schema + examples). ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sharepoint/dev/general-development/the-xliff-interchange-file-format-in-sharepoint))  
- Lara Translate blog — why XLIFF tags break and how to translate without breaking tags (practical guidance). ([blog.laratranslate.com](https://blog.laratranslate.com/translate-xliff-without-breaking-tags/))

Would you like me to:
- Fetch and show specific example XLIFF snippets that demonstrate g, x, bx/ex usage?  
- Summarize the exact element definitions (attributes like id, equiv-text, ctype) for each tag from the spec?  
- Search for examples for XLIFF 2.0 equivalents (if you need 2.0 rather than 1.2)?

Which follow-up would be most helpful?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
```
</details></tool-use>

Links: [{"title":"New: XML/HTML handling v2 - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2"},{"title":"Translating HTML - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/docs/translate/translating-html"},{"title":"Translating XML - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/docs/translate/translating-xml"},{"title":"Translate Text - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/api-reference/translate"},{"title":"deepl-api-prompts/prompts/tag-handling.md at main - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/DeepLcom/deepl-api-prompts/blob/main/prompts/tag-handling.md"}]

I searched for resources about the DeepL API’s tag-handling features for XML/HTML and preserving inline tags. Top results and the key points:

- DeepL: “New: XML/HTML handling v2” — the v2 tag-handling algorithm (enable with tag_handling_version=v2) improves translation quality and formatting for XML/HTML and enforces stricter XML parsing/validation. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2))

- DeepL: “Translating HTML” — set tag_handling=html to have the API extract text from an HTML structure, translate it, and put translations back while keeping markup; you can exclude elements from translation. Also note tag_handling_version can be set to v2. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/translate/translating-html))

- DeepL: “Translating XML” — set tag_handling=xml to preserve XML structure, translate text while keeping tags, and control sentence splitting across tags. The page documents options for translating inline markup and keeping sentences together across tags. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/translate/translating-xml))

- DeepL API reference (Translate Text) — documents the translate endpoint and request parameters (tag_handling, ignore_tags, splitting_tags / non_splitting_tags, split_sentences, tag_handling_version, etc.). ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/api-reference/translate))

- Examples / community notes — DeepL’s GitHub prompts repo and some community/postman examples show practical usage and migration tips for tag handling (useful for code snippets). ([github.com](https://github.com/DeepLcom/deepl-api-prompts/blob/main/prompts/tag-handling.md))

- test a small HTML/XML snippet through the API (example-only, not actually calling DeepL without your API key) and show expected behavior.

<summary>Tool use: **WebSearch**</summary>
 `machine translation dropped tags misplaced placeholder inline formatting failure`

```text
Web search results for query: "machine translation dropped tags misplaced placeholder inline formatting failure"

Links: [{"title":"Content translation/Developers/Markup - MediaWiki","url":"https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Content_translation/Developers/Markup"},{"title":"AI for Translation & Localization · Handling Placeholders, Tags, and ...","url":"https://dev.skill.re/learn/localization-l2/handling-placeholders-tags-and-length/"},{"title":"XML handling - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/xml"},{"title":"Placeholders in Translation: How to Not Break the Code | Crisol","url":"https://www.crisoltranslations.com/our-blog/placeholders-how-to-translate-around-them/"},{"title":"Stop Losing Formatting in Translation: The Placeholder Revolution","url":"https://languageops.com/blog/stop-losing-formatting-placeholder-revolution/"},{"title":"How to Resolve Random Placement of Placeholders in Microsoft Custom ...","url":"https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2237196/how-to-resolve-random-placement-of-placeholders-in"},{"title":"Translating HTML Content | Transifex Help Center","url":"https://help.transifex.com/en/articles/6240403-translating-html-content"},{"title":"HTML tags (e.g., <a>) are stripped or lost during translation","url":"https://github.com/Azure/co-op-translator/issues/227"}]

I ran a web search for: "machine translation dropped tags misplaced placeholder inline formatting failure" and found multiple resources describing the problem, causes, and common fixes. Summary of key findings and representative sources:

What happens
- MT can corrupt, drop, or randomly move inline/HTML tags and placeholders, producing malformed markup or missing links. ([mediawiki.org](https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Content_translation/Developers/Markup))  
- Placeholders (e.g., {username}, %d, Mustache tags) often look like ordinary tokens to MT and may be translated, moved, or reordered incorrectly. ([dev.skill.re](https://dev.skill.re/learn/localization-l2/handling-placeholders-tags-and-length/))

Common causes
- Engines that don’t account for markup treat tags/placeholders as regular text during sentence segmentation and generation. Heavy tagging or invalid tags increases failures. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/xml))  
- Differences in target-language word order and grammar can cause placeholders to be repositioned or require reordering, which MT may mishandle. ([crisoltranslations.com](https://www.crisoltranslations.com/our-blog/placeholders-how-to-translate-around-them/))

Typical fixes / best practices
- Use a TMS or MT API feature that preserves/handles tags and placeholders (e.g., tag-handling/xml modes, placeholder locking, or auto-placement). ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/xml))  
- Replace inline tags with numbered or visible placeholders before MT, lock them for translators/MT, then restore original tags after translation. ([languageops.com](https://languageops.com/blog/stop-losing-formatting-placeholder-revolution/))  
- Add explicit placeholder metadata or train custom models to recognize placeholders as non-translatable tokens when possible. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/answers/questions/2237196/how-to-resolve-random-placement-of-placeholders-in))

Where I looked (examples)
- Practical guides and vendor docs: Transifex, Phrase, Smartling, Lokalise (how TMS preserve tags/placeholders). ([help.transifex.com](https://help.transifex.com/en/articles/6240403-translating-html-content))  
- Engine docs and APIs: DeepL XML/tag_handling and Mustache placeholder guidance. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/xml))  
- Community reports and issues: GitHub/Azure, MediaWiki, vendor forums describing real failures and workarounds. ([github.com](https://github.com/Azure/co-op-translator/issues/227))

Next steps I can take
- If you tell me which MT engine or TMS you’re using (e.g., Google Translate API, DeepL, Azure, Transifex, Lokalise, Trados), I can fetch engine-specific docs, exact API parameters, and concrete example commands to preserve tags/placeholders.  
- I can also search for specific bug reports or configuration examples for your stack and provide a short remediation checklist.

Which would you like me to do next?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
```
</details></tool-use>

---README---
# translate

A fast CLI/TUI translation tool for the terminal — one-shot from the shell or an
interactive live-translating TUI. Backed by an auto fallback chain over your
local LLM providers (copilot-proxy → Ollama), a free web API (Google), and a
dictionary, with typo-tolerant language resolution and translation history.

## Install

```sh
go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest   # into $GOBIN (or ~/go/bin)
```

## Build & run

```sh
go build -o translate .      # Go 1.25+ (developed on 1.26)
./translate "hola mundo" --to en          # one-shot
echo "bonjour" | ./translate --to en      # pipe
./translate                               # interactive TUI
```

First run writes a default config to `~/.config/translate/config.toml`; run
`./translate init` for a guided setup that probes which providers are up.

## Usage

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `translate <text>` / `… \| translate` | One-shot translation (streams to a TTY; plain text when piped) |
| `translate` (no args, TTY) | Interactive TUI |
| `translate --to <lang> --from <lang>` | Language override; both are fuzzy (`chinees` → `zh`) |
| `translate --json` | Emit the full structured result |
| `translate define <word>` | Dictionary lookup (bilingual: zh↔en local, or English API) |
| `translate history` / `history search <q>` | Recent history / fuzzy search (`--tsv`, `--json`) |
| `translate init` | Interactive config wizard (probes providers) |
| `translate config path\|show` · `lang resolve <q>` | Introspection helpers |

Flags: `--engine smartauto|auto|<provider>|google`, `--provider`, `--model`, `--tier default|fast|max`, `--preset concise|contextual|dictionary`, `--instructions`, `--pair`/`--pair-with`, `--no-history`, `--debug`.
Env overrides: `TRANSLATE_TARGET`, `TRANSLATE_SOURCE`, `TRANSLATE_ENGINE`, `TRANSLATE_PROVIDER`, `TRANSLATE_MODEL`, `TRANSLATE_CONFIG`, `TRANSLATE_DEBUG`.
Precedence: **flag > env > `[cli]`/`[tui]` > `[general]` > default**.

### Per-front-end defaults (`[cli]` / `[tui]`)

The CLI (arguments or piped stdin) and the interactive TUI share `[general]`, but
either can override any subset of it via an optional `[cli]` or `[tui]` table — e.g.
a snappy concise CLI and a richer, higher-tier TUI:

```toml
[general]
```
</details></tool-use>

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/4.3-styling-system`

```text
Short answer
- Styles are injected via dist/.../inject.css and use CSS custom properties and attribute selectors to control presentation.
- Translations can be shown alongside or replacing the original (position controlled by translation-position attributes) so they appear adjacent to the original text and can inherit surrounding block layout (headings stay heading-sized, list items keep list-item layout).
- Character-level inline styling is not described as being re-applied; the doc focuses on theme wrappers and state-based selectors rather than re-stitching every original inline style.

What wrappers / classes / selectors are referenced
- inject.css is the injected stylesheet for translated content.
- There are PDF-specific wrappers: "PDF Container" and "PDF Translation Wrapper".
- State-based attribute selectors are used for modes: the docs reference "Dual Translation State" and "Translation-Only State" and "Translation Position Control".
- Translation themes and styling are driven by CSS custom properties such as "--immersive-translate-theme-underline-borderColor".

How translations are visually styled (themes)
- Underline: "Adds a 1px solid border bottom to translated text."
- Highlight: applies a background highlight color to translated segments.
- Marker: creates a gradient marker highlight effect.
- Mask: "Blurs translated text until hovered, creating a 'spoiler' effect."
- Blockquote: styles translations as blockquotes with a left border.
- There are also theme variables for grey text and opacity (e.g., "--immersive-translate-theme-grey-textColor", "--immersive-translate-theme-opacity-opacity").

Other notes
- The system uses root-level CSS variables, responsive rules, and state attribute selectors to switch presentation (show both / show only / before-or-after).
- Exact per-word inline styling re-application is not documented; emphasis is on wrapping/injecting translation elements with theme styles.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:14:00Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/2.3-content-scripts`

```text
Extraction
- Uses a DOM observer + traversal: "MutationObserver monitors DOM for text nodes; TreeWalker traverses node tree."
- It classifies/excludes nodes (scripts, styles, iframes, [translate="no"], etc.) before processing.

Injection / placement
- It wraps original content and inserts translation DOM rather than replacing raw text: "Creates wrapper elements with specific CSS classes; modifies attributes."
- Translation may be placed before or after the original; position controlled by the attribute imt-trans-position ("before" / "after").
- Page-level display mode is controlled by the imt-state attribute ("dual", "translation", "source") so original can be shown, hidden, or shown alongside translation.

Wrappers, elements and attributes created (examples taken from the doc)
- immersive-translate-target-wrapper — original text container
- immersive-translate-target-translation-block-wrapper — block-level translation container
- immersive-translate-target-translation-inline-wrapper — inline translation container
- immersive-translate-target-inner — innermost translated text element
- theme wrappers: immersive-translate-target-translation-theme-{name}-wrapper / -inner
- State attributes: imt-state, imt-trans-position

Inline formatting behavior
- The docs describe wrapping and separate translation elements (above) but do not claim word-level style preservation. They present translations as content inside the translation inner element (immersive-translate-target-inner), implying the translated text is rendered in that element rather than reusing original inline styling. In short: original inline markup is preserved in its wrapper; translated text is provided in dedicated translation wrappers (so word-level inline formatting is not described as preserved).
```
</details></tool-use>

 `po4a inline tags translation reordering placeholder how handled`

```text
Web search results for query: "po4a inline tags translation reordering placeholder how handled"

Links: [{"title":"Locale::Po4a::Xml (3pm) — po4a — Debian unstable — Debian Manpages","url":"https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/po4a/Locale%3A%3APo4a%3A%3AXml.3pm.en.html"},{"title":"po4a - Welcome","url":"https://www.po4a.org/index.php.en"},{"title":"XML: Placeholder for empty element · Issue #389 · mquinson/po4a","url":"https://github.com/mquinson/po4a/issues/389"}]

Done — I searched the web for "po4a inline tags translation reordering placeholder how handled" and related queries. Key findings:

- po4a’s documentation and manpages describe per-format options that let you classify XML/HTML tags as inline, break, placeholder, or untranslated. Tags listed in "inline" are treated as inline content, "break" tags affect wrapping, and "placeholder" tags are treated as placeholders. ([manpages.debian.org](https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/po4a/Locale%3A%3APo4a%3A%3AXml.3pm.en.html))

- Placeholders “do not break the sequence, but the content of placeholders is translated separately” — po4a marks the placeholder location inside the extracted block (so the translator sees a marker for the placeholder while its content may be handled separately). This is how po4a lets you keep tag structure while allowing translators to provide translated text and (if needed) reorder/position placeholders in the translated string. ([manpages.debian.org](https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/po4a/Locale%3A%3APo4a%3A%3AXml.3pm.en.html))

- There was an explicit improvement in po4a v0.69 to “greatly improve the handling of placeholders in XML” (release notes), so placeholder handling has been a deliberate focus in recent releases. ([po4a.org](https://www.po4a.org/index.php.en))

- There are related issues/discussions in the project (e.g., GitHub issue about placeholders for empty elements), which show some corner cases (empty vs non-empty elements) and ongoing bug fixes / behavior tweaks. ([github.com](https://github.com/mquinson/po4a/issues/389))

If you want, I can next:
- open the Locale::Po4a::Xml manpage and copy the exact option names and the example placeholder marker that po4a inserts into PO entries; or
- fetch concrete example PO entries produced by po4a for inline/placeholder tags so you can see how translators would reorder placeholders in practice.

Which would you like me to fetch or show?

I ran a web search on: "OmegaT tag protection tag validation missing tags translation memoQ inline tags" and found these relevant results and quick takeaways.

Top results (what they are and why they matter)
- OmegaT user manual — Validate tags / tagged files: explains Tools > Validate Tags, what the check detects, and why altered/missing tags can corrupt compiled targets. ([omegat.sourceforge.io](https://omegat.sourceforge.io/manual-latest/en/index.html))  
- OmegaT guides (cApStAn) — practical pages on tags, editing and exercises (including inserting missing tags, auto-completer shortcut). Good step-by-step help for translators using OmegaT. ([capstanlqc.github.io](https://capstanlqc.github.io/omegat-guides/en/verification/tags/))  
- OmegaT SourceForge thread / feature requests — discussion about tag validation behavior and requests for automatic validation or dismissing errors. Useful for tooling limitations and user expectations. ([sourceforge.net](https://sourceforge.net/p/omegat/feature-requests/141/))  
- memoQ documentation — authoritative pages explaining inline tags, structural vs inline tags, how to insert inline tags, and Regex Tagger to create inline tags from document parts. Also QA/Inline-tags checks configuration. ([docs.memoq.com](https://docs.memoq.com/current/en/Workspace/inline-tag.html))  
- Community/FAQ/forum posts (ProZ, help blogs) — common problems and troubleshooting (e.g., export blocked by missing tags; guidance how to restore tags or use tag insertion features). Useful practical tips from other users. ([proz.com](https://www.proz.com/forum/memoq_support/182423-unable_to_export_translated_document_due_to_missing_tags.html))

Short practical summary / recommended next steps
- If you’re in OmegaT: run Tools > Validate Tags to find segments with missing/altered tags, then edit the target segment to restore tags (OmegaT shows tag markers and has an auto-completer to insert tags). This is the standard way to detect/fix missing tags before compiling targets. ([omegat.sourceforge.io](https://omegat.sourceforge.io/manual-latest/en/index.html))  
- If you’re in memoQ: check the Inline tags docs and QA settings (Inline tags QA) — memoQ shows inline tags and lets you insert inline tags or use the Regex Tagger to convert document bits into tags; the QA will flag missing inline tags on export. ([docs.memoq.com](https://docs.memoq.com/current/en/Workspace/inline-tag.html))  
- If export fails due to missing tags: review flagged segments, copy the source tag into the target using the editor’s tag insert shortcuts (or replace with the correct inline tag type). Community threads describe this common cause and fixes. ([proz.com](https://www.proz.com/forum/memoq_support/182423-unable_to_export_translated_document_due_to_missing_tags.html))  
- Be aware of tool differences and limitations: different CAT tools represent tags differently and some users have requested improved/automatic validation behavior in OmegaT (see SourceForge discussions). Test compiled outputs if format is sensitive (XML/OOXML). ([sourceforge.net](https://sourceforge.net/p/omegat/feature-requests/141/))

A ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.09210"},{"title":"Benchmarking GPT-4 against Human Translators: A Comprehensive ...","url":"https://arxiv.org/html/2411.13775v1"},{"title":"New: XML/HTML handling v2 - DeepL Documentation","url":"https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2"},{"title":"HTML Translation API | Preserve Tags & Structure | TranslatePlus","url":"https://translateplus.io/solutions/translate-html"},{"title":"HTML tags (e.g., <a>) are stripped or lost during translation","url":"https://github.com/Azure/co-op-translator/issues/227"}]

I ran searches for papers, tools, issues and posts about LLM / GPT translation of HTML (preserving tags, dropped or reordered tags, and evaluations). Below are the most relevant findings and links you can open for details.

Top academic / research results
- “How Should Markup Tags Be Translated?” — ACL paper studying tag representation and data-augmentation methods for models to learn correct tag placement (tag placement is crucial for formatted input). ([aclanthology.org](https://aclanthology.org/2020.wmt-1.138/))  
- “Data Augmentation for Inline Tag-Aware Neural Machine Translation” — WMT/ACL-style paper proposing tag-injection/augmentation for inline tag-aware NMT and showing gains vs. detag-and-project approaches. ([aclanthology.org](https://aclanthology.org/2022.wmt-1.81/))  
- “Hybrid Synthesis Tag and Two-Stage Training for LLM-Enhanced Tag-Aware …” — recent OpenReview paper proposing an LLM-based tag-aware MT framework to balance fluency and tag-structure preservation (tag-aware training and synthetic data). ([openreview.net](https://openreview.net/pdf?id=QedcyRsKBW))  
- “Overcoming contextless web markup text translation: A parallel and …” — paper describing a translation dataset and a five-point HTML-consistency evaluation metric for markup translation. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417426021366))

Papers and evaluations of GPT / LLMs for translation (general, relevant background)
- “How Good Are GPT Models at Machine Translation?” (arXiv) — broad evaluation of GPT models for MT; useful background but not tag-focused. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.09210))  
- Several recent LLM translation evaluation studies (e.g., benchmarking GPT-4 vs humans) exist and may include discussions of robustness to document structure; see arXiv benchmarking work. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/html/2411.13775v1))

Industry/engineering resources and bug reports (practical behaviour of GPT/APIs)
- DeepL documentation for XML/HTML handling (tag_handling v2) — describes an algorithm that balances translation fluency with tag/hierarchy preservation. Good example of production tag-handling decisions. ([developers.deepl.com](https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2))  
- Blog posts and tooling guides (TranslatePlus, AIbiTranslator, Translate HTML how-tos) describing practical approaches to translate HTML while preserving tags and structure. These give engineering patterns (detag/retag, preserve-only-text-nodes, or use tag-aware APIs). ([translateplus.io](https://translateplus.io/solutions/translate-html))  
- GitHub issues and projects reporting real problems where tags are stripped, lost, or reordered when using translation tools or LLM-based pipelines (examples show tags dropped or malformed). ([github.com](https://github.com/Azure/co-op-translator/issues/227))

Practical notes & observed patterns in the results
- Research and documentation consistently treat HTML/tag preservation as a separate sub-problem: naive detagging, translating only text, or passing raw HTML to a model each have trade-offs (context loss vs. tag corruption). See both ACL/WMT papers and vendor docs. ([aclanthology.org](https://aclanthology.org/2020.wmt-1.138/))  
- Recent work looks at tag-aware training, synthetic tagged data, or LLM-guided strategies to reduce tag dropping/reordering and to evaluate structural consistency. ([aclanthology.org](https://aclanthology.org/2022.wmt-1.81/))  
- There are dedicated evaluation metrics / datasets proposed for HTML/markup consistency (e.g., HTML-consistency, five-point metrics) rather than relying only on BLEU/ROUGE. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417426021366))

=== Style String() body ===
func NewStyle(attrs ...Attr) Style {
	if len(attrs) == 0 {
		return Style{}
	}
	s := make(Style, 0, len(attrs))
	for _, a := range attrs {
		attr, ok := attrStrings[a]
		if ok {
			s = append(s, attr)
		} else {
			if a < 0 {
				a = 0
			}
			s = append(s, strconv.Itoa(a))
		}
	}
	return s
}

// String returns the ANSI SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) style sequence for
// the given style.
func (s Style) String() string {
	if len(s) == 0 {
		return ResetStyle
	}
	return "\x1b[" + strings.Join(s, ";") + "m"
}

// Styled returns a styled string with the given style applied. The style is
// applied at the beginning and reset at the end of the string.
func (s Style) Styled(str string) string {
	if len(s) == 0 {
		return str
	}
	return s.String() + str + ResetStyle
}

// Reset appends the reset style attribute to the style. This resets all
// formatting attributes to their defaults.
func (s Style) Reset() Style {
	return append(s, attrReset)
}

// Bold appends the bold or normal intensity style attribute to the style.
```

```text
// DecodeSequence decodes the first ANSI escape sequence or a printable
// grapheme from the given data. It returns the sequence slice, the number of
// bytes read, the cell width for each sequence, and the new state.
//
// The cell width will always be 0 for control and escape sequences, 1 for
// ASCII printable characters, and the number of cells other Unicode characters
// occupy. It uses the uniseg package to calculate the width of Unicode
// graphemes and characters. This means it will always do grapheme clustering
// (mode 2027).
//
// Passing a non-nil [*Parser] as the last argument will allow the decoder to
// collect sequence parameters, data, and commands. The parser cmd will have
// the packed command value that contains intermediate and prefix characters.
// In the case of a OSC sequence, the cmd will be the OSC command number. Use
// [Cmd] and [Param] types to unpack command intermediates and prefixes as well
// as parameters.
//
// Zero [Cmd] means the CSI, DCS, or ESC sequence is invalid. Moreover, checking the
// validity of other data sequences, OSC, DCS, etc, will require checking for
// the returned sequence terminator bytes such as ST (ESC \\) and BEL).
//
// We store the command byte in [Cmd] in the most significant byte, the
// prefix byte in the next byte, and the intermediate byte in the least
// significant byte. This is done to avoid using a struct to store the command
// and its intermediates and prefixes. The command byte is always the least
// significant byte i.e. [Cmd & 0xff]. Use the [Cmd] type to unpack the
// command, intermediate, and prefix bytes. Note that we only collect the last
// prefix character and intermediate byte.
//
// The [p.Params] slice will contain the parameters of the sequence. Any
// sub-parameter will have the [parser.HasMoreFlag] set. Use the [Param] type
// to unpack the parameters.
//
// Example:
//
//	var state byte // the initial state is always zero [NormalState]
//	p := NewParser(32, 1024) // create a new parser with a 32 params buffer and 1024 data buffer (optional)
//	input := []byte("\x1b[31mHello, World!\x1b[0m")
//	for len(input) > 0 {
//		seq, width, n, newState := DecodeSequence(input, state, p)
//		log.Printf("seq: %q, width: %d", seq, width)
//		state = newState
//		input = input[n:]
//	}
//
// This function treats the text as a sequence of grapheme clusters.
func DecodeSequence[T string | []byte](b T, state byte, p *Parser) (seq T, width int, n int, newState byte) {
	return decodeSequence(GraphemeWidth, b, state, p)
}
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:14:30Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm getting a clearer picture of the ansi library's capabilities. The `Strip` function removes all ANSI escape sequences, which is useful for extracting plain text in approaches A and B, while approach C doesn't need it since it preserves the original formatting. The `DecodeSequence` function is the core tool for iterating through a string and separating printable text from escape codes, making it essential for parsing styled runs and identifying the dominant styling on each line.

For approach A, rather than trying to semantically parse SGR sequences into Style objects, I can simply capture the raw escape sequence bytes that appear at the start of each line and reapply them to the translated text. However, determining what's truly "dominant" requires walking through the runs with `DecodeSequence`, tracking which SGR state is active, and counting how many runes fall under each style. I should check if there's already a utility in the TUI library or elsewhere that handles this parsing work.

Before committing to an approach, let me verify a few practical constraints: the engine only accepts a single `Request.Text`, so batching lines would mean either joining them with newlines and asking the model to preserve line count, or making multiple requests—there's no batch API. Output reassembly would happen in the CLI's print logic. And since `lipgloss` is already a dependency, approach C's uniform dim styling is readily available. I need to quickly scan the TUI package and the engine wrapper to see what ANSI handling already exists.
</details></think>

```text
=== smartauto.go ===
package engine

import (
	"context"
	"strings"
	"unicode"

	"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/debug"
	"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/lang"
)

// SmartAutoEngine is the "smart" default: it treats a single word/term as a
// dictionary lookup (delegating to the smart-dict engine, which itself falls back
// to the LLM on a miss) and a phrase or sentence as an LLM translation. It is
// bidirectional-friendly — the caller sets Pair/PairHome/PairAway and the routed
// Target, and both branches honor them.
//
// Because dictionary word lookups are direction-agnostic (script routes them to
// CC-CEDICT vs ECDICT) and phrase translation uses the pair-aware LLM prompt, a
// single word like "test" is answered from the dictionary instead of risking an
// LLM echo, and a full sentence is translated into the other pair language.
type SmartAutoEngine struct {
	smart Engine // dictionary + LLM fallback (serves ModeDict)
	llm   Engine // LLM translate, possibly a fallback chain (serves ModeTranslate)
}

// NewSmartAuto builds a smart-auto engine over a smart-dict engine (word lookups)
// and an LLM/translate engine (phrases).
func NewSmartAuto(smart, llm Engine) *SmartAutoEngine {
	return &SmartAutoEngine{smart: smart, llm: llm}
}

// Name returns "smart-auto".
func (e *SmartAutoEngine) Name() string { return "smart-auto" }

// Supports translate mode (the main-path request mode); it dispatches to a
// dictionary lookup internally when the input looks like a single word.
func (e *SmartAutoEngine) Supports(m Mode) bool { return m == ModeTranslate }

// Detect is not meaningful here (the LLM/dict sub-engines own detection).
func (e *SmartAutoEngine) Detect(ctx context.Context, text string) (string, error) {
	return "", nil
}

// Available reports true when either branch can serve.
func (e *SmartAutoEngine) Available(ctx context.Context) bool {
	return e.smart.Available(ctx) || e.llm.Available(ctx)
}

// Translate routes a single word/term to the dictionary (smart-dict) and a phrase
// to the LLM. Streaming is preserved by returning the chosen sub-engine's channel.
func (e *SmartAutoEngine) Translate(ctx context.Context, req Request) (<-chan Chunk, error) {
	if isLookup(req.Text) {
		debug.Logf("smart-auto: %q → dictionary lookup (smart-dict), target=%s", truncateText(req.Text), req.Target)
		r := req
		r.Mode = ModeDict
		return e.smart.Translate(ctx, r)
	}
	debug.Logf("smart-auto: %q → LLM translate, target=%s pair=%v", truncateText(req.Text), req.Target, req.Pair)
	r := req
	r.Mode = ModeTranslate
	return e.llm.Translate(ctx, r)
}

// isLookup reports whether text should be treated as a dictionary lookup (a
// single word or short term) rather than a phrase to translate. Latin/other: one
// token of letters (plus '-' / apostrophe), up to 32 runes. CJK: a short run of
// Han without spaces (a word or idiom, up to 4 characters). Anything with
// internal whitespace is a phrase.
func isLookup(text string) bool {
	t := strings.TrimSpace(text)
	if t == "" {
		return false
	}
	if strings.ContainsAny(t, " \t\n\r") {
		return false // multiple tokens → phrase
	}
	if lang.IsChinese(t) {
		return len([]rune(t)) <= 4
	}
	for _, r := range t {
		if !unicode.IsLetter(r) && r != '-' && r != '\'' {
			return false // digits/punctuation → not a plain word
		}
	}
	return len([]rune(t)) <= 32
}

// truncateText shortens a string for a single-line debug label.
func truncateText(s string) string {
	s = strings.TrimSpace(s)
	r := []rune(s)
	if len(r) > 40 {
		return string(r[:40]) + "…"
	}
	return s
}

Read Style type core methods and SGR body

`D=/Users/david/.local/share/go/pkg/mod/github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi@v0.11.7; sed -n '17,66p' "$D/style.go"; echo "=== sgr.go body ==="; sed -n '10,40p' "$D/sgr.go"`

```text
// Style represents an ANSI SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) style.
type Style []string

// NewStyle returns a new style with the given attributes. Attributes are SGR
// (Select Graphic Rendition) codes that control text formatting like bold,
// italic, colors, etc.
func NewStyle(attrs ...Attr) Style {
	if len(attrs) == 0 {
		return Style{}
	}
	s := make(Style, 0, len(attrs))
	for _, a := range attrs {
		attr, ok := attrStrings[a]
		if ok {
			s = append(s, attr)
		} else {
			if a < 0 {
				a = 0
			}
			s = append(s, strconv.Itoa(a))
		}
	}
	return s
}

// String returns the ANSI SGR (Select Graphic Rendition) style sequence for
// the given style.
func (s Style) String() string {
	if len(s) == 0 {
		return ResetStyle
	}
	return "\x1b[" + strings.Join(s, ";") + "m"
}

// Styled returns a styled string with the given style applied. The style is
// applied at the beginning and reset at the end of the string.
func (s Style) Styled(str string) string {
	if len(s) == 0 {
		return str
	}
	return s.String() + str + ResetStyle
}

// Reset appends the reset style attribute to the style. This resets all
// formatting attributes to their defaults.
func (s Style) Reset() Style {
	return append(s, attrReset)
}

// Bold appends the bold or normal intensity style attribute to the style.
=== sgr.go body ===
func SelectGraphicRendition(ps ...Attr) string {
	if len(ps) == 0 {
		return ResetStyle
	}

	return NewStyle(ps...).String()
}

// SGR is an alias for [SelectGraphicRendition].
func SGR(ps ...Attr) string {
	return SelectGraphicRendition(ps...)
}

var attrStrings = map[int]string{
	AttrReset:                        attrReset,
	AttrBold:                         attrBold,

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:15:07Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2`

```text
Summary — how tag_handling = v2 treats inline XML/HTML tags and controls

How v2 operates
- The API extracts text from the XML/HTML, translates it, and reinserts translations into the structure (use tag_handling=xml and tag_handling_version=v2).
- Tags that wrap words stay attached to those words in the translation; placeholder tags are placed next to the translation of the words that precede or follow them. Example behaviors shown:
  - Inline markup: tags remain attached to wrapped words after translation.
  - Placeholder tags: placed next to the translation of nearby words.
  - Nested tags: nesting is preserved and reinserted.

Placement when word order changes
- If translated word order differs, v2 can duplicate or move tags so the translation is grammatically correct; duplicated tags are an expected outcome when tag positions must shift to match the target-language word order (see the non_splitting_tags example where tags are duplicated).

Failure modes and limitations (quoted specifics)
- Strict parsing error on malformed XML: "Tag handling parsing failed, please check input."
- Expected problematic outcomes illustrated in the docs:
  - Tags can be duplicated when their wrapped text moves in the translation (the docs show duplicated tags as expected).
  - If sentence splitting happens incorrectly (e.g., newlines splitting a sentence), translations can be wrong and tags/text can be separated across translations (example shows an English line break producing two translated parts).
  - Heavy/complex inline tagging and incorrect automatic splitting can yield misplaced or fragmented translations; the docs advise testing representative content.

Parameters that control behavior
- tag_handling=xml and tag_handling_version=v2 — enable v2 algorithm.
- ignore_tags — list tags whose content should not be translated (keeps inner text verbatim).
- split_sentences (e.g., nonewlines) — controls whether newlines cause sentence splits; use nonewlines to avoid unwanted splits inside XML files.
- non_splitting_tags — list tags that should be treated so text across those tags is translated as a single sentence (prevents splitting when a sentence spans multiple tags).
- splitting_tags — explicit list of structure tags to break on when outline_detection is disabled.
- outline_detection=0 — turn off automatic XML outline detection so you can supply splitting_tags and fully control sentence boundaries.

Practical guidance / warnings
- Ensure input is well-formed XML/HTML; v2 is strict and will return the parsing error quoted above for invalid markup.
- Test representative files before switching to v2 in production: results differ between v1 and v2 and tag placement can change when word order changes.
- For whole XML documents, use split_sentences=nonewlines so line breaks don't split sentences.
- If automatic behavior fails for complex files, disable outline detection and set splitting_tags/non_splitting_tags to force desired boundaries.

Selected quotes (<=125 chars each)
- "Tag handling parsing failed, please check input."
- "tags stay attached to the words they wrap, and placeholder tags are placed next to the translation of the words"
```
</details></tool-use>
