Done — I searched the web for: "Google Cloud Translation API HTML format tags reordered notranslate translate=\"no\" preserve" and related queries. Below are the most relevant results and a short summary of what they say.

Top results (click titles in your UI to open):

- Translation API Markup (Google Cloud docs) — guidance on translating HTML/markup and using lang attributes around translated text. ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/markup))  
- Translate text with Cloud Translation (Google Cloud docs) — says when format=HTML the API does not translate tags, only text between them, and returns tags around translated text "to the extent possible". ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/docs/translate-text))  
- Troubleshooting (Cloud Translation) — notes the API may add style attributes (text-align/direction) when changing text direction, i.e., the HTML output may be adjusted. ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/troubleshooting))  
- Stack Overflow: "span class='notranslate' tags disappear (Google Cloud Translation API)" — user reports notranslate spans disappearing from translation output and discussion of workarounds (e.g., using <pre class='notranslate'>). ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54466495/span-class-notranslate-tags-disappear-google-cloud-translation-api))  
- Stack Overflow: "How can I tell Google Translate to not translate a section…" — long-running thread showing translate=\"no\" sometimes has no effect for Google Translate tools; many recommend using class="notranslate". ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/9628507/how-can-i-tell-google-translate-to-not-translate-a-section-of-a-website))  
- MDN / W3C on the translate global attribute — describes translate="no" (HTML global attribute) and intended semantics. ([developer.mozilla.org](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Global_attributes/translate))

Short summary / findings
- Official docs: Cloud Translation supports format=HTML and generally preserves HTML tags rather than translating them; however the output can be adjusted (e.g., extra style attributes) to accommodate language-direction differences. ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/docs/translate-text))  
- Practical reports: users have observed HTML tag reordering or removal of certain wrapper tags/attributes (notably class="notranslate" spans) in translation results; there are multiple StackOverflow threads describing this behavior and suggesting workarounds. ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54466495/span-class-notranslate-tags-disappear-google-cloud-translation-api))  
- translate="no" vs class="notranslate": translate="no" is a standard HTML attribute (MDN/W3C) but many practical guides and threads recommend class="notranslate" (Google-specific) for better coverage with Google translation tools; behavior can differ between browser Google Translate, the Cloud Translation API, and other services. ([developer.mozilla.org](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTML/Reference/Global_attributes/translate))  
- Other services: some providers (e.g., AWS Translate, Azure Translator) offer explicit do-not-translate tags/features for HTML; if you need strict preservation you may compare alternatives. ([docs.aws.amazon.com](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/translate/latest/dg/customizing-translations-tags.html))

Quick mitigations people use
- Wrap non-translatable segments in <pre class="notranslate"> or other block tags (some users found that helped). ([stackoverflow.com](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54466495/span-class-notranslate-tags-disappear-google-cloud-translation-api))  
- Preprocess: replace protected segments with placeholders before calling the API, translate, then re-insert the original HTML segments. (This is a common engineering pattern; see general HTML-translation guides.) ([aibitranslator.com](https://www.aibitranslator.com/blogs/how-to-translate-html-content-api))  
- Use lang="" attributes as suggested in the Cloud docs to mark translated sections or to contain the effect of the API’s language tagging. ([docs.cloud.google.com](https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/markup))  
- If you need built-in “do-not-translate” tag support, evaluate providers that explicitly support it (AWS, Azure) or check whether the Cloud Translation Advanced features/glossary can help for your use case. ([docs.aws.amazon.com](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/translate/latest/dg/customizing-translations-tags.html))

If you want, I can:
- Open and extract the most relevant passages from any of the results above (e.g., the StackOverflow thread or the Cloud docs).  
- Run a narrower search (e.g., “span class='notranslate' disappears Google Cloud Translation API 2019 2024”) to find more user reports or bug trackers.  
- Suggest and show example code demonstrating a placeholder/preprocess workflow for preserving HTML and attributes.

Which follow-up would you like?

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:15:15Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/2.3-content-scripts`

```text
How inline vs. block is chosen
- The content script classifies the element by type/selectors: block elements get a block handler, inline elements get an inline handler. Quote: "Holds translated text for block elements (p, div, h1-h6)". Quote: "Holds translated text for inline elements (span, a, em, strong)".

What happens for headings and list items
- Headings (h1–h6) and list items (li) are treated as block-level content, so the block-level translation wrapper is used. Because the original content is wrapped (the script "Creates wrapper elements" and "Wraps original content" in order to toggle visibility), the translated text is placed inside a block translation container associated with that element so styling like font-size or list markers remains governed by the element or its wrapper.

Exact DOM classes/structure produced (canonical pieces)
- immersive-translate-target-wrapper — original text container (wraps original content)
- immersive-translate-target-translation-block-wrapper — block translation container (for p, div, h1-h6, li)
- immersive-translate-target-translation-inline-wrapper — inline translation container (for span, a, em, strong)
- immersive-translate-target-inner — innermost element that holds the translated text
- immersive-translate-target-translation-theme-{name}-wrapper / -inner — theme-specific wrapper/inner

State & position attributes that control visibility/placement
- imt-state = "dual" | "translation" | "source"
- imt-trans-position = "before" | "after"

Representative (schematic) DOM arrangement for a heading or list item
- <original-element imt-state="dual" imt-trans-position="after">
    <div class="immersive-translate-target-wrapper">...original text...</div>
    <div class="immersive-translate-target-translation-block-wrapper">
      <div class="immersive-translate-target-inner">...translated text...</div>
    </div>
  </original-element>

Representative (schematic) DOM arrangement for inline text
- <span imt-state="dual" imt-trans-position="after">
    <span class="immersive-translate-target-wrapper">...original...</span>
    <span class="immersive-translate-target-translation-inline-wrapper">
      <span class="immersive-translate-target-inner">...translated...</span>
    </span>
  </span>

Sources (from the provided content)
- "Wraps original content that can be hidden in translation-only mode"
- "Holds translated text for block elements (p, div, h1-h6)"
- "Holds translated text for inline elements (span, a, em, strong)"
- Class names listed under "CSS Class Hierarchy and Purpose"
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:15:04Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Run a probe program exercising DecodeSequence, Strip, width, Truncate, Style

```bash
cat > /tmp/ansi_probe_test_dir_check.go <<'EOF'
package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi"
)

func main() {
	input := "\x1b[1;31mHello\x1b[0m \x1b[32m世界\x1b[0m!"
	var state byte
	p := ansi.NewParser()
	var curStyle string // captured raw SGR open sequence
	fmt.Printf("Strip:        %q\n", ansi.Strip(input))
	fmt.Printf("StringWidth:  %d\n", ansi.StringWidth(input))
	fmt.Println("--- runs ---")
	rem := input
	for len(rem) > 0 {
		seq, width, n, newState := ansi.DecodeSequenceInString(rem, state, p)
		if width > 0 {
			// visible text grapheme(s)
			fmt.Printf("TEXT  %q width=%d  under-style=%q\n", seq, width, curStyle)
		} else {
			// control/escape; check if it's an SGR (CSI ... 'm')
			cmd := ansi.Cmd(p.Command())
			if len(seq) > 1 && seq[0] == 0x1b && cmd.Final() == 'm' {
				curStyle = seq // capture raw SGR sequence
				fmt.Printf("SGR   %q  finalByte=%q params=%v\n", seq, rune(cmd.Final()), p.Params())
			} else {
				fmt.Printf("CTRL  %q\n", seq)
			}
		}
		state = newState
		rem = rem[n:]
	}
	// Re-emit a text run wrapped in a captured style:
	captured := ansi.NewStyle(ansi.AttrBold, ansi.AttrRedForegroundColor)
	fmt.Printf("Re-emit Styled: %q\n", captured.Styled("re-styled"))
	fmt.Printf("Truncate:       %q\n", ansi.Truncate(input, 6, "…"))
}
EOF
cp /tmp/ansi_probe_test_dir_check.go ./zz_ansi_probe_main.go && go run ./zz_ansi_probe_main.go; rm -f ./zz_ansi_probe_main.go
```

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:15:27Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Now I'm pulling together the full picture across the three approaches. I've traced the pipe path through `cmd/root.go` where input gets read, trimmed, and passed as a single request to the engine, then the result gets printed. The `oneShot` function handles building that request and is shared between both the args and pipe paths. Since `Engine.Request` only accepts a single text field and returns one translation per call, handling multiple lines means either making sequential API calls or joining them into one text and relying on the model to preserve line boundaries. The resolved engine defaults to smart-auto routing, which could send a single-word colored line to the dictionary instead of the LLM, so for multi-line structure preservation I'd want to force the LLM engine using the existing `learnEngineFromConfig` pattern. I also have `ansi.Strip` available to cleanly extract source text without escape sequences, and lower-level utilities like `ansi.DecodeSequence` for walking through printable runs versus escape codes.

For styling, `ansi.Style` can build SGR sequences and there are ANSI-aware utilities like `Truncate` and `Wrap`, but there's no direct parser from sequence bytes to a Style struct—so for approach A, capturing and reapplying the raw leading SGR escape string verbatim is simpler than trying to reconstruct the style semantically. lipgloss v2 is already a dependency, so approach C's uniform dimming via `lg.NewStyle().Faint(true)` is straightforward. Since piped output doesn't stream (the condition `stream = streamPref && stdoutTTY` evaluates to false when piped), all three approaches work on fully-drained results with no streaming complications to worry about.

Now I'm estimating the effort for each approach, starting with approach C which looks simplest. I'm weighing the trade-offs: per-line sequential calls reuse existing logic but hit latency hard, a single whole-blob translation is fastest but loses line-by-line pairing, and a batched call with line alignment needs prompt changes. I'll go with the whole-blob approach to keep it simple—just one LLM call, one dim style in lipgloss, a new `--color` flag in cmd/root.go, and handle edge cases like blank lines passing through unchanged. Detecting dominant SGR sequences by walking each line and tallying display width per active style, then reapplying that style around translated text. For batching, I'd join stripped lines with a separator for a single LLM call, then split the response back—though this requires careful prompt engineering to guarantee N-in/N-out alignment. Handling edge cases like blank lines and pure box-drawing characters that shouldn't be translated. Building the span-level infrastructure with a new `ansimark` package to parse ANSI sequences into styled runs and replace them with placeholder tokens, then reconstruct after translation. The tricky part is getting the LLM to reliably preserve these markers when word order shifts between languages—models often drop or mangle them, so I'll need validation and fallback logic when the markers don't round-trip correctly.

For the A/B/C-line routing issue, I need to force all three variants to use the LLM engine directly rather than letting smart-auto route single-word lines to the dictionary, which would produce the wrong output shape.

Looking at the implementation costs: Approach C is cleanest at 50-100 lines of code with just one LLM call for the whole blob, keeping latency and token usage roughly the same as today. Approach A spreads across multiple files with 200-350 lines total, and depending on whether I batch the LLM calls or process per-line, I'm either dealing with fragile batching or multiplying both latency and token costs by the number of lines.

Approach B needs 300-450 lines across multiple files plus validation and fallback logic, which inflates token usage by 10-30% with markers and introduces higher reliability risk. Now I need to check if there's already a `learnEngineFromConfig` helper and where `buildEngine` is located so I can identify the exact reuse points.
</details></think>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:15:33Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I've gathered enough information to provide a solid answer. The key insight is that block-level elements preserve their styling through a wrapper approach—the translated text gets inserted alongside the original content within the same DOM element, so it inherits the parent's CSS properties like font sizes for headings and list markers for list items.

However, character-level styling like bold or color on individual words doesn't carry over. The entire translated segment receives a single uniform theme style (underline, highlight, marker, mask, blockquote, or dimmed text) rather than per-word formatting. The bilingual layout is controlled by state variables that determine whether to show both versions or just one, with the translation typically appearing after the original text by default. The GitHub issue #2711 confirms these class names are actually being injected in real translations, which validates the reverse-engineered documentation I'm working from.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I've confirmed the implementation is working correctly. The `ansi.Strip` function removes all escape sequences to get plain text like "Hello 世界!", while `ansi.DecodeSequence` walks through the string character by character, tracking visible graphemes with their cell widths (accounting for CJK characters like 世 which take up 2 cells) and distinguishing them from control or escape sequences by checking if the command ends with 'm'.

For re-emitting styled text, I capture the raw SGR sequence from the decoder and wrap the text with it plus a reset code using `Style.Styled(str)`, which produces output like `"\x1b[1;31mre-styled\x1b[m"`. The key insight is that the decoder gives raw SGR sequences rather than a merged cumulative style, so when multiple SGR codes are applied without a reset (like `\x1b[1m` followed by `\x1b[31m`), I need to accumulate them to track what the active style actually is at any point in the string. I'm verifying the width calculation is correct—5 for "Hello", 1 for the space, 4 for the two CJK characters at 2 width each, and 1 for the exclamation mark totals 11. I'm also confirming that helper functions like TruncateLeft and Cut are available in the library, and checking whether there's a cumulative style state mechanism or if I need to build it myself by tracking raw SGR sequences. Now I have what I need to structure the output and clarify what exists versus what needs to be built.

The existing utilities include the tokenizer that yields sequences with width information, the parser for command and parameter extraction, the Style type with methods for applying formatting attributes, color decoding functions for extended colors, and various width and truncation functions for handling both ASCII and wide characters.

Now I need to build the core piece myself: a loop that tracks the accumulated active style as it processes each SGR sequence, maintaining a buffer of the current formatting state. When a reset sequence appears, I clear the buffer; otherwise I append new style parameters. Then I re-emit each text span wrapped with the accumulated opening codes and a reset at the end. There's no pre-built function for this—it's about fifteen lines of logic that ties everything together.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm organizing the concrete details across the three approaches—tracking file locations, function signatures, line counts, function calls, configuration flags, edge cases, and complexity metrics for each one. Now I'm structuring this into a comprehensive output that captures the pipe path flow through cmd/root.go where a single engine.Request gets built and passed to oneShot, which handles the translation and prints the result.

The engine itself only supports single-text requests with no batch API, returning a channel with one ChunkDone event, so translating multiple lines requires either N separate calls or joining them with a prompt contract. The pipe uses the resolved primary engine (smart-auto by default) which routes short lines to the dictionary, but per-line approaches need to bypass that and force a bare LLM using the existing helper functions. For ANSI handling, the ansi library provides stripping and low-level sequence parsing, but there's no high-level parser that converts sequences to Style structs, so approach A should capture and reapply raw SGR bytes verbatim rather than reconstructing them. Lipgloss v2 is available as a direct dependency for styling.

Now I'm weighing the three approaches: approach C is the smallest at 40-90 lines of code with one flag and one LLM call; approach A needs 200-350 lines across a new internal package with either fragile batching or N per-line calls; approach B requires 300-450 lines for marker handling and request plumbing. In terms of latency and tokens, today's single call stays the same for C's whole-blob approach, while A's batched version adds format overhead and per-line variants repeat the system prompt across N calls.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 04:17:34Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Content_translation/Developers/Markup`

```text
Failure modes (concrete, enumerated), each followed by a short quoted excerpt from the page and a brief paraphrase:

1) Corrupt markup  
   - Quote: "Corrupt markup"  
   - Paraphrase: MT can move or break tags so the output HTML is syntactically invalid.

2) Wrongly placed annotations (tags attached to wrong text)  
   - Quote: "Wrongly placed annotations"  
   - Paraphrase: Tags/links can end up around the wrong words after reorderings (e.g., link targets swapped).

3) Missing annotations (tags dropped)  
   - Quote: "Missing annotations"  
   - Paraphrase: The MT engine may remove some tags entirely, so annotations disappear.

4) Split annotations (single annotation must cover multiple target words)  
   - Quote: "Split annotations"  
   - Paraphrase: A source token with a tag may translate into multiple words — unclear whether the tag should wrap all pieces or each piece.

5) Markup treated as whitespace → semantic/syntactic errors  
   - Quote: "Markup such as HTML tags is treated as a form of blank space."  
   - Paraphrase: Some engines ignore tags, causing word reordering or missing structure (examples shown for Apertium).

6) Tag corruption from naive inline translation (examples)  
   - Quote: "Soy</b> David"  
   - Paraphrase: An example where tag boundaries are broken by MT, producing mismatched closing tags in output.

7) Annotation matching failures due to inflection, case, reorder, or word-splitting  
   - Quote: "This shows that we will require language specific tailoring"  
   - Paraphrase: Inflectional endings, capitalization, word-order changes, and expansions/splits make exact reattachment of annotations unreliable.

8) False matches from edit-distance alone (wrong target matched)  
   - Quote: "With an edit distance approach, `eat` will match more with `meat` than `eating`."  
   - Paraphrase: Pure edit-distance can pick the wrong neighbor (matches similar substrings rather than correct translation).

9) Complex reordering that breaks simple substring matching (e.g., cannot → puede no)  
   - Quote: "Here we need to match `Puede no` and `no pueden` which of course wont match"  
   - Paraphrase: Word-order inversion can make subsequence correspondences non-obvious to simple searches.

Mitigation techniques and algorithms described (quoted snippets plus paraphrase):

A) Linearize markup into a chunked representation  
   - Quote: "input HTML content is translated into a LinearDoc"  
   - Paraphrase: Convert HTML to a linear token/chunk model that stores inline markup as attributes on text chunks to simplify manipulation.

B) Send plain text to MT; reapply markup after using alignments  
   - Quote: "Plain text sentences ... are sent to the MT engine for translation."  
   - Paraphrase: Strip inline tags before MT, get back plain translations and alignment info, then restore annotations.

C) Use subsentence alignment / n-gram fuzzy matching to locate translated annotations  
   - Quote: "The MT engine returns a plain text translation, together with subsentence alignment information"  
   - Paraphrase: Use returned alignments and approximate search to find where subsequences map in the target.

D) Approximate matching per-word (n-gram) rather than whole-string Levenshtein  
   - Quote: "we do not consider sub sequence as a string, but an n-gram"  
   - Paraphrase: Match each word in the subsequence to words in the translated text left-to-right to tolerate reorder and inflection.

E) Language-specific heuristics and thresholds (start-letter checks, configurable edit distance)  
   - Quote: "That must be configurable per language modules."  
   - Paraphrase: Tune edit-distance thresholds and add rules (e.g., same initial letter) per language to avoid bad matches.

F) Nested-subsequence strategy (apply outer annotation first, then inner)  
   - Quote: "Rojo grande need to be first located and bold tag should be applied. Then search for `Rojo`"  
   - Paraphrase: For nested formatting, locate and apply larger spans before inner ones to preserve nesting.

G) Previous uppercase-differencing approach noted as limited  
   - Quote: "initially we tried an algorithm based on case change observation."  
   - Paraphrase: Earlier method uppercased a source word to spot its translation location; found insufficient versus the n-gram fuzzy approach.

Summary (one-line): The page lists broken/corrupt tags, misplaced or missing annotations, and split annotations as the main failure modes, and describes restoring markup by linearizing content, sending plain text to MT, using subsentence alignment plus n-gram/fuzzy matching, and language-specific heuristics to reapply annotations.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
package engine

import (
	"fmt"
	"strings"

	"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/lang"
)

// Preset selects the LLM translate prompt style. Only ModeTranslate LLM engines
// honor it; google/dict ignore it.
const (
	PresetConcise    = "concise"    // terse direct translation (default)
	PresetContextual = "contextual" // short list of common-sense translations
	PresetDictionary = "dictionary" // translation + 1-2 example sentences
)

// translateSystemPromptConcise drives the primary (hot-path) translation. It asks
// for ONLY the translated text so the reply streams token-by-token identically
// across every backend and is latency-optimal.
const translateSystemPromptConcise = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
Translate the user's text into the target language faithfully and idiomatically.

Rules:
- Preserve the meaning, tone, and register (formal/informal, technical/casual) of the source.
- The user's text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical phrasing. Interpret their
  INTENDED meaning and translate that; do not translate a typo literally, and never refuse.
- Output ONLY the translated text. No quotes, no explanations, no language labels,
  no commentary before or after.
- ALWAYS translate into the target language. A single word, name, technical term, or
  loanword written in a DIFFERENT language must still be translated — never echo it back.
  Only when the text is ALREADY in the target language should you return it (lightly
  corrected if it was misspelled).`

// translateSystemPromptContextual lists translations across common senses.
const translateSystemPromptContextual = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
Translate the user's text into the target language, showing how the translation changes
across the most common senses or contexts of the source.

Rules:
- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. The text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical
  phrasing — interpret the INTENDED meaning; never refuse and never translate a typo literally.
- Output a SHORT list of 2-4 lines, one line per distinct common sense/context.
- Format each line EXACTLY as: "N. <translation> — <short context label in the TARGET language>".
- Order lines from the most likely/common sense to the least.
- If the text has only one natural sense, output a single line — do not invent senses.
- Output ONLY the list. No preamble, no headings, no commentary before or after.`

// translateSystemPromptDictionary gives a translation plus example sentences.
const translateSystemPromptDictionary = `You are a precise, professional translation engine
that also gives brief usage examples.
Translate the user's text into the target language, then illustrate it with example sentences.

Rules:
- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. Interpret the INTENDED meaning of typos/slang; never refuse.
- The FIRST line is ONLY the translation of the user's text — no label, no quotes.
- Then a blank line, then 1-2 example sentences that use the translation naturally.
- Format each example on two lines:
    line 1: the example sentence in the TARGET language,
    line 2: its translation in the source language (the language of the user's input),

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The streaming architecture rules out any placeholder-token approach since that would require buffering the complete output and reinserting translations afterward, which breaks the token-by-token streaming model and reintroduces all the known failure modes. I'm ready to synthesize the findings from the OASIS XLIFF specification and other key sources into a structured analysis. I'm clarifying the XLIFF element types and their constraints: `<x/>` is a standalone placeholder with inherent ordering ambiguity, while `<bx/>`/`<ex/>` pairs can be split or reordered by translators, creating invalid pairings. You are a skeptical senior engineer reviewing a proposed recommendation for the 'translate' CLI.
PROPOSED RECOMMENDATION: For piped colored input, DO NOT chase per-word ANSI color (approach B). Instead ship a bilingual "immersive" mode that keeps the original colored line intact and prints the translation beneath it (uniform dim style, or carrying the line's dominant color) — approaches C/A. Treat full per-span color preservation as over-engineering.

Here is what investigation found:
[Finding 1] github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi@v0.11.7 (in cache at /Users/david/.local/share/go/pkg/mod/github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi@v0.11.7) covers all three needs with real exported symbols, verified by running a probe program. (a) ansi.Strip strips all SGR/escapes to plain text. (b) ansi.DecodeSequence + ansi.NewParser + ansi.Cmd give a real tokenizer: loop yields (seq, width, n, newState); width>0 means a visible grapheme, width==0 means a control/escape, and ansi.Cmd(p.Command()).Final()=='m' identifies SGR runs. You capture the raw SGR seq string as the "active style" and attach it to following text graphemes. (c) ansi.StringWidth/StringWidthWc are wcwidth/CJK-aware; ansi.Truncate/TruncateWc/TruncateLeft/Cut truncate while preserving ANSI styles. Re-emit is trivial: ansi.Style.Styled(text) or captured-open-seq + text + ansi.ResetStyle. The one thing you must BUILD: accumulating individual SGR sequences into a cumulative active style — the decoder emits each raw SGR sequence but does not maintain running merged style state, and there is no single string→[]StyledRun function.
Facts: Package location: /Users/david/.local/share/go/pkg/mod/github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi@v0.11.7 (go.mod pins v0.11.7 as indirect; had to `go mod download` it first). GOMODCACHE = /Users/david/.local/share/go/pkg/mod. | (a) STRIP EXISTS: `func ansi.Strip(s string) string` in width.go:10. Verified: Strip("\x1b[1;31mHello\x1b[0m 世界!") -> "Hello 世界!". | (b) TOKENIZER EXISTS: `func ansi.DecodeSequence[T string | []byte](b T, state byte, p *Parser) (seq T, width int, n int, newState byte)` (parser_decode.go:70); byte-slice/string method variants `Method.DecodeSequence` and `Method.DecodeSequenceInString` (method.go:146/194); wide-char variant `DecodeSequenceWc` (parser_decode.go:120). width is per-decoded-token cell width: 0=control/escape, 1=ASCII, N=Unicode cells. | Parser API (parser.go): `func ansi.NewParser() *Parser`; `(*Parser).Command() int`; `(*Parser).Params() Params`; `(*Parser).Param(i, def int) (int, bool)`. NOTE: NewParser() takes NO args in v0.11.7 (the doc-comment example showing NewParser(32,1024) is stale; use SetParamsSize/SetDataSize instead). | SGR detection: unpack p.Command() via `type Cmd int` (parser_decode.go:456) with `(Cmd).Final() byte`, `.Prefix() byte`, `.Intermediate() byte`. An SGR run is a CSI whose Final()=='m'. Params via `type Param int` with `(Param).Param(def int) int` and `.HasMore() bool` (sub-params). Verified: "\x1b[1;31m" decodes to Final='m', Params=[1 31]. | Style re-emit EXISTS: `type Style []string`; `ansi.NewStyle(attrs ...Attr) Style`; `(Style).String() string` -> "\x1b[..m"; `(Style).Styled(str string) string` = prepend style + append reset; const `ansi.ResetStyle = "\x1b[m"` (style.go:12). Builders: `ansi.SGR(...)`/`ansi.SelectGraphicRendition(ps ...Attr)`. Attr constants like ansi.AttrBold, ansi.AttrRedForegroundColor exist (style.go:320+). Verified: NewStyle(AttrBold,AttrRedForegroundColor).Styled("x") -> "\x1b[1;31mx\x1b[m". | Color param decoding EXISTS: `func ansi.ReadStyleColor(params Params, co *color.Color) int` (style.go:675) decodes 16/256/truecolor (38;2;r;g;b / 38;5;n, colon- or semicolon-separated) into a color.Color; returns params consumed. Structured setters: (Style).ForegroundColor(c Color)/BackgroundColor/UnderlineColor/Bold()/Italic(bool)/Underline(bool)/etc. | (c) WIDTH EXISTS: `ansi.StringWidth(s string) int` (width.go:65) and `ansi.StringWidthWc` (width.go:74); CJK/emoji + ANSI aware (uses clipperhouse/displaywidth + mattn/go-runewidth + uax29 grapheme clustering). Verified: StringWidth("Hello 世界!")=11 (世/界 count 2 each). | (c) TRUNCATE EXISTS (all ANSI- & wide-char-aware, preserve styles): `ansi.Truncate(s, length int, tail string)`, `TruncateWc`, `TruncateLeft(s, n int, prefix string)`, `TruncateLeftWc`, `Cut(s string, left, right int)`, `CutWc`, plus helper `ByteToGraphemeRange(str, byteStart, byteStop) (charStart, charStop int)` (truncate.go). Verified: Truncate(colored,6,"…") keeps the \x1b[1;31m...\x1b[0m codes and re-appends trailing style state. | Bonus wrapping EXISTS (wrap.go / method.go): `Wrap`, `Wordwrap`, `Hardwrap` (and Method variants), all ANSI/width aware. | `Method` type (method.go): GraphemeWidth (default, grapheme-cluster mode 2027) vs WcWidth (rune/wcwidth). Top-level funcs use GraphemeWidth; the *Wc variants use WcWidth.
Risks: The decoder does NOT maintain cumulative/merged style state. It emits each raw SGR sequence independently. If input does \x1b[1m ... \x1b[31m without a reset between, the active style over later text is BOTH — you must accumulate yourself (concatenate raw open sequences since the last reset, or parse params into a running ansi.Style). Naively storing only the last-seen SGR seq loses earlier attributes. My probe's `curStyle = seq` was the simplistic version and would drop the bold if bold and color came as separate sequences. | There is NO single string -> []StyledRun function. You build the run-splitting loop. Don't hunt for a higher-level API; it isn't there. | Reset semantics: an SGR with param 0 (or empty \x1b[m) clears style; partial resets (e.g. 22 no-bold, 39 default-fg, 24 no-underline) require handling if you accumulate structured attrs rather than just replaying raw bytes. Replaying raw bytes + a single ResetStyle at run end is the safe, low-effort path. | NewParser() takes no args in v0.11.7 despite the stale doc-comment example showing NewParser(32,1024); use SetParamsSize()/SetDataSize() if you need non-default buffers. Passing args will not compile. | Grapheme vs Wc width mismatch: pick one Method consistently across Strip-adjacent measuring, truncation, and your run loop, or column math will drift on emoji/ZWJ sequences. DecodeSequence (grapheme) pairs with StringWidth/Truncate; DecodeSequenceWc pairs with StringWidthWc/TruncateWc. | Over-engineering trap: for pure width/truncate/strip you do NOT need the Parser at all — only reach for DecodeSequence+Parser when you specifically need per-span style attribution. Don't build a full styled-cell model if you only need to truncate a colored line. | Non-SGR escapes (OSC hyperlinks \x1b]8, cursor moves, etc.) also decode with width==0; your loop must pass them through / ignore them rather than treat them as text or style, or you'll corrupt output.

[Finding 2] Immersive Translate's philosophy is block-level style preservation + bilingual layout, NOT per-word inline style reproduction. When translating a page it walks the DOM (MutationObserver + TreeWalker), and for each translatable block element (p, div, h1-h6, li) it inserts a translation container INSIDE/next to that same original element. Because the translation lives within the original block, it inherits that element's CSS cascade — a translated heading stays heading-sized, a translated list item keeps its list marker. Inline elements (span, a, em, strong) get an inline wrapper instead so flow isn't broken. The translated text itself is dropped into a single innermost node (immersive-translate-target-inner) and rendered in ONE uniform theme (underline / dashed / grey-dimmed / highlight / marker / mask-blur / blockquote). There is no documented mechanism that re-bolds or re-colors the specific words that were bold/colored in the source; source inline character styling is preserved only in the retained ORIGINAL copy, not re-applied to the translation. Layout is bilingual by default: original kept, translation appended after (position/visibility controlled by imt-state=dual|translation|source and imt-trans-position=before|after).
Facts: Content script traversal: 'MutationObserver monitors DOM for text nodes; TreeWalker traverses node tree' and excludes scripts/styles/iframes/[translate=no] before processing (https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/2.3-content-scripts). | It wraps rather than replaces: 'Creates wrapper elements with specific CSS classes; modifies attributes' and 'Wraps original content that can be hidden in translation-only mode' (https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/2.3-content-scripts). | Block vs inline is chosen by element type: block wrapper 'Holds translated text for block elements (p, div, h1-h6)'; inline wrapper 'Holds translated text for inline elements (span, a, em, strong)'. Headings and list items are treated as block-level, so the translation sits in a block container tied to that element and inherits font-size / list markers. | Canonical injected classes: immersive-translate-target-wrapper (original), immersive-translate-target-translation-block-wrapper (block translation container), immersive-translate-target-translation-inline-wrapper (inline translation container), immersive-translate-target-inner (innermost node holding the translated text), plus immersive-translate-target-translation-theme-{name}-wrapper/-inner. | State/position attributes: imt-state = dual | translation | source; imt-trans-position = before | after — these control bilingual vs translation-only display and whether translation goes before/after the original. | Translated text is styled with a single uniform theme, not per-word: Underline ('Adds a 1px solid border bottom to translated text'), Highlight (background color), Marker (gradient highlight), Mask ('Blurs translated text until hovered, creating a spoiler effect'), Blockquote (left border), plus grey/opacity variables like --immersive-translate-theme-grey-textColor and --immersive-translate-theme-opacity-opacity (https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/4.3-styling-system). | DeepWiki explicitly notes character-level inline styling is NOT re-applied: emphasis is on wrapping/injecting translation elements with theme styles; 'word-level inline formatting is not described as preserved'; translated text is rendered inside the dedicated inner element rather than re-stitching original inline styles. | Real-world corroboration of the class names in injected output: GitHub issue #2711 shows immersive-translate-target-translation-block-wrapper and related tags/classes appearing inside translated table HTML on export (https://github.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/issues/2711). | Styles are delivered via an injected stylesheet (dist styles/inject.css) using :root CSS custom properties, attribute/data-state selectors, and responsive rules to switch presentation (https://deepwiki.com/immersive-translate/immersive-translate/4.3-styling-system). | Official positioning (marketing/docs) describes bilingual side-by-side vs translation-only modes and 'preserves original formatting/layout' — framed at the document/layout level, not per-word character styling (https://immersivetranslate.com/en/home). | The public GitHub repo (immersive-translate/immersive-translate) is a releases/distribution repo of prebuilt dist artifacts; the extension is not fully open-source, so the DeepWiki pages are reverse-engineered documentation of those built files.
Risks: Chasing per-word color/bold transfer is a trap: it requires source-to-target word alignment that MT does not provide reliably (word counts and order change across languages); Immersive Translate sidesteps this entirely by using one uniform theme per segment. | The block-level inheritance in Immersive Translate is a side effect of DOM+CSS, not explicit re-styling. Don't over-read it as 'they reconstruct styles' — they simply inject into the existing element. A TUI has no equivalent cascade, so you must explicitly carry the block role across the translation boundary. | Primary technical source (DeepWiki) is AI-generated/reverse-engineered from closed-source dist artifacts; treat exact class/attribute strings as high-confidence-but-not-authoritative (corroborated by issue #2711) rather than canonical API. | The uniform translation styling is intentional and legibility-driven (dim/underline to distinguish translation from original). Reproducing rich inline styling on the translation could actually hurt the 'clearly-marked bilingual' UX that is the point. | Marketing claims of 'preserves formatting/layout' can be over-interpreted; verified behavior is structural/layout preservation of blocks, not fidelity of per-character emphasis inside translated text. | Inline vs block handling adds edge cases (mixed inline emphasis inside a block, tables) — issue #2711 shows their own wrapper tags/classes leaking into exported markdown/HTML, a reminder that even the reference implementation's wrapping approach has messy boundary bugs.

[Finding 3] Preserving inline markup across a translation that reorders words is a long-standing, well-studied hard problem, and the entire localization industry treats the engine's own tag-repositioning as untrustworthy. XLIFF formalizes inline codes as paired (<g>, <bpt>/<ept>, XLIFF2 <pc>) vs standalone/placeholder (<x/>, <ph>, split <bx/><ex/>, XLIFF2 <sc>/<ec>); XLIFF 2.0 even adds canReorder/canCopy/canDelete attributes precisely because reorder, duplication, and deletion of codes are recognized hazards. CAT tools (OmegaT, memoQ) and po4a don't prevent misplacement — they detect it after the fact via mandatory tag validation that blocks export on count mismatch. MT APIs (DeepL tag_handling, Google format=HTML) preserve tags heuristically but openly duplicate/misplace tags on reorder and have documented drops (notranslate spans vanish). MediaWiki's Content Translation team abandoned inline tags to MT entirely: they strip markup, translate plain text, then reattach via subsentence alignment + n-gram fuzzy matching with per-language heuristics — and still hit inflection/reorder/split failures. Documented failure modes: dropped, duplicated, corrupted, wrong-span, split, and whitespace-drift tags. For a streaming terminal tool, per-span ANSI reattachment via placeholder tokens inherits all of this and is fragile unless scoped down.
Facts: XLIFF 1.2 (OASIS) defines inline elements: <g> = generic PAIRED inline code that wraps translatable text (stays attached to its span); <x/> = generic STANDALONE placeholder (a single point, no content); <bx/>/<ex/> = begin/end standalone placeholders that represent the two halves of a paired code split apart, linked by an 'rid' attribute; plus <ph>, <bpt>/<ept>, <it>. Source: https://docs.oasis-open.org/xliff/v1.2/os/xliff-core.html | The <bx/>/<ex/> split-placeholder design is itself the structural root of the ordering-ambiguity failure: because begin and end are independent empty elements, an engine can drop one half, emit them out of order (ex before bx), or duplicate one, producing an unpairable/invalid result. Paired <g> avoids this by construction but cannot represent a span whose start/end land in different re-ordered clauses. | XLIFF 2.0 adds explicit control attributes on inline codes (<pc>, <sc>/<ec>, <ph>): canCopy, canDelete, and canReorder. The standard codifying 'may this code be duplicated / removed / moved?' is direct evidence that copy, delete, and reorder of inline codes are the recognized failure modes the format must guard against. | DeepL tag_handling v2 (tag_handling=xml/html, tag_handling_version=v2) documents that when target word order differs it will DUPLICATE or move tags to stay grammatical — i.e., duplicated tags are an EXPECTED output, not a bug. It is strict on malformed input ('Tag handling parsing failed, please check input.') and needs split_sentences=nonewlines, non_splitting_tags, splitting_tags, ignore_tags, outline_detection=0 to control sentence/tag boundaries. Source: https://developers.deepl.com/docs/xml-and-html-handling/tag-handling-v2 | Google Cloud Translation format=HTML 'does not translate tags, only text between them, and returns the tags around the translated text to the extent possible' (note the hedge). Real reports document wrapper tags being dropped — e.g. <span class="notranslate"> disappearing from output — and extra style attributes injected on RTL direction changes. Sources: https://docs.cloud.google.com/translate/docs/translate-text , https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54466495/span-class-notranslate-tags-disappear-google-cloud-translation-api | MediaWiki Content Translation (Wikipedia) explicitly enumerates the failure modes: 'Corrupt markup', 'Wrongly placed annotations', 'Missing annotations', 'Split annotations' (one source tag maps to multiple target words), and markup 'treated as a form of blank space'. Their solution deliberately does NOT delegate tag placement to MT: they linearize to a LinearDoc, send PLAIN TEXT to the engine, and reattach markup using subsentence ALIGNMENT plus n-gram fuzzy matching, with per-language configurable thresholds — and still note failures from inflection, case, reorder, and word-splitting. Source: https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/Content_translation/Developers/Markup | po4a classifies XML/HTML tags as inline / break / placeholder / untranslated; 'placeholder' content is pulled out and translated separately with a marker left in the parent string, so reordering is handled by the human translator, not the engine. po4a v0.69 shipped a release specifically to 'greatly improve the handling of placeholders in XML', showing it remains an actively-patched corner-case area. Source: https://manpages.debian.org/unstable/po4a/Locale%3A%3APo4a%3A%3AXml.3pm.en.html | CAT tools rely on DETECTION, not prevention: OmegaT provides Tools > Validate Tags to catch missing/altered tags and warns altered tags 'can corrupt compiled targets'; memoQ flags missing inline tags via QA and can BLOCK export on tag-count mismatch (documented user reports of 'unable to export due to missing tags'). This confirms even human-in-the-loop workflows treat tag loss/misplacement as a routine, expected error class. Sources: https://omegat.sourceforge.io/manual-latest/en/index.html , https://docs.memoq.com/current/en/Workspace/inline-tag.html | Academic work confirms it is non-trivial for neural engines: WMT2020 'How Should Markup Tags Be Translated?' compares tag-representation methods and uses data augmentation to TEACH tag placement, showing tag complexity and language pair materially affect reliability (https://aclanthology.org/2020.wmt-1.138/); WMT2022 'Data Augmentation for Inline Tag-Aware NMT' proposes tag-injection training that beats naive detag-and-project (https://aclanthology.org/2022.wmt-1.81/). Dedicated HTML-consistency metrics exist precisely because BLEU doesn't capture tag breakage (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0957417426021366). | Industry best-practice mitigations are consistent: (1) constrain the engine — glossary/ignore_tags/do-not-translate, DeepL tag_handling, XLIFF canReorder=no; (2) placeholder LOCKING — replace inline runs with numbered/opaque non-translatable tokens before MT and RESTORE originals after; (3) out-of-band reattachment via alignment + fuzzy match (MediaWiki). Sources: https://languageops.com/blog/stop-losing-formatting-placeholder-revolution/ , https://www.crisoltranslations.com/our-blog/placeholders-how-to-translate-around-them/ , https://docs.aws.amazon.com/translate/latest/dg/customizing-translations-tags.html | Local project context: the translate hot path streams plain translated text token-by-token; translateSystemPromptConcise in /Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-translate-cli-tui/internal/engine/prompt.go instructs the model to 'Output ONLY the translated text', and engine.go (Stream field, ChunkToken/ChunkDone) is built around incremental token emission. Any placeholder-token/ANSI reattachment step requires buffering the FULL output and post-processing, which defeats streaming and adds the fragile reinsertion stage described above. No ANSI/per-span color preservation feature currently exists in the codebase (grep of internal/, cmd/, TODO.md, backlog/ found none).
Risks: Trusting the LLM/MT to reposition placeholder tokens across word-order change reproduces the exact documented failure set: dropped, duplicated, misplaced-to-wrong-span, split, and whitespace-drifted markers. This is not a tail risk — DeepL duplicates tags by design and MediaWiki abandoned the inline approach because of it. | Buffering the whole translation to run placeholder reinsertion defeats the project's token-by-token streaming design (prompt.go asks for ONLY translated text; engine streams ChunkToken). You lose the latency/UX that is a stated goal, and the concise system prompt would need markup rules that also degrade translation quality. | Split annotations have no correct answer without linguistics: a single ANSI-colored source word can become multiple target words (or vanish via inflection/dropping). Deciding whether the color wraps all pieces, one piece, or none needs alignment data an LLM won't reliably give you. | Fuzzy re-attachment (the only engine-independent robust method) requires subsentence alignment + n-gram matching + per-language heuristics (MediaWiki's own conclusion). That is a large, language-specific subsystem — massive over-engineering for a terminal color nicety. | Validation is mandatory but only detects, never fixes: like OmegaT/memoQ you can catch count/pairing mismatches, but then you must choose a fallback (drop all color? uncolored output?), so the feature is best-effort by nature and will silently lose color on hard sentences. | Opaque placeholder tokens can collide with real content or be word-segmented/normalized by the model; picking a truly inert delimiter (e.g. private-use/control chars) and surviving the model's tendency to renumber or omit them is itself a source of subtle bugs. | Over-scoping trap: implementing general per-span color invites reimplementing a mini-XLIFF inline-code engine (paired vs standalone, rid pairing, canReorder semantics) inside a CLI translate tool — effort wildly disproportionate to a cosmetic terminal feature.

[Finding 4] test
Facts: one | two
Risks: r1 | r2

Adversarially stress-test the recommendation. Argue the strongest case that it is WRONG or incomplete, then argue the strongest case FOR it, then give a balanced final verdict. Consider: does bilingual line-interleaving actually work when a single logical item spans wrapped lines or a table? Is detecting a line's "dominant style" meaningful for tldr output where color is per-word? Would users piping `cmd | translate` actually want bilingual, or just a clean translation? Is there a cheaper win being missed (e.g. just STRIP ansi before sending to the LLM so at least the input isn't polluted)? Is this worth building at all vs a doc note?
Return structured findings where 'summary' is your final balanced verdict.

> implemented this session (signals: "maybe later", "nice to have",
> "工程量太大需要再評估", "先記下來"), add it here with priority + effort tags.
> Do not create new `ROADMAP.md` / `IDEAS.md` / `BACKLOG.md` files —
> `TODO.md` is the single backlog index. Long-form research goes in
> [`backlog/<slug>.md`](backlog/).

<!-- Use the exact section order: P1, P2, P3, P?, Done.
     The bundled scripts/todo-kanban.sh validator only inspects top-level
     `- [ ]` and `- ✅` items inside these sections. Prose paragraphs,
     blockquotes, indented sub-bullets, HTML comments, and `---` rules are
     ignored — feel free to add inline guidance like this without breaking
     machine readability. -->

## P1

Likely next batch — items you'd reach for if you sat down to work today.

## P2

Worth doing, no rush.

- [ ] **[S] Align `just install` with `go install` / `~/.local/bin`** — the `install` recipe copies the binary to `~/.dotfiles/bin` (PATH position 11), which **shadows** the `go install` location `~/.local/bin` (position 13); a stale `just install` copy would win silently. Point the recipe at `GOBIN=$HOME/.local/bin go install .` (or drop it) so both paths agree. See [pitfalls/duplicate-translate-on-path-dotfiles-bin-shadows-local-bin.md](pitfalls/duplicate-translate-on-path-dotfiles-bin-shadows-local-bin.md).

## P3

Someday / nice-to-have.

- [ ] **[M] lingua-go detection upgrade behind the `Detector` seam** — whatlanggo is light/fast but weak on short and mixed-script text; lingua-go is more accurate but heavy (embedded n-gram models → bigger binary, slower init). Swap behind the existing `internal/lang` interface only if short-text detection proves insufficient. Detection is mostly a fallback (Google returns the source, LLM returns `DetectedSource`), so keeping it light is defensible.
- [ ] **[S] MyMemory fallback engine** — trivial flat JSON (`responseData.translatedText`, `matches[]`); wire as an easy secondary free path after Google. Limits: 5k chars/day anon (50k with `&de=email`), 500 B max per `q`, no source auto-detect.
- [ ] **[M] Azure Translator engine behind the engine seam** — the only supported Microsoft path (needs an Azure subscription key). Keyless Bing scraping (`ttranslatev3` + transient `IG`/`IID`/token) is too fragile/ToS-risky for v1. Leave the engine interface ready; add when a key is available.
- [ ] **[S] Drop the whole-request `http.Client{Timeout: 60s}` on the streaming path** — in `internal/engine/llm.go`, `NewLLM` sets a client-wide `Timeout` that Go applies to the *entire* request including reading the streamed body, so a long translation is cut at 60s mid-stream. Now surfaced as `⚠ truncated` (see [pitfalls/llm-stream-truncation-silently-rendered-as-complete.md](pitfalls/llm-stream-truncation-silently-rendered-as-complete.md)) instead of silently accepted, but the long output still can't finish. Fix by relying on `ctx` deadlines for the streaming `Do()` (keep the short timeout only for `Available`/`Models` probes).

## P?

Needs a spike before committing to a real priority. Tag as `[?/Effort]`.

- [ ] **[?/L] Ship prebuilt release binaries (goreleaser + GitHub Releases)** — cross-compile per OS/arch (pure-Go, no cgo — trivial) so hosts without a Go toolchain install via chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal` with a templated `{{ .chezmoi.os }}/{{ .chezmoi.arch }}` URL instead of `go install`. Also unlocks shipping the dictionary DB as a release asset. → [research](backlog/release-binaries.md)
- [ ] **[?/M] Bundle or prebuild the dictionary vs the 67 MB runtime `dict update`** — evaluate embedding a trimmed DB via `go:embed`, or shipping the built ECDICT sqlite + CC-CEDICT as release assets, so first run isn't a big download/build. Weigh binary-size blowup vs first-run friction. → [research](backlog/dict-bundling.md)

## Done

- ✅ [2026-07-09] [P?/L] Wire `translate` into chezmoi dotfiles as an auto-installed go tool — go_tools ansible role added to dotfiles (commit 306bfb0): go install → ~/.local/bin, mise-gated, + cat_go upgrade path. Pending chezmoi apply on normal cadence.

Recently shipped. When implementing an active item, in the same commit run:

```
scripts/promote-todo.sh --title "<substring>" --summary "<one-line shipped summary>"
```

This moves the entry here using the dated `Done` syntax and re-validates.

- ✅ [2026-07-10] [P1/M] Publish as a public repo + `go install` — renamed the module `translate` → `github.com/daviddwlee84/translate`, rewrote 22 internal imports, tagged `v0.1.0`, and confirmed `go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest` installs into `~/.local/bin` (GOBIN-pinned, XDG-clean, stable across mise Go upgrades).

`echo "---README head---" && head -120 README.md 2>/dev/null`

```text
---README head---
# translate

A fast CLI/TUI translation tool for the terminal — one-shot from the shell or an
interactive live-translating TUI. Backed by an auto fallback chain over your
local LLM providers (copilot-proxy → Ollama), a free web API (Google), and a
dictionary, with typo-tolerant language resolution and translation history.

## Install

```sh
go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest   # into $GOBIN (or ~/go/bin)
```

## Build & run

```sh
go build -o translate .      # Go 1.25+ (developed on 1.26)
./translate "hola mundo" --to en          # one-shot
echo "bonjour" | ./translate --to en      # pipe
./translate                               # interactive TUI
```

First run writes a default config to `~/.config/translate/config.toml`; run
`./translate init` for a guided setup that probes which providers are up.

## Usage

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `translate <text>` / `… \| translate` | One-shot translation (streams to a TTY; plain text when piped) |
| `translate` (no args, TTY) | Interactive TUI |
| `translate --to <lang> --from <lang>` | Language override; both are fuzzy (`chinees` → `zh`) |
| `translate --json` | Emit the full structured result |
| `translate define <word>` | Dictionary lookup (bilingual: zh↔en local, or English API) |
| `translate history` / `history search <q>` | Recent history / fuzzy search (`--tsv`, `--json`) |
| `translate init` | Interactive config wizard (probes providers) |
| `translate config path\|show` · `lang resolve <q>` | Introspection helpers |

Flags: `--engine smartauto|auto|<provider>|google`, `--provider`, `--model`, `--tier default|fast|max`, `--preset concise|contextual|dictionary`, `--instructions`, `--pair`/`--pair-with`, `--no-history`, `--debug`.
Env overrides: `TRANSLATE_TARGET`, `TRANSLATE_SOURCE`, `TRANSLATE_ENGINE`, `TRANSLATE_PROVIDER`, `TRANSLATE_MODEL`, `TRANSLATE_CONFIG`, `TRANSLATE_DEBUG`.
Precedence: **flag > env > `[cli]`/`[tui]` > `[general]` > default**.

### Per-front-end defaults (`[cli]` / `[tui]`)

The CLI (arguments or piped stdin) and the interactive TUI share `[general]`, but
either can override any subset of it via an optional `[cli]` or `[tui]` table — e.g.
a snappy concise CLI and a richer, higher-tier TUI:

```toml
[general]
preset = "contextual"
tier   = "fast"

[cli]                 # one-shot / piped translation
preset = "concise"

[tui]                 # interactive front-end
tier          = "max"
live_translate = true
```

Only keys you list are overridden; the rest inherit `[general]`. Flags and env vars
still win over both.

## TUI keys

`↵` translate · live-debounce auto-translates ~700ms after you stop typing (off by default) ·
`^y` copy · `^l` toggle live · `^e` cycle engine · `^t` target language · `^p` model ·
`^o` style · `^g` toggle pair mode · `^u` clear · `⇥` switch input/output focus · `^r` history · `⌥↵` newline · `^c`/`esc` quit.

`⇥` (Tab) moves keyboard focus between the input box and the result box; the focused
box gets an accent border. With the result focused, `↑/↓ j/k PgUp/PgDn g/G space` scroll
it; typing any character snaps focus back to the input. A mouse click focuses the box
it lands in.

## Engines & fallback

The `auto` engine tries `chain.order` in turn, failing over **before the first
token** (so a down provider switches cleanly; a mid-stream error surfaces rather
than restarting):

- **copilot-proxy** — OpenAI-compatible at `http://localhost:4141/v1`, no API key.
  Default tier is **fast** (`claude-haiku-4-5`); `--tier default` → `claude-sonnet-5`,
  `--tier max` → `claude-opus-4-8`. Claude models are served only via the proxy's
  Anthropic Messages endpoint (`/v1/messages`) — the engine routes `claude-*` ids
  there automatically (they 400 on `/chat/completions`).
- **Ollama** — `http://localhost:11434/v1` (offline; `llama3.2:3b`).
- **Google** — free, keyless; also reports the detected source language.
- **openrouter** — configured but off the default chain; needs `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`.

> ⚠️ Using a GitHub Copilot subscription to back non-GitHub tools (copilot-proxy)
> may violate Copilot's Terms of Service. Reorder `chain.order` (drop `copilot`,
> lead with `ollama`/`google`) in the config if that matters to you.

Config lives at `~/.config/translate/config.toml`, history at
`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`, last-pair state at
`~/.local/state/translate/state.json` (XDG-honored on macOS and Linux).

### Smart auto (`smartauto`)

The recommended default (choose it in `translate init`). It routes by input shape:
a **single word/term** is looked up in the dictionary (with the same LLM fallback as
`smart-dict` on a miss), while a **phrase or sentence** is translated by the LLM. Set
`engine = "smartauto"` in `[general]` (or `--engine smartauto`). It composes with
pair mode, so a single word and a full sentence both translate in the right direction.

### Pair mode (bidirectional)

With `pair = true` + `pair_with`, the tool detects which of the two languages your
input is in and translates it into the **other** — one box, both directions
(`你好 → hi`, `test → 測試`). For a CJK/non-CJK pair (e.g. `zh-TW`⇄`en`) direction is
decided by script, so even short input routes correctly; the LLM translate prompt is
also told to detect-and-route and to **never echo** the input. `pair_with` must differ
from the target (`translate init` enforces this; a degenerate config warns).

### Debugging

`--debug` (or `TRANSLATE_DEBUG=1`, or `[general] debug = true`) logs the intermediate
decisions — resolved settings, pair routing, word-vs-phrase classification, dictionary
hit/miss, and chain fallback. The one-shot CLI logs to **stderr**; the TUI logs to
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
===prompt.go===
package engine

import (
	"fmt"
	"strings"

	"github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/internal/lang"
)

// Preset selects the LLM translate prompt style. Only ModeTranslate LLM engines
// honor it; google/dict ignore it.
const (
	PresetConcise    = "concise"    // terse direct translation (default)
	PresetContextual = "contextual" // short list of common-sense translations
	PresetDictionary = "dictionary" // translation + 1-2 example sentences
)

// translateSystemPromptConcise drives the primary (hot-path) translation. It asks
// for ONLY the translated text so the reply streams token-by-token identically
// across every backend and is latency-optimal.
const translateSystemPromptConcise = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
Translate the user's text into the target language faithfully and idiomatically.

Rules:
- Preserve the meaning, tone, and register (formal/informal, technical/casual) of the source.
- The user's text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical phrasing. Interpret their
  INTENDED meaning and translate that; do not translate a typo literally, and never refuse.
- Output ONLY the translated text. No quotes, no explanations, no language labels,
  no commentary before or after.
- ALWAYS translate into the target language. A single word, name, technical term, or
  loanword written in a DIFFERENT language must still be translated — never echo it back.
  Only when the text is ALREADY in the target language should you return it (lightly
  corrected if it was misspelled).`

// translateSystemPromptContextual lists translations across common senses.
const translateSystemPromptContextual = `You are a precise, professional translation engine.
Translate the user's text into the target language, showing how the translation changes
across the most common senses or contexts of the source.

Rules:
- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. The text may contain typos, slang, or ungrammatical
  phrasing — interpret the INTENDED meaning; never refuse and never translate a typo literally.
- Output a SHORT list of 2-4 lines, one line per distinct common sense/context.
- Format each line EXACTLY as: "N. <translation> — <short context label in the TARGET language>".
- Order lines from the most likely/common sense to the least.
- If the text has only one natural sense, output a single line — do not invent senses.
- Output ONLY the list. No preamble, no headings, no commentary before or after.`

// translateSystemPromptDictionary gives a translation plus example sentences.
const translateSystemPromptDictionary = `You are a precise, professional translation engine
that also gives brief usage examples.
Translate the user's text into the target language, then illustrate it with example sentences.

Rules:
- Preserve meaning, tone, and register. Interpret the INTENDED meaning of typos/slang; never refuse.
- The FIRST line is ONLY the translation of the user's text — no label, no quotes.
- Then a blank line, then 1-2 example sentences that use the translation naturally.
- Format each example on two lines:
    line 1: the example sentence in the TARGET language,
    line 2: its translation in the source language (the language of the user's input),
            prefixed with "  ↳ ".
- Keep examples short and idiomatic. Output ONLY the translation and the examples —
  no headings, no numbering of the first line, no commentary.`

// systemPromptFor returns the system prompt for a preset (defaults to concise).
func systemPromptFor(preset string) string {
	switch preset {
	case PresetContextual:
		return translateSystemPromptContextual
	case PresetDictionary:
		return translateSystemPromptDictionary
	default:
		return translateSystemPromptConcise
	}
}

// pairDirective is appended to the system prompt in bidirectional pair mode. It
// makes the model own language detection (more robust than a pre-computed target
// for short/ambiguous input) and forbids echoing the input — the exact failure
// this fixes ("test" → "test" instead of "測試").
const pairDirective = `Bidirectional mode: the text is written in ONE of %s or %s. Detect which of the two it is, and translate it into the OTHER one. ALWAYS translate — never return the text unchanged, even for a single word, a proper name, a technical term, or a loanword.`

// translateUserPrompt frames the concrete request.
const translateUserPrompt = `Source language: %s
Target language: %s

Text:
%s`

// buildTranslatePrompt returns the (system, user) messages for a plain-text
// translation request. Language codes are expanded to readable names so the
// model handles regional variants (e.g. zh-TW → "chinese (traditional)").
func buildTranslatePrompt(req Request) (system, user string) {
	src := strings.TrimSpace(req.Source)
	if src == "" || src == "auto" {
		src = "auto"
	} else {
		src = fmt.Sprintf("%s (%s)", lang.Name(src), src)
	}
	tgt := fmt.Sprintf("%s (%s)", lang.Name(req.Target), req.Target)
	system = systemPromptFor(req.Preset)
	// In pair mode, let the model detect the input language and route to the other
	// side, and never echo — this is appended to (not a replacement of) the preset
	// so the chosen output format (concise/contextual/dictionary) is preserved.
	if req.Pair && req.PairHome != "" && req.PairAway != "" {
		system += "\n\n" + fmt.Sprintf(pairDirective, lang.Name(req.PairHome), lang.Name(req.PairAway))
	}
	if extra := strings.TrimSpace(req.Extra); extra != "" {
		system += "\n\nUser preferences (apply where relevant, e.g. domain terminology):\n" + extra
	}
	return system, fmt.Sprintf(translateUserPrompt, src, tgt, req.Text)
}

// learnDirection reports the learn-mode direction, decided OFFLINE via the same
// script-aware router as pair mode (lang.PairTarget): native (home) input → "teach"
// (translate + glosses); foreign (away) input → "correct" (grammar-correct + explain).
// Trusting this over the model's own guess keeps the two output shapes deterministic.
func learnDirection(req Request) string {
	if lang.PairTarget(req.PairHome, req.PairAway, req.Text) == req.PairAway {
		return "teach"
	}
	return "correct"
}

// learnTeachPrompt drives native→foreign tutoring. %[1]s = native (home) language,
// %[2]s = foreign (away) language being learned.
const learnTeachPrompt = `You are a warm, encouraging language tutor helping a native %[1]s speaker learn %[2]s.
The user writes in %[1]s. Translate their text into natural, idiomatic %[2]s, then teach it.

Respond with ONE JSON object and NOTHING else — no markdown code fence, no prose before or after it.
Schema (fill every relevant field; omit a field only when it genuinely has no content):
{
  "direction": "teach",
  "original": "<the user's %[1]s input, verbatim>",
  "translation": "<the idiomatic %[2]s translation>",
  "vocab": [
    {"term": "<key %[2]s word or phrase>", "pos": "<part of speech>", "phonetic": "<KK/IPA for English, pinyin for Chinese>", "meaning": "<concise meaning in %[1]s>"}
  ],
  "examples": [
    {"foreign": "<a short natural %[2]s sentence using the translation>", "native": "<its %[1]s translation>"}
  ],
  "notes": "<one short usage or register tip in %[1]s, optional>"
}

Rules:
- Write every meaning, note, and explanation in %[1]s (the learner's language).
- vocab: only the key content words/phrases worth learning (at most 8); skip trivial function words.
- examples: 1-2, short and idiomatic.
- Interpret the intended meaning of typos/slang; never refuse. Output ONLY the JSON object.`

// learnCorrectPrompt drives foreign→native correction. %[1]s = foreign (away)
// language being learned, %[2]s = native (home) language.
const learnCorrectPrompt = `You are a warm, encouraging language tutor helping a %[2]s speaker who is learning %[1]s.
The user writes a sentence in %[1]s that may contain mistakes. Correct it and explain, kindly.

Respond with ONE JSON object and NOTHING else — no markdown code fence, no prose before or after it.
Schema (fill every relevant field; omit a field only when it genuinely has no content):
{
  "direction": "correct",
  "original": "<the user's %[1]s input, verbatim>",
  "corrected": "<the grammatically correct, idiomatic %[1]s sentence>",
  "translation": "<a %[2]s translation of the intended meaning>",
  "issues": [
    {"span": "<the problematic fragment of the input>", "fix": "<the corrected fragment>", "explanation": "<why, in %[2]s>"}
  ],
  "notes": "<one short encouraging tip in %[2]s, optional>"
}

Rules:
- Write every explanation and note in %[2]s (the learner's native language).
- If the sentence is already correct, echo it in "corrected" and return "issues": [].
- List each distinct mistake as one issue; keep explanations concise and beginner-friendly.
- Interpret the intended meaning of typos/slang; never refuse. Output ONLY the JSON object.`

// learnUserPrompt frames the concrete learn request. The system prompt already
// names both languages, so the user turn carries only the text.
const learnUserPrompt = `Text:
%s`

// buildLearnPrompt returns the (system, user) messages for a learn-mode request,
// choosing the teach or correct prompt from the offline-detected direction. Codes
// are expanded to readable names so regional variants (zh-TW) read naturally.
func buildLearnPrompt(req Request) (system, user string) {
	home := lang.Name(req.PairHome) // native
	away := lang.Name(req.PairAway) // foreign
	if learnDirection(req) == "teach" {
		system = fmt.Sprintf(learnTeachPrompt, home, away)
	} else {
		system = fmt.Sprintf(learnCorrectPrompt, away, home)
	}
	if extra := strings.TrimSpace(req.Extra); extra != "" {
		system += "\n\nUser preferences (apply where relevant, e.g. domain terminology):\n" + extra
	}
	return system, fmt.Sprintf(learnUserPrompt, req.Text)
}
```
</details></tool-use>
