I'm settling on the flag design with `--bilingual` and `-2` as the short form, then I'll skip translating code blocks and only handle prose content. Since this is a flag-only pattern that gets read directly at the call site, I can keep it simple. Now I'm drafting the plan file following the required structure—laying out the context, the recommended approach, and the two main parts covering the ANSI stripping fix and the bilingual mode implementation, along with which files need changes.

For the implementation, I'm thinking through how to handle the ANSI stripping cleanly: keep the raw bytes intact for bilingual display purposes, but strip ANSI codes before sending text to the LLM. I'll create a new internal package for the testable helpers like block-splitting and code detection logic, rather than scattering trivial wrappers throughout. Defining the bitext package structure... But that's acceptable as a limitation for now. I could refine it further by checking if indented blocks look command-like—few natural words, lots of flags or shell syntax—but keeping it simple works: a block counts as code if every non-blank line starts with at least 2 spaces of indentation. That matches the tldr format well enough.

Now for the rendering side, I'm building a new function that takes the raw bilingual text, splits it into blocks, identifies which prose sections need translation, and then fans out translation requests for each prose block's plain text.  When translations come back, I'm collecting them in an ordered results slice keyed by block index, respecting context cancellation and gracefully handling failures by skipping translation for that block and collecting warnings to report at the end. Then during rendering, I iterate through blocks in order—blanking blank lines, printing code blocks verbatim with ANSI formatting, and for prose blocks printing the original followed by the translation indented with a dimmed arrow prefix if we're in a TTY and NO_COLOR isn't set.

For the history tracking, I'm skipping per-block history in v1 since bilingual is just a view. I'm also extracting a reusable `translateOnce` helper that wraps the core translation logic without printing, keeping it separate from the streaming oneShot function to minimize risk and avoid duplication.

Now I'm handling the styling for continuation lines—I'll use lipgloss to apply a dim color (#6C6C6C) that matches the TUI's existing dim style, but only when stdout is a TTY and NO_COLOR isn't set; otherwise I'll render plain text. I'm adding a small helper function to conditionally apply this styling.

I need to verify that lipgloss v2 respects the TTY detection and NO_COLOR environment variable automatically, so my manual gate will work cleanly with its color profile detection. Then I'll wire up the bilingual flag as a package variable in root.go and register it with the command.

For the bilingual mode, I'm adding a boolean flag that users can pass to keep both the original colored output and the translation displayed together. The flag registration uses a single-character shorthand, and I'll check it directly at the pipe branch to route to the bilingual handler. For now, I'm keeping bilingual support limited to the stdin pipe mode in v1, so if someone passes the flag with arguments, it'll just fall through to the normal single-shot behavior.

I also need to make sure the LLM input gets stripped the same way as the normal path, and bilingual mode should force the concise preset—if a user tries to combine it with contextual or dictionary presets, those get ignored since they'd break the 1:1 line pairing. For interactions with `--json` and `--bilingual`, I'm treating them as mutually exclusive with json taking precedence, and I'll document that bilingual implies concise mode.

On the concurrency side, I'm using a bounded semaphore with a limit of 5 concurrent goroutines, a wait group, and pre-sized result slices so each goroutine writes to its own index without needing a mutex. The context gets passed through to respect cancellation. For tests, I'm setting up a new test file to cover the bilingual functionality. Defining the Render function signature...

Now I need to document this in the README with a new bilingual mode section explaining the feature—original line stays colored with translation underneath, each translation line indented and styled, only works with piped input, skips code blocks and tables, and strips ANSI from input before translating. I'll also create a design doc covering the approach, why we rejected alternatives like dominant-color placeholders or token-based systems (noting the failures in XLIFF, DeepL, and MediaWiki), the x/ansi API considerations, and the heuristics with known limitations around wrapped prose and tables.

For the backlog index and TODO tracking, I'll add a Done entry for this feature since it's shipping this session, plus a P3 follow-up for improving table and wrapped-prose awareness.

Since a new flag means a minor version bump, I should note that in the plan but remember that actual tagging is a separate manual step handled outside this code change.

For verification, I'll run the standard checks with `just check` and `just test`, then walk through a few manual scenarios: testing ANSI escape code stripping to ensure clean translations without any codes leaking through, checking bilingual mode preserves the original colored output with dimmed translations underneath while leaving code blocks untouched, and confirming the pipe-clean behavior works end-to-end.

There's a design tension here—bilingual mode is meant as a terminal reading experience where our dim styling gets suppressed when piped to non-TTY output, but the original ANSI codes pass through unchanged. That's acceptable since bilingual is explicitly opt-in and documented as a reading mode rather than a general-purpose filter. I'll document that piping bilingual output onward isn't the intended workflow, though the original formatting is preserved.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 05:40:08Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```md
# Plan: `--bilingual` immersive pipe mode + ANSI-strip-on-input

## Context

Today, `tldr rg | translate --to zh.TW` loses all color: the pipe branch reads
stdin, `TrimSpace`es it, and sends the whole blob — **ANSI escape codes and all** —
to the LLM (`cmd/root.go:205-214`), which returns one plain paragraph printed with
`fmt.Println` (`cmd/root.go:468-469`). The user wants the Immersive-Translate
experience: keep the original (with its styling) and show the translation
alongside it.

Investigation (see the companion `backlog/` doc) established two things:

1. **Per-word color transfer (re-coloring `grep` red in the translation) is
   over-engineering** and is *not* what Immersive Translate does — it preserves
   **block-level** style and renders the translation in one uniform (dimmed)
   theme. Chasing per-span color reproduces the XLIFF/DeepL inline-tag
   reordering problem (dropped/duplicated/misplaced tags), defeats streaming, and
   needs cross-lingual word alignment. Rejected.
2. The right altitude is a **bilingual, block-interleaved reading mode**: original
   line kept verbatim (color intact) + translation beneath in a uniform dim style.
   And separately, a **one-call hygiene fix**: strip ANSI from piped input before
   it reaches the LLM.

`github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi` (already an indirect dep) provides `ansi.Strip`
and a CJK-aware tokenizer, so no new third-party dep is needed.

This plan ships **two independent changes + docs**:

- **Fix (patch):** strip ANSI from stdin before translating (helps every piped run).
- **Feat (minor):** opt-in `--bilingual` / `-2` mode for the pipe path.

## Decisions (confirmed with user)

- Flag: **`--bilingual`** with shorthand **`-2`**.
- Mixed prose+code input: **skip code/command blocks** (echo verbatim, untranslated), translate only prose.
- **Flag-only** (standalone, Pattern B) — never a config/pipe default. Protects the Unix-filter contract documented at `cmd/root.go:411-413`.

---

## Part 1 — Strip ANSI from piped input (fix)

**File:** `cmd/root.go`, pipe branch (`case !term.IsTerminal(...Stdin...)`, ~line 204-209).

- Keep the raw bytes (needed by Part 2), but the text sent to the engine becomes
  ANSI-free: `text := strings.TrimSpace(bitext.Strip(string(b)))`.
- `bitext.Strip` is a thin wrapper over `ansi.Strip` (new package below), so the
  import lives in one testable place.
- No behavior change for output; strictly cleaner/cheaper LLM input. Safe for all
  piped runs regardless of `--bilingual`.

---

## Part 2 — `--bilingual` / `-2` mode (feat)

### New package `internal/bitext` (pure, testable)

Per repo convention, logic that needs coverage lives under `internal/` (there are
no `cmd/` tests). New file `internal/bitext/bitext.go`:

\```go
package bitext

type Kind int
const ( Blank Kind = iota; Prose; Code )

// Block is one blank-line-delimited unit of piped input.
type Block struct {
    Raw   string // original lines joined, ANSI intact (for display)
    Plain string // ANSI-stripped text (for the LLM + classification)
    Kind  Kind
}

func Strip(s string) string          // wraps ansi.Strip
func Split(raw string) []Block       // split on blank lines, classify each
func Render(blocks []Block, translations map[int]string, dim func(string) string) string
\```

- **`Split`**: break input into blocks separated by blank line(s). Consecutive
  non-blank lines coalesce into one block (this also groups soft-wrapped prose so
  it translates with sentence context).
- **Classification heuristic** (`Code`): a block is `Code` when every non-blank
  physical line begins with ≥2 columns of indentation (matches tldr/man example
  blocks like `    rg pattern`). Everything else with letters is `Prose`. This is
  the "skip code, translate prose" decision. Documented as a heuristic with known
  misfires (fully-indented man pages, tables — see Limitations).
- **`Render`**: emit each block's `Raw` verbatim; for `Prose` blocks whose index
  has a translation, append the translation beneath, each line prefixed `  ↳ `
  (matching the house glyph in `renderLearnCLI`, `cmd/root.go:496-538`) and passed
  through `dim()`. `Code`/`Blank` blocks pass through untouched. Keeping styling in
  a caller-supplied `dim` closure keeps `Render` pure (test with identity func).

### `cmd/root.go` wiring

1. **Flag (Pattern B, standalone):** add `flagBilingual bool` to the var block
   (~line 30-47) and register in `NewRootCmd` (~line 82):
   `f.BoolVarP(&flagBilingual, "bilingual", "2", false, "pipe mode: keep original (with color) + translation beneath")`.
   Read directly at the call site — **no** Overrides/Resolve/Config wiring
   (mirrors `flagJSON`/`flagNoHistory`).

2. **Dispatch:** in the stdin pipe branch, before the normal `oneShot` call:
   `if flagBilingual && !flagJSON && !res.Learn { return runBilingual(ctx, oneShotEng, string(b), src, effTgt, res, stdoutTTY) }`.
   `--bilingual` is honored **only on the pipe path**; with positional args it
   falls through to normal `oneShot` (documented). `--json`/`--learn` keep their
   structured output and take precedence.

3. **`translateOnce` helper (reuse seam):** extract the non-printing core
   (`eng.Translate(ctx, req)` → `engine.Drain(ch, nil)`) into
   `func translateOnce(ctx, eng engine.Engine, req engine.Request) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)`.
   Used by `runBilingual`; `oneShot` left as-is to limit blast radius.

4. **`runBilingual`:**
   - `blocks := bitext.Split(raw)`.
   - For each `Prose` block, translate `block.Plain` via `translateOnce` with a
     Request forcing `Stream:false` and `Preset:""` (**concise** — contextual/
     dictionary presets reshape output and would break the 1-block↔1-translation
     mapping). Reuse `src`/`effTgt`/`res.Instructions`; ignore pair/learn here.
   - **Bounded concurrency** (no new dep): `sem := make(chan struct{}, 5)` +
     `sync.WaitGroup`; each goroutine writes its own index into a
     `map[int]string` guarded by a mutex (or a pre-sized slice). Derive per-call
     work from `ctx` so cancellation propagates. Per-block calls are short, so the
     60s `http.Client` timeout footgun (TODO.md P2) is not triggered the way one
     giant concatenated call would be.
   - On a block's translation error: skip its translation (still print the
     original) and collect a one-line `stderr` warning at the end — never silent,
     matching the existing warning idiom (`cmd/root.go:450-455`).
   - Print `bitext.Render(blocks, translations, dim)`.

5. **`dim` styling (pipe-safe):** a small local helper. Style only when
   `stdoutTTY && os.Getenv("NO_COLOR") == ""`, using
   `lg.NewStyle().Foreground(lg.Color("#6C6C6C"))` (the TUI's `colDim`,
   `internal/tui/styles.go:8,46`; the primitive is a one-liner — no shared helper
   exists to import). Otherwise identity (plain `  ↳ text`). The **original block's
   own ANSI always passes through** (that's the whole point); only our *added*
   translation lines are gated.

6. **History/speak in bilingual:** v1 skips per-block history and `--speak`
   (bilingual is a multi-block reading view). Documented.

### Known limitations (documented, not fixed in v1)

- Tabular/aligned output (`ls -l`, `kubectl get`, `git status`) — interleaving
  breaks columns; bilingual is meant for prose/docs (tldr, man, `--help`).
- Fully-indented man pages may be misclassified as all-`Code` (skipped).

The plan is written. Here's the summary of what I'm proposing:

**Two shippable changes + docs:**

1. **Fix (patch)** — strip ANSI from piped stdin before it reaches the LLM. One-call hygiene win using `ansi.Strip` (dep already present), helps *every* piped run.

2. **Feat (minor)** — opt-in `--bilingual` / `-2` pipe mode: keeps each original line verbatim (color intact) and prints the translation beneath it in uniform dim, block-interleaved like Immersive Translate. New pure `internal/bitext` package (`Split`/`Strip`/`Render`) does the testable work; `cmd/root.go` wires the flag (standalone, no config), a `translateOnce` reuse helper, bounded-concurrency per-block translation, and TTY/`NO_COLOR`-gated dim styling. **Code/command blocks are skipped** (echoed verbatim), only prose is translated — matching the preview you picked.

3. **Docs** — `backlog/bilingual-immersive-mode.md` (design + Immersive-Translate competitor analysis + why per-word color / approaches A & B were rejected, with the XLIFF/DeepL/MediaWiki grounding), a README `### Bilingual mode` section + flag row, and TODO Done/P3-follow-up entries — all following the repo's `TODO.md`/`backlog/` conventions.

**Deliberately rejected:** per-word color transfer (approach B) and dominant-color (A) — over-engineering, documented in the backlog doc.

Key limitations I've documented rather than solved in v1: tables/columns and hard-wrapped prose (bilingual targets prose docs like tldr/man/`--help`).

## Approved Plan (edited by user):
# Plan: `--bilingual` immersive pipe mode + ANSI-strip-on-input

## Context

Today, `tldr rg | translate --to zh.TW` loses all color: the pipe branch reads
stdin, `TrimSpace`es it, and sends the whole blob — **ANSI escape codes and all** —
to the LLM (`cmd/root.go:205-214`), which returns one plain paragraph printed with
`fmt.Println` (`cmd/root.go:468-469`). The user wants the Immersive-Translate
experience: keep the original (with its styling) and show the translation
alongside it.

Investigation (see the companion `backlog/` doc) established two things:

1. **Per-word color transfer (re-coloring `grep` red in the translation) is
   over-engineering** and is *not* what Immersive Translate does — it preserves
   **block-level** style and renders the translation in one uniform (dimmed)
   theme. Chasing per-span color reproduces the XLIFF/DeepL inline-tag
   reordering problem (dropped/duplicated/misplaced tags), defeats streaming, and
   needs cross-lingual word alignment. Rejected.
2. The right altitude is a **bilingual, block-interleaved reading mode**: original
   line kept verbatim (color intact) + translation beneath in a uniform dim style.
   And separately, a **one-call hygiene fix**: strip ANSI from piped input before
   it reaches the LLM.

`github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi` (already an indirect dep) provides `ansi.Strip`
and a CJK-aware tokenizer, so no new third-party dep is needed.

This plan ships **two independent changes + docs**:

- **Fix (patch):** strip ANSI from stdin before translating (helps every piped run).
- **Feat (minor):** opt-in `--bilingual` / `-2` mode for the pipe path.

## Decisions (confirmed with user)

- Flag: **`--bilingual`** with shorthand **`-2`**.
- Mixed prose+code input: **skip code/command blocks** (echo verbatim, untranslated), translate only prose.
- **Flag-only** (standalone, Pattern B) — never a config/pipe default. Protects the Unix-filter contract documented at `cmd/root.go:411-413`.

---

## Part 1 — Strip ANSI from piped input (fix)

**File:** `cmd/root.go`, pipe branch (`case !term.IsTerminal(...Stdin...)`, ~line 204-209).

- Keep the raw bytes (needed by Part 2), but the text sent to the engine becomes
  ANSI-free: `text := strings.TrimSpace(bitext.Strip(string(b)))`.
- `bitext.Strip` is a thin wrapper over `ansi.Strip` (new package below), so the
  import lives in one testable place.
- No behavior change for output; strictly cleaner/cheaper LLM input. Safe for all
  piped runs regardless of `--bilingual`.

---

## Part 2 — `--bilingual` / `-2` mode (feat)

### New package `internal/bitext` (pure, testable)

Per repo convention, logic that needs coverage lives under `internal/` (there are
no `cmd/` tests). New file `internal/bitext/bitext.go`:

```go
package bitext

type Kind int
const ( Blank Kind = iota; Prose; Code )

// Block is one blank-line-delimited unit of piped input.
type Block struct {
    Raw   string // original lines joined, ANSI intact (for display)
    Plain string // ANSI-stripped text (for the LLM + classification)
    Kind  Kind
}

func Strip(s string) string          // wraps ansi.Strip
func Split(raw string) []Block       // split on blank lines, classify each
func Render(blocks []Block, translations map[int]string, dim func(string) string) string
```

- **`Split`**: break input into blocks separated by blank line(s). Consecutive
  non-blank lines coalesce into one block (this also groups soft-wrapped prose so
  it translates with sentence context).
- **Classification heuristic** (`Code`): a block is `Code` when every non-blank
  physical line begins with ≥2 columns of indentation (matches tldr/man example
  blocks like `    rg pattern`). Everything else with letters is `Prose`. This is
  the "skip code, translate prose" decision. Documented as a heuristic with known
  misfires (fully-indented man pages, tables — see Limitations).
- **`Render`**: emit each block's `Raw` verbatim; for `Prose` blocks whose index
  has a translation, append the translation beneath, each line prefixed `  ↳ `
  (matching the house glyph in `renderLearnCLI`, `cmd/root.go:496-538`) and passed
  through `dim()`. `Code`/`Blank` blocks pass through untouched. Keeping styling in
  a caller-supplied `dim` closure keeps `Render` pure (test with identity func).

### `cmd/root.go` wiring

1. **Flag (Pattern B, standalone):** add `flagBilingual bool` to the var block
   (~line 30-47) and register in `NewRootCmd` (~line 82):
   `f.BoolVarP(&flagBilingual, "bilingual", "2", false, "pipe mode: keep original (with color) + translation beneath")`.
   Read directly at the call site — **no** Overrides/Resolve/Config wiring
   (mirrors `flagJSON`/`flagNoHistory`).

2. **Dispatch:** in the stdin pipe branch, before the normal `oneShot` call:
   `if flagBilingual && !flagJSON && !res.Learn { return runBilingual(ctx, oneShotEng, string(b), src, effTgt, res, stdoutTTY) }`.
   `--bilingual` is honored **only on the pipe path**; with positional args it
   falls through to normal `oneShot` (documented). `--json`/`--learn` keep their
   structured output and take precedence.

3. **`translateOnce` helper (reuse seam):** extract the non-printing core
   (`eng.Translate(ctx, req)` → `engine.Drain(ch, nil)`) into
   `func translateOnce(ctx, eng engine.Engine, req engine.Request) (*engine.TranslateResult, error)`.
   Used by `runBilingual`; `oneShot` left as-is to limit blast radius.

4. **`runBilingual`:**
   - `blocks := bitext.Split(raw)`.
   - For each `Prose` block, translate `block.Plain` via `translateOnce` with a
     Request forcing `Stream:false` and `Preset:""` (**concise** — contextual/
     dictionary presets reshape output and would break the 1-block↔1-translation
     mapping). Reuse `src`/`effTgt`/`res.Instructions`; ignore pair/learn here.
   - **Bounded concurrency** (no new dep): `sem := make(chan struct{}, 5)` +
     `sync.WaitGroup`; each goroutine writes its own index into a
     `map[int]string` guarded by a mutex (or a pre-sized slice). Derive per-call
     work from `ctx` so cancellation propagates. Per-block calls are short, so the
     60s `http.Client` timeout footgun (TODO.md P2) is not triggered the way one
     giant concatenated call would be.
   - On a block's translation error: skip its translation (still print the
     original) and collect a one-line `stderr` warning at the end — never silent,
     matching the existing warning idiom (`cmd/root.go:450-455`).
   - Print `bitext.Render(blocks, translations, dim)`.

5. **`dim` styling (pipe-safe):** a small local helper. Style only when
   `stdoutTTY && os.Getenv("NO_COLOR") == ""`, using
   `lg.NewStyle().Foreground(lg.Color("#6C6C6C"))` (the TUI's `colDim`,
   `internal/tui/styles.go:8,46`; the primitive is a one-liner — no shared helper
   exists to import). Otherwise identity (plain `  ↳ text`). The **original block's
   own ANSI always passes through** (that's the whole point); only our *added*
   translation lines are gated.

6. **History/speak in bilingual:** v1 skips per-block history and `--speak`
   (bilingual is a multi-block reading view). Documented.

### Known limitations (documented, not fixed in v1)

- Tabular/aligned output (`ls -l`, `kubectl get`, `git status`) — interleaving
  breaks columns; bilingual is meant for prose/docs (tldr, man, `--help`).
- Fully-indented man pages may be misclassified as all-`Code` (skipped).
- Hard-wrapped prose split by blank lines loses cross-block context.

---

Per `AGENTS.md:39-96` conventions (no new `docs/` dir; `TODO.md` is the single index):

1. **`backlog/bilingual-immersive-mode.md`** (new) — the design + competitor

is a separate manual step, out of scope unless requested).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm verifying the `ansi.Strip` function signature from the charmbracelet/x/ansi package at v0.11.7, which confirms it takes a string and returns a string. Now I'm ready to start building the bitext package.

For the Split algorithm, I need to iterate through the raw input line by line, grouping consecutive non-blank lines into blocks while treating each blank line as its own Blank block to preserve exact spacing for rendering. A line counts as blank if stripping whitespace and ANSI codes leaves nothing.

For classification, a non-blank block is Code if every physical line has at least 2 spaces of indentation (checking the plain form to avoid ANSI codes interfering), otherwise it's Prose. Single flush-left lines like command headers get classified as Prose and translated, which is acceptable even if the translation seems odd.

Looking at the approved preview though, the "rg" header appears without a translation line beneath it, which suggests single-token lines might need special handling. I'm thinking I should refine the heuristic: only translate Prose blocks that contain actual translatable content—multiple words or sufficient length—and skip single short tokens like "rg" that function as headings.

Actually, a cleaner approach might be to suppress translations that match the source text (case-insensitive), since the LLM often echoes proper nouns and command names back unchanged. That way I avoid showing redundant translations like "grep ↳ grep" and handle cases like "rg" naturally in the rendering logic. Equality-suppression will handle the echo case cleanly without over-engineering. I'll keep the split and classification straightforward (Blank/Prose/Code by indentation), put the echo-skipping logic in runBilingual, and attach translations per-block to print after the original. The Render function will take blocks, a translations map, and a dim function to format output for each block index.

Building the Render function... Handling trailing newlines in the split and making sure the dim function applies styling to the translation lines correctly in the bilingual output.

For indentation classification, I need to use the plain stripped version of each line since ANSI codes would throw off the indent count — so a colored line like `\x1b[...m    rg` strips to `    rg` giving the correct indent of 4. I'll compute Plain as the stripped and joined text of each block for the LLM, and use per-line stripped indentation for classification.

Writing bitext.go now with a Strip function that removes ANSI codes...

Defining the Kind type and constants... Defining the Split function to parse piped input into blocks... Finishing the classification logic and defining a helper function to count leading whitespace columns, accounting for tabs as multi-column indents. Then I'm working on the Render function that outputs the bilingual content with translations prefixed and styled appropriately. Refining the translation formatting so the first line gets the arrow prefix while continuation lines align underneath with just spaces, then moving on to handle the equality-suppression logic in the bilingual runner. Writing bitext.go...

<tool-use data-tool-type="write" data-tool-name="Write"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Write**</summary>

```go
// Package bitext splits piped terminal input into blank-line-delimited blocks and
// renders a bilingual ("immersive") view: each original block is kept verbatim
// (ANSI/color intact) and a translation is shown beneath prose blocks. It powers
// the CLI's --bilingual pipe mode. ANSI stripping is delegated to
// github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi so the tokenizer stays wcwidth/CJK-aware.
package bitext

import (
	"strings"

	"github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi"
)

// Kind classifies a block of piped input.
type Kind int

const (
	// Blank is a whitespace-only line, preserved so original spacing survives.
	Blank Kind = iota
	// Prose is natural-language text — the only kind that gets translated.
	Prose
	// Code is an indented command/example block — echoed verbatim, not translated.
	Code
)

// Block is one unit of piped input: a run of consecutive non-blank lines, or a
// single blank line.
type Block struct {
	Raw   string // original line(s) joined by "\n", ANSI escapes intact (for display)
	Plain string // Raw with all ANSI/SGR escapes stripped (for the LLM + classification)
	Kind  Kind
}

// Strip removes every ANSI/SGR escape sequence from s, returning display text.
func Strip(s string) string { return ansi.Strip(s) }

// codeIndent is the leading-column threshold at/above which a block counts as a
// command/code example (matches tldr/man's indented example blocks). A single
// leading tab also clears it.
const codeIndent = 2

// Split breaks raw piped input into blocks. Consecutive non-blank lines coalesce
// into one block (grouping soft-wrapped prose so it translates with context); each
// blank line becomes its own Blank block so original spacing is reproduced. A
// trailing newline is trimmed so it does not yield a spurious final blank block.
func Split(raw string) []Block {
	raw = strings.TrimRight(raw, "\n")
	if raw == "" {
		return nil
	}
	lines := strings.Split(raw, "\n")
	var blocks []Block
	var cur []string // current run of non-blank raw lines

	flush := func() {
		if len(cur) == 0 {
			return
		}
		rawBlk := strings.Join(cur, "\n")
		blocks = append(blocks, Block{
			Raw:   rawBlk,
			Plain: Strip(rawBlk),
			Kind:  classify(cur),
		})
		cur = nil
	}

	for _, ln := range lines {
		if strings.TrimSpace(Strip(ln)) == "" {
			flush()
			blocks = append(blocks, Block{Raw: ln, Kind: Blank})
			continue
		}
		cur = append(cur, ln)
	}
	flush()
	return blocks
}

// classify returns Code when every (stripped) line is indented at least codeIndent
// columns, else Prose.
func classify(rawLines []string) Kind {
	for _, ln := range rawLines {
		if leadingCols(Strip(ln)) < codeIndent {
			return Prose
		}
	}
	return Code
}

// leadingCols counts leading whitespace columns, treating a tab as codeIndent.
func leadingCols(s string) int {
	n := 0
	for _, r := range s {
		switch r {
		case ' ':
			n++
		case '\t':
			n += codeIndent
		default:
			return n
		}
	}
	return n
}

// Render assembles the bilingual output. Each block's Raw is emitted verbatim; for
// Prose blocks whose index has a translation, each translation line is emitted
// beneath — the first prefixed "  ↳ ", continuations aligned under it — and passed
// through dim (identity when styling is off). Code and Blank blocks pass through
// untouched.
func Render(blocks []Block, translations map[int]string, dim func(string) string) string {
	if dim == nil {
		dim = func(s string) string { return s }
	}
	var b strings.Builder
	for i, blk := range blocks {
		b.WriteString(blk.Raw)
		b.WriteByte('\n')
		tr, ok := translations[i]
		if !ok || blk.Kind != Prose {
			continue
		}
		for j, ln := range strings.Split(strings.TrimRight(tr, "\n"), "\n") {
			prefix := "  ↳ "
			if j > 0 {
				prefix = "    "
			}
			b.WriteString(dim(prefix + ln))
			b.WriteByte('\n')
		}
	}
	return b.String()
}

```

```go
package bitext

import (
	"strings"
	"testing"
)

func TestStrip(t *testing.T) {
	cases := []struct {
		name string
		in   string
		want string
	}{
		{"plain", "hello", "hello"},
		{"sgr", "\x1b[1;31mHello\x1b[0m", "Hello"},
		{"sgr_cjk", "\x1b[32m世界\x1b[0m!", "世界!"},
		{"mixed", "a \x1b[31mfaster\x1b[0m alt to \x1b[31mgrep\x1b[0m.", "a faster alt to grep."},
	}
	for _, c := range cases {
		t.Run(c.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			if got := Strip(c.in); got != c.want {
				t.Errorf("Strip(%q) = %q, want %q", c.in, got, c.want)
			}
		})
	}
}

func TestSplit(t *testing.T) {
	// A tldr-shaped fixture: a flush-left command name, a two-line prose block, and
	// an indented (colored) command example.
	input := "\x1b[35mrg\x1b[0m\n\n" +
		"Ripgrep, a \x1b[32mfast\x1b[0m tool.\nMore info.\n\n" +
		"    rg \x1b[31mpattern\x1b[0m\n"

	blocks := Split(input)
	want := []struct {
		kind  Kind
		plain string
	}{
		{Prose, "rg"},
		{Blank, ""},
		{Prose, "Ripgrep, a fast tool.\nMore info."},
		{Blank, ""},
		{Code, "    rg pattern"},
	}
	if len(blocks) != len(want) {
		t.Fatalf("Split returned %d blocks, want %d: %#v", len(blocks), len(want), blocks)
	}
	for i, w := range want {
		if blocks[i].Kind != w.kind {
			t.Errorf("block %d Kind = %d, want %d", i, blocks[i].Kind, w.kind)
		}
		if blocks[i].Plain != w.plain {
			t.Errorf("block %d Plain = %q, want %q", i, blocks[i].Plain, w.plain)
		}
	}
	// Raw must retain the original ANSI so display keeps color.
	if !strings.Contains(blocks[0].Raw, "\x1b[35m") {
		t.Errorf("block 0 Raw lost ANSI: %q", blocks[0].Raw)
	}
	if !strings.Contains(blocks[4].Raw, "\x1b[31m") {
		t.Errorf("code block Raw lost ANSI: %q", blocks[4].Raw)
	}
}

func TestSplitClassify(t *testing.T) {
	cases := []struct {
		name string
		in   string
		want Kind
	}{
		{"flush_left_prose", "just a sentence.", Prose},
		{"two_space_indent_is_code", "  indented example", Code},
		{"tab_indent_is_code", "\tcmd --flag", Code},
		{"one_space_is_prose", " nearly flush", Prose},
		{"mixed_block_is_prose", "flush line\n    indented continuation", Prose},
	}
	for _, c := range cases {
		t.Run(c.name, func(t *testing.T) {
			blocks := Split(c.in)
			if len(blocks) != 1 {
				t.Fatalf("expected 1 block, got %d", len(blocks))
			}
			if blocks[0].Kind != c.want {
				t.Errorf("Kind = %d, want %d", blocks[0].Kind, c.want)
			}
		})
	}
}

func TestSplitEmpty(t *testing.T) {
	if got := Split(""); got != nil {
		t.Errorf("Split(\"\") = %#v, want nil", got)
	}
	if got := Split("\n\n"); len(got) != 2 {
		t.Errorf("Split(blank-only) = %d blocks, want 2", len(got))
	}
}

func TestRender(t *testing.T) {
	blocks := []Block{
		{Raw: "\x1b[35mrg\x1b[0m", Plain: "rg", Kind: Prose},
		{Raw: "", Kind: Blank},
		{Raw: "A search tool.", Plain: "A search tool.", Kind: Prose},
		{Raw: "    rg pattern", Plain: "    rg pattern", Kind: Code},
	}
	translations := map[int]string{
		0: "rg",                  // echo — caller may drop it, but Render honors the map
		2: "一個搜尋工具。",       // prose translation
		3: "should be ignored",   // Code must never render a translation
	}
	out := Render(blocks, translations, nil) // nil dim => identity

	if !strings.Contains(out, "  ↳ 一個搜尋工具。") {
		t.Errorf("prose translation missing/malformed:\n%s", out)
	}
	if strings.Contains(out, "should be ignored") {
		t.Errorf("Code block was translated:\n%s", out)
	}
	if !strings.Contains(out, "\x1b[35mrg\x1b[0m") {
		t.Errorf("original ANSI not preserved:\n%s", out)
	}
	// Blank block reproduces the empty line between original and next block.
	if !strings.Contains(out, "rg\n\n") {
		t.Errorf("blank spacing not reproduced:\n%q", out)
	}
}

func TestRenderDimApplied(t *testing.T) {
	blocks := []Block{{Raw: "hi", Plain: "hi", Kind: Prose}}
	out := Render(blocks, map[int]string{0: "嗨"}, func(s string) string { return "<" + s + ">" })
	if !strings.Contains(out, "<  ↳ 嗨>") {
		t.Errorf("dim func not applied to translation line:\n%s", out)
	}
	if strings.Contains(out, "<hi>") {
		t.Errorf("dim func wrongly applied to original line:\n%s", out)
	}
}

func TestRenderMultilineTranslation(t *testing.T) {
	blocks := []Block{{Raw: "orig", Plain: "orig", Kind: Prose}}
	out := Render(blocks, map[int]string{0: "line one\nline two"}, nil)
	if !strings.Contains(out, "  ↳ line one\n    line two\n") {
		t.Errorf("multiline translation not aligned:\n%q", out)
	}
}

```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
When I trim the input `"   \n\t\n"` with `TrimRight(..., "\n")`, it only removes trailing newlines, leaving `"   \n\t"`. Splitting that gives me `["   ", "\t"]`, and since both lines are whitespace-only, they each become Blank blocks—so the function returns 2 Blank blocks rather than nil. The issue is that my `TrimRight` only handles newlines, not other whitespace characters, which means lines containing only spaces become Blank blocks, though that's actually acceptable behavior.

Looking at this more carefully, the test case with spaces is an unrealistic edge case. I should simplify by removing that specific sub-case and keeping only the `""` and `"\n\n"` tests, both of which correctly return nil. The behavior where whitespace-with-spaces produces Blank blocks isn't worth special-casing, so I'll adjust the test to focus on the two cases that should definitely return nil.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
The pty output file came back empty, which suggests the `script` command on macOS didn't capture the output properly—either the input redirection failed or the translation hit a rate limit. I should try a more straightforward approach instead of continuing down this path.

Looking at lipgloss v2's behavior: it auto-detects the color profile from stdout, and when running under `script` (which creates a pty), stdout appears as a tty. However, lipgloss might also check the `$TERM` environment variable and downgrade to ASCII if it's unset in the subprocess. The empty file suggests either the Google call failed due to rate limiting or buffering issues with `script` itself.

Rather than getting stuck on the pty and dim cosmetics, the dim functionality is already solid—it's unit-tested at the Render level for the translation lines, the dimFunc gating is straightforward and correct by inspection, and the real question is just whether lipgloss emits color when it's a TTY, which is standard lipgloss behavior.

The concern is that lipgloss v2's global renderer might detect the color profile once at package initialization against the real stdout, potentially caching "no color" if stdout is a pipe at that moment. But in this case, stdout is determined at runtime and lipgloss v2 detects lazily using `colorprofile.Detect` on `os.Stdout`, so if we're on a TTY it should detect truecolor or 256-color support. Since capturing a real TTY in the sandbox is difficult and this is just cosmetic, I could verify by setting `CLICOLOR_FORCE=1` to force lipgloss to emit color regardless of TTY detection, then test with stdout piped to see if lipgloss respects that override.

However, there's a catch: `dimFunc` gates on `stdoutTTY` (checking `term.IsTerminal`), so when piped, `stdoutTTY` is false and `dimFunc` returns the identity function, meaning no color escapes regardless of `CLICOLOR_FORCE`. So I can't test that way without an actual TTY. Instead, I'll trust that lipgloss works correctly since the TUI already uses the same lipgloss styles successfully, and `dimFunc` uses the exact same primitive (`lg.NewStyle().Foreground(lg.Color(...))`) that the rest of the styles use. Let me re-run the PTY test with a fallback and `TERM` set to confirm Google still works, or verify the pipe-clean assertion more rigorously — the key contract is that when piped, no dim escape sequences should leak from the app.

I've already confirmed the raw output had no color codes, which is good. Now I want to double-check `NO_COLOR` handling, make sure Google didn't rate-limit, and verify that `--bilingual` with arguments (non-pipe) still falls through to normal behavior. Let me run a few quick checks to confirm the bilingual mode still produces output before moving on to documentation.
</details></think>

```text
1	# Backlog Research
2	
3	Long-form research, design notes, and paused troubleshooting for items listed in
4	[`../TODO.md`](../TODO.md). One file per topic, named with a slug that matches
5	the TODO entry.
6	
7	## Why this exists
8	
9	`TODO.md` is the **index** — short titles grouped by priority section with
10	effort tags. This folder
11	is the **knowledge base** — the actual investigation, options considered,
12	benchmarks, error messages, and decisions that informed the TODO entry. The goal
13	is **resume-friendliness**: when you (or an agent) come back to an item three
14	months later, the doc here lets you pick up in 5 minutes instead of re-running
15	30 minutes of investigation.
16	
17	This folder is maintainer-facing repo metadata. It lives in the git repo but
18	never reaches users — `go install` / `go build` compile only Go sources, so it
19	has no effect on the built binary.
20	
21	## When to add a doc here
22	
23	Add a `backlog/<slug>.md` file when **any** of these apply:
24	
25	- The TODO item carries a `P?` tag (it requires evaluation; record what was tried)
26	- You did meaningful troubleshooting but didn't ship a fix (capture the trace
27	  before it evaporates)
28	- Multiple options were considered (record the trade-offs, not just the winner)
29	- An external blocker exists (waiting on upstream release, host availability)
30	- The implementation is `[L]` or `[XL]` (architectural; needs design before code)
31	
32	`[S]` items rarely need a doc unless there's surprising context.
33	
34	## When NOT to add a doc here
35	
36	- Item is `[S]` and obvious — just put the file path in the TODO entry
37	- Already covered by an existing doc page (cross-link instead — that's
38	  user-facing reference, this is maintainer-facing speculation)
39	- Speculation only ("would be cool to...") with no investigation — keep it as a
40	  one-liner in `TODO.md` first; promote to a doc when you actually look into it
41	
42	## File template
43	
44	See [`backlog-doc.md.template`] in the skill's `assets/` for the per-doc
45	template, or copy the structure of an existing entry below.
46	
47	## Index
48	
49	Add new entries here as you create them. Keep alphabetical.
50	
51	| Slug | Status | TODO entry |
52	|---|---|---|
53	| `chezmoi-go-tool-integration` | spike done, paused on scope (2026-07) | P? "Wire `translate` into chezmoi…" |
54	| `dict-bundling` | deferred (2026-07) | P? "Bundle or prebuild the dictionary…" |
55	| `release-binaries` | deferred (2026-07) | P? "Ship prebuilt release binaries…" |
56
```
</details></tool-use>

 `./backlog/release-binaries.md`

```text
1	# Ship prebuilt release binaries (goreleaser + GitHub Releases)
2	
3	**Status**: P? — needs a spike
4	**Effort**: L
5	**Related**: `TODO.md` P? · `.goreleaser.yaml` (none yet) · chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl` in the dotfiles repo · [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md)
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	2026-07, surfaced while making translate a "resident binary" in the chezmoi
10	dotfiles. Chosen install path for now is **`go install
11	github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest`** into `~/.local/bin` (GOBIN-pinned).
12	That requires a Go toolchain on every host. The dotfiles fleet is multi-host /
13	multi-arch (this host is `macos_intel`/amd64; others differ). Prebuilt release
14	binaries would let hosts **without** Go install via chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal`
15	(archive-file, templated per OS/arch) — the same mechanism the dotfiles already
16	use for oh-my-zsh etc.
17	
18	## Investigation
19	
20	- Binary is **pure Go, no cgo** (`modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go), so
21	  `GOOS`/`GOARCH` cross-compilation is trivial — a single goreleaser run can emit
22	  darwin/{amd64,arm64} + linux/{amd64,arm64} archives.
23	- Current binary size ~22 MB (dictionary is NOT embedded — see dict-bundling).
24	- chezmoi external pattern (from the dotfiles `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl`):
25	  `type = "archive-file"` with a URL templated on `{{ .chezmoi.os }}` /
26	  `{{ .chezmoi.arch }}`, `refreshPeriod`, extract the single binary to
27	  `~/.local/bin`.
28	
29	## Options considered
30	
31	| Option | Pros | Cons |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| A. `go install @version` (current) | zero release infra; always builds for the host | needs Go toolchain per host; version pin is a git tag |
34	| B. goreleaser + Releases + `.chezmoiexternal` archive | no toolchain on target; multi-arch; can attach dict DB | release pipeline to maintain; must tag + run CI on each version |
35	| C. Homebrew tap | `brew`-native upgrade story | tap repo to maintain; macOS/Linuxbrew only |
36	
37	## Current blocker / open questions
38	
39	- Is `go install` "good enough"? Go is present on all current hosts (mise), so B
40	  is only needed if a Go-less host joins the fleet.
41	- If we do B, should the dictionary DB ship as a release asset (ties this doc to
42	  [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md))?
43	
44	## Decision (if any)
45	
46	`2026-07 deferred` — `go install` covers all current hosts. Revisit B if a
47	Go-less host needs translate, or once versioned releases are wanted for their own
48	sake.
49	
50	## References
51	
52	- goreleaser: https://goreleaser.com/
53	- chezmoi externals: https://www.chezmoi.io/reference/special-files-and-directories/chezmoiexternal-format/
54
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:07:39Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./README.md`

```text
1	# translate
2	
3	A fast CLI/TUI translation tool for the terminal — one-shot from the shell or an
4	interactive live-translating TUI. Backed by an auto fallback chain over your
5	local LLM providers (copilot-proxy → Ollama), a free web API (Google), and a
6	dictionary, with typo-tolerant language resolution and translation history.
7	
8	## Install
9	
10	```sh
11	go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest   # into $GOBIN (or ~/go/bin)
12	```
13	
14	## Build & run
15	
16	```sh
17	go build -o translate .      # Go 1.25+ (developed on 1.26)
18	./translate "hola mundo" --to en          # one-shot
19	echo "bonjour" | ./translate --to en      # pipe
20	./translate                               # interactive TUI
21	```
22	
23	First run writes a default config to `~/.config/translate/config.toml`; run
24	`./translate init` for a guided setup that probes which providers are up.
25	
26	## Usage
27	
28	| Command | What it does |
29	|---|---|
30	| `translate <text>` / `… \| translate` | One-shot translation (streams to a TTY; plain text when piped) |
31	| `translate` (no args, TTY) | Interactive TUI |
32	| `translate --to <lang> --from <lang>` | Language override; both are fuzzy (`chinees` → `zh`) |
33	| `translate --json` | Emit the full structured result |
34	| `translate define <word>` | Dictionary lookup (bilingual: zh↔en local, or English API) |
35	| `translate history` / `history search <q>` | Recent history / fuzzy search (`--tsv`, `--json`) |
36	| `translate init` | Interactive config wizard (probes providers) |
37	| `translate config path\|show` · `lang resolve <q>` | Introspection helpers |
38	
39	Flags: `--engine smartauto|auto|<provider>|google`, `--provider`, `--model`, `--tier default|fast|max`, `--preset concise|contextual|dictionary`, `--instructions`, `--pair`/`--pair-with`, `--no-history`, `--debug`.
40	Env overrides: `TRANSLATE_TARGET`, `TRANSLATE_SOURCE`, `TRANSLATE_ENGINE`, `TRANSLATE_PROVIDER`, `TRANSLATE_MODEL`, `TRANSLATE_CONFIG`, `TRANSLATE_DEBUG`.
41	Precedence: **flag > env > `[cli]`/`[tui]` > `[general]` > default**.
42	
43	### Per-front-end defaults (`[cli]` / `[tui]`)
44	
45	The CLI (arguments or piped stdin) and the interactive TUI share `[general]`, but
46	either can override any subset of it via an optional `[cli]` or `[tui]` table — e.g.
47	a snappy concise CLI and a richer, higher-tier TUI:
48	
49	```toml
50	[general]
51	preset = "contextual"
52	tier   = "fast"
53	
54	[cli]                 # one-shot / piped translation
55	preset = "concise"
56	
57	[tui]                 # interactive front-end
58	tier          = "max"
59	live_translate = true
60	```
61	
62	Only keys you list are overridden; the rest inherit `[general]`. Flags and env vars
63	still win over both.
64	
65	## TUI keys
66	
67	`↵` translate · live-debounce auto-translates ~700ms after you stop typing (off by default) ·
68	`^y` copy · `^l` toggle live · `^e` cycle engine · `^t` target language · `^p` model ·
69	`^o` style · `^g` toggle pair mode · `^u` clear · `⇥` switch input/output focus · `^r` history · `⌥↵` newline · `^c`/`esc` quit.
70	
71	`⇥` (Tab) moves keyboard focus between the input box and the result box; the focused
72	box gets an accent border. With the result focused, `↑/↓ j/k PgUp/PgDn g/G space` scroll
73	it; typing any character snaps focus back to the input. A mouse click focuses the box
74	it lands in.
75	
76	## Engines & fallback
77	
78	The `auto` engine tries `chain.order` in turn, failing over **before the first
79	token** (so a down provider switches cleanly; a mid-stream error surfaces rather
80	than restarting):
81	
82	- **copilot-proxy** — OpenAI-compatible at `http://localhost:4141/v1`, no API key.
83	  Default tier is **fast** (`claude-haiku-4-5`); `--tier default` → `claude-sonnet-5`,
84	  `--tier max` → `claude-opus-4-8`. Claude models are served only via the proxy's
85	  Anthropic Messages endpoint (`/v1/messages`) — the engine routes `claude-*` ids
86	  there automatically (they 400 on `/chat/completions`).
87	- **Ollama** — `http://localhost:11434/v1` (offline; `llama3.2:3b`).
88	- **Google** — free, keyless; also reports the detected source language.
89	- **openrouter** — configured but off the default chain; needs `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`.
90	
91	> ⚠️ Using a GitHub Copilot subscription to back non-GitHub tools (copilot-proxy)
92	> may violate Copilot's Terms of Service. Reorder `chain.order` (drop `copilot`,
93	> lead with `ollama`/`google`) in the config if that matters to you.
94	
95	Config lives at `~/.config/translate/config.toml`, history at
96	`~/.local/share/translate/history.jsonl`, last-pair state at
97	`~/.local/state/translate/state.json` (XDG-honored on macOS and Linux).
98	
99	### Smart auto (`smartauto`)
100	
101	The recommended default (choose it in `translate init`). It routes by input shape:
102	a **single word/term** is looked up in the dictionary (with the same LLM fallback as
103	`smart-dict` on a miss), while a **phrase or sentence** is translated by the LLM. Set
104	`engine = "smartauto"` in `[general]` (or `--engine smartauto`). It composes with
105	pair mode, so a single word and a full sentence both translate in the right direction.
106	
107	### Pair mode (bidirectional)
108	
109	With `pair = true` + `pair_with`, the tool detects which of the two languages your
110	input is in and translates it into the **other** — one box, both directions
111	(`你好 → hi`, `test → 測試`). For a CJK/non-CJK pair (e.g. `zh-TW`⇄`en`) direction is
112	decided by script, so even short input routes correctly; the LLM translate prompt is
113	also told to detect-and-route and to **never echo** the input. `pair_with` must differ
114	from the target (`translate init` enforces this; a degenerate config warns).
115	
116	### Debugging
117	
118	`--debug` (or `TRANSLATE_DEBUG=1`, or `[general] debug = true`) logs the intermediate
119	decisions — resolved settings, pair routing, word-vs-phrase classification, dictionary
120	hit/miss, and chain fallback. The one-shot CLI logs to **stderr**; the TUI logs to
121	`~/.local/state/translate/debug.log` (its alt-screen hides stderr).
122	
123	## Dictionary mode
124	
125	`^e` cycles to the dictionary engine (or use `translate define <word>`). It is an
126	**offline bilingual** dictionary that routes by script — Chinese → CC-CEDICT
127	(zh→en), English → ECDICT (en→zh):
128	
129	```sh
130	translate dict update all      # one-time ~67 MB download/build into ~/.local/share/translate/dict
131	```
132	
133	Until then, English lookups fall back to dictionaryapi.dev (`[dict] api_fallback`),
134	and Chinese lookups prompt you to run the update. Misses show a ranked "did you
135	mean" list. Set `[dict] source = "api"` to use only dictionaryapi.dev.
136	
137	### Smart dictionary (`smart-dict`)
138	
139	A distinct engine that keeps the offline dictionary fast and deterministic, but on
140	a miss — no entry, or a fuzzy match too far off — falls back to the LLM for a gloss
141	plus example sentences (never silently: the result carries a `⚠ … defined via <provider> (LLM)`
142	warning). Close typos (edit distance ≤ `[smartdict] close_distance`, default 1) still
143	show "did you mean" without calling the LLM.
144	
145	```sh
146	translate define serendipity        # exact ECDICT entry
147	translate define zzzznotaword       # miss → LLM definition (⚠ warning on stderr)
148	translate define helllo             # distance-1 typo → "did you mean: hello, …"
149	translate define --plain <word>     # force the offline dictionary, no LLM fallback
150	```
151	
152	`translate define` uses smart-dict whenever an LLM provider is reachable
153	(`[smartdict] define_default`, `--smart`/`--plain` to force); in the TUI, `^e`
154	cycles to a `smart-dict` engine alongside the plain `dictionary`.
155	
156	```toml
157	[smartdict]
158	close_distance = 1            # en edit-distance ≤ this stays "did you mean"; beyond → LLM
159	preset         = "dictionary" # LLM output style for the fallback
160	define_default = true         # `translate define` prefers smart-dict when a provider is up
161	```
162	
163	Data: **CC-CEDICT** (CC BY-SA 4.0, © Paul Andrew Denisowski / MDBG) and
164	**ECDICT** (MIT, © skywind3000).
165	
166	## Developer note — Charm v2 (`charm.land/*`)
167	
168	This project pins the **v2** Charm stack on the `charm.land/*` vanity module paths
169	(`charm.land/bubbletea/v2`, `bubbles/v2`, `lipgloss/v2`, `huh/v2`) — **not**
170	`github.com/charmbracelet/*`. v1 code will not compile here. When editing the TUI:
171	`View()` returns `tea.View` (wrap strings with `tea.NewView`); keys arrive as
172	`tea.KeyPressMsg`; alt-screen is the declarative `View.AltScreen` field; window
173	size is requested via `tea.RequestWindowSize`.
174	
175	## Architecture
176	
177	The `internal/engine` and `internal/store` layers are pure Go (no TUI imports);
178	the one-shot CLI and the Bubble Tea TUI drive the identical engine values and
179	diverge only at presentation. A single monotonic `seq` counter in the TUI drives
180	debounce-collapse, in-flight cancellation, and stale-result dropping.
181	
182	<!-- project-knowledge-harness:readme-roadmap -->
183	<!-- Snippet for project's README.md, placed near other meta sections like
184	     "Customization" or "Contributing". -->
185	
186	## Roadmap & lessons learned
187	
188	Forward-looking work — long-term ideas, deferred items, things needing
189	evaluation — lives in [`TODO.md`](TODO.md), prioritised P1 → P3 with effort
190	estimates (S/M/L/XL). Items with accompanying research, design notes, or paused
191	troubleshooting link to a corresponding [`backlog/<slug>.md`](backlog/) doc.
192	
193	Backward-looking knowledge — past traps and non-obvious debugging — lives in
194	[`pitfalls/`](pitfalls/), titled by symptom so future-you can grep the error
195	message and land on the root cause + workaround instead of re-debugging from
196	scratch.
197	<!-- project-knowledge-harness:readme-roadmap --> (end)
198
```

```md
# Bilingual / "immersive" pipe mode + ANSI-strip-on-input

**Status**: shipped (2026-07-20)
**Effort**: M
**Related**: `TODO.md` Done · `cmd/root.go` (`runBilingual`, `translateOnce`, `dimFunc`, pipe branch) · `internal/bitext/` · `.claude/plans/plan-c-strip-enchanted-hummingbird.md`

## Context

2026-07, surfaced from piping colored docs through the tool:
`tldr rg | translate --to zh-TW` produced a clean but **colorless** blob. Two
problems hid behind that:

1. The piped ANSI escape codes reached the LLM verbatim (`io.ReadAll` →
   `TrimSpace` → `Request.Text`; `TrimSpace` only trims edges), wasting tokens and
   risking mis-segmentation.
2. There was no way to see the translation **alongside** the original — the ask,
   inspired by [Immersive Translate](https://immersivetranslate.com/en/), whose
   philosophy is "translate in place, keep the original, preserve the element's
   own style".

## What shipped

- **Strip fix (patch):** `bitext.Strip` (wraps `ansi.Strip`) is applied to piped
  stdin before it becomes `Request.Text`. Cleaner/cheaper prompt for every piped
  run; output unchanged.
- **`--bilingual` / `-2` (feat):** opt-in, stdin-only. Splits input into
  blank-line-delimited blocks, echoes each block verbatim (ANSI/color intact), and
  prints a translation beneath each **prose** block (prefixed `  ↳ `, dimmed grey
  on a TTY). Indented **command/code** blocks are echoed untranslated. Prose blocks
  translate concurrently (bounded to 5), forced concise so each block maps to
  exactly one translation. Echoed translations (proper nouns the model returns
  unchanged) are suppressed. `--json`/`--learn` take precedence; with positional
  args it falls through to the normal one-shot.

## Key finding — what "the Immersive Translate philosophy" actually is

Reverse-engineered docs (DeepWiki) + the injected DOM classes show Immersive
Translate preserves **block/element-level** style and renders the translation in a
**single uniform theme** (grey/underline/dimmed). It inserts the translation into
the *same* block element, so a heading stays heading-sized via **CSS inheritance** —
it does **not** re-bold or re-color the specific words that were styled in the
source. Source inline styling survives only in the retained original copy.

Conclusion: "preserve per-word color in the translation" is **not** part of the
referenced philosophy. The faithful terminal analog is: keep the original block
(with its color) + one uniformly-dimmed translation beneath. That is exactly what
shipped.

## Options considered

| Option | What | Verdict |
|---|---|---|
| **A. Dominant-color per line** | detect each line's most-common SGR, recolor the translation in it | **Rejected.** tldr/`--help` color is *per-word* (command vs placeholder); "most frequent SGR" is a semantically meaningless color. |
| **B. Per-span color transfer** | parse ANSI → styled runs → placeholder tokens → LLM translates keeping markers → re-substitute | **Rejected — over-engineering.** Same problem class as XLIFF inline codes; unreliable across word reordering; defeats streaming. |
| **C. Block-interleaved, uniform dim** (shipped) | keep original block verbatim, translation beneath in one dim style; skip code blocks | **Chosen.** Matches the real Immersive Translate philosophy, robust, no alignment problem. |

### Why B is a trap (prior art)

Preserving inline markup across a translation that **reorders words** is a
well-studied hard problem, and the localization industry does **not** trust the
engine to reposition the tags:

- **XLIFF 2.0** adds `canReorder`/`canCopy`/`canDelete` on inline codes precisely
  because reorder/duplication/deletion are the recognized hazards. The split
  `<bx/>`/`<ex/>` placeholder design is itself a source of unpairable output.
- **DeepL** `tag_handling` **duplicates** tags by design when word order changes;
  **Google** `format=HTML` returns tags "to the extent possible" (documented drops,
  e.g. vanishing `notranslate` spans).
- **MediaWiki Content Translation** *abandoned* delegating tag placement to MT:
  they strip markup, translate plain text, then reattach via subsentence alignment
  + n-gram fuzzy matching — and still hit inflection/reorder/split failures.
- CAT tools (OmegaT, memoQ) rely on **detection** (tag-count validation that blocks
  export), not prevention.

A single ANSI-colored source word can become many target words or vanish via
inflection — there is no reliable "which target span wears the color". And the
reattachment step needs the **full** output buffered, defeating the token-by-token
streaming hot path (`internal/engine/prompt.go` asks for "ONLY the translated
text"; `engine.go` streams `ChunkToken`). Effort ≈ a mini-XLIFF engine for a
cosmetic terminal nicety.

## Implementation notes

- **`internal/bitext`** (pure, tested): `Strip`, `Split` (blank-line blocks +
  Prose/Code/Blank classification by ≥2-column indentation), `Render` (interleave;
  `dim` supplied as a closure so the package stays presentation-free and testable).
- **`github.com/charmbracelet/x/ansi`** (was already an indirect dep, now direct)
  provides `ansi.Strip` and a CJK/wcwidth-aware tokenizer — no new third-party dep.
  Its API also offers `DecodeSequence`/`StringWidth`/`Truncate` if a future
  refinement needs per-span parsing.
- **`cmd/root.go`**: `--bilingual` is a standalone flag (Pattern B — read directly,
  no config/resolve wiring), dispatched only on the stdin pipe branch;
  `runBilingual` fans out `translateOnce` over prose blocks with a
  `chan struct{}`/`WaitGroup` semaphore; `dimFunc` styles only when
  `stdoutTTY && NO_COLOR unset`.

## Known limitations (not fixed here)

- **Tables/columns** (`ls -l`, `kubectl get`, `git status`): interleaving a
  translation beneath each row breaks alignment. Bilingual targets prose docs
  (tldr, man, `--help`), not tabular output.
- **Fully-indented man pages** may be misclassified as all-`Code` and skipped.
- **Hard-wrapped prose** split by blank lines loses cross-block context.
- Pair/learn routing and `--speak`/history are intentionally out of scope for this
  reading view.

→ tracked as a `P3 [M]` follow-up in `TODO.md` (table/wrapped-prose-aware bilingual).

## References

- Immersive Translate (DeepWiki, reverse-engineered): content-script DOM insertion
  + theme styling system.
- XLIFF 1.2 / 2.0 inline codes (OASIS); DeepL `tag_handling` v2; Google Cloud
  Translation `format=HTML`; MediaWiki Content Translation markup handling.
- `charmbracelet/x/ansi` v0.11.7 (`ansi.Strip`, `DecodeSequence`, `StringWidth`).

```

## Done — `C + Strip` implemented, verified, documented

**Code**
- **Strip fix (`cmd/root.go` pipe branch):** piped stdin is now `bitext.Strip`ed before it becomes the LLM prompt — colored input (`tldr`, `grep --color=always`) no longer pollutes the request. Verified: `hola mundo` (colored) → clean `hello world`.
- **`internal/bitext`** (new, pure, 95.9% covered): `Strip` (wraps `ansi.Strip`), `Split` (blank-line blocks classified Prose/Code/Blank by ≥2-col indent), `Render` (interleave original + `↳` translation via a caller-supplied `dim` closure).
- **`--bilingual` / `-2` (`cmd/root.go`):** standalone flag, stdin-only. `runBilingual` fans out `translateOnce` over prose blocks (bounded to 5, concise-forced), suppresses echoes, and `dimFunc` greys the translation only on a TTY with `NO_COLOR` unset. Code blocks echoed untranslated; `--json`/`--learn` take precedence; args fall through to normal one-shot.

**Verified end-to-end** (Google engine, no key): bilingual on tldr-shaped colored input keeps originals + `↳` translations, skips the `rg pattern` code line, suppresses the `rg` echo; `--json` precedence; `NO_COLOR` stays plain; args fall-through. `just check` (fmt+vet+build) and `just test` both green; `x/ansi` promoted to a direct dep.

**Docs**
- `backlog/bilingual-immersive-mode.md` — the design + **competitor analysis** you asked for: what Immersive Translate actually does (block-level style + uniform dim, *not* per-word color), and why approaches A (dominant-color) and B (per-span placeholder tokens) were rejected, grounded in XLIFF/DeepL/MediaWiki inline-tag failure modes.
- `README.md` — new **Bilingual mode** section + usage row + flags line + the strip note.

Before committing the specstory transcripts though, I should scan them for any secrets to be safe. I'll make a single clean commit with the feature code, docs, plan, and today's transcript, then separately flag the two older unrelated transcript files in case the user wants to commit those too. are tracked in git. I should run a few checks first — looking at git status, verifying what's in `.claude/plans/` and `.specstory`, checking the go.sum situation, and scanning the specstory file for any secrets. Then I can commit.

On the second part, the user is asking in Chinese whether chezmoi will automatically pull the latest version when we push a new one.

Looking at how `translate` is set up in their dotfiles — it's installed via a `go_tools` ansible role that runs `go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest` into `~/.local/bin`, managed through mise. The AGENTS.md release process shows that running `just upgrade-go` installs each go tool at `@latest`, and the version pin in the ansible defaults is just a floor for fresh installs, not something that blocks upgrades when you run `chezmoi apply`.

But here's the critical part: pushing a new version doesn't automatically update the installed binary. The version is determined by git tags, so `go install @latest` only resolves to the latest published tag, not commits on `main`. You have to explicitly tag and push the tag for it to be discoverable. And even after tagging, `chezmoi apply` and ansible are install-only — they won't upgrade a binary that's already there, only install it fresh.

To actually get the new version onto a machine, you'd run `just upgrade-go`, which does `go install @latest` and pulls the new tag into ~/.local/bin. The `go_tools` pin in defaults/main.yml is just the baseline for fresh installs, not something you bump for upgrades. There's also a potential pitfall where an older copy of the tool earlier on PATH (like in ~/.dotfiles/bin) could shadow the one in ~/.local/bin.

I'm realizing this is actually about the dotfiles repo's release process, not chezmoi's own behavior, so I should check what the `chezmoi-dotfiles` skill covers. I need to handle the commit first, then use the chezmoi-dotfiles skill to verify the actual dotfiles configuration for the auto-follow behavior. Let me start by checking the git status and tracking information to determine the right commit scope.
</details></think>

583:411	// Tokens stream live to stdout only when stdout is a TTY (so `translate x | pbcopy`
639:467			fmt.Println() // newline after the streamed tokens
732:There's a spectrum of difficulty here. Preserving line-level styles (like keeping a header line uniformly cyan) is manageable — you translate and re-wrap in the same style. But word-level preservation (keeping individual colored words like `grep` or `pattern` red within a sentence) requires cross-lingual token alignment, which is genuinely hard and where the over-engineering risk becomes real.
738:Approach B goes deeper — parsing ANSI into individual text and style runs, replacing styled runs with placeholder tokens, sending the translation request with those markers intact so the LLM repositions them, then substituting them back.  It's more precise but fragile; the LLM might drop or duplicate markers, and word order differences between languages make alignment ambiguous. It's essentially what document translators do with inline tags, and that's notoriously error-prone.
776:19	// for ONLY the translated text so the reply streams token-by-token identically
999:36	- [ ] **[M] Azure Translator engine behind the engine seam** — the only supported Microsoft path (needs an Azure subscription key). Keyless Bing scraping (`ttranslatev3` + transient `IG`/`IID`/token) is too fragile/ToS-risky for v1. Leave the engine interface ready; add when a key is available.
1198:The code reads all of stdin, trims whitespace, and sends the entire blob—ANSI codes and all—directly to the LLM as plain text. The LLM strips or mangles the escape sequences and returns plain translated text, which gets printed without any color restoration. Even though streaming is normally disabled when stdout isn't a TTY, in this case stdout IS a TTY (they're seeing it in the terminal), so it streams plain tokens either way.
1237:(b) Can it TOKENIZE/parse a string into a sequence of (SGR-style, text) runs so I can map each visible text span to the style active over it? git commit -F - <<'EOF'
feat(cli): bilingual pipe mode (--bilingual/-2) + strip ANSI on stdin

Piped colored input (e.g. `tldr rg | translate`) previously reached the LLM
with its ANSI escapes intact and came back as one plain, colorless blob. Two
changes:

- fix: strip ANSI/SGR from piped stdin (bitext.Strip) before it becomes the
  prompt — cleaner, cheaper input for every piped run; output unchanged.
- feat: opt-in `--bilingual` / `-2`, stdin-only. Split input into blank-line
  blocks, echo each verbatim (color intact), print a dim translation beneath
  each prose block; indented command/code blocks are echoed untranslated.
  Prose blocks translate concurrently (bounded), forced concise so each maps
  to one translation; echoed proper nouns are suppressed. `--json`/`--learn`
  take precedence; with args it falls through to the normal one-shot.

New pure, tested `internal/bitext` pkg (Split/Strip/Render). Per-word color
transfer was evaluated and rejected as over-engineering (XLIFF/DeepL/MediaWiki
inline-tag reordering is unreliable and defeats streaming) — see
backlog/bilingual-immersive-mode.md. `charmbracelet/x/ansi` promoted to a
direct dep (already in the graph; no new third-party dep).

New flag → minor version bump per AGENTS.md.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
echo "=== result ==="; git log --oneline -1; git status --short
```

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I've committed the changes to main with hash `d64a45f`, though the SpecStory transcript file is showing as modified again since it keeps appending content as the session continues—that's expected and will be captured in a future commit. There are still those two orphaned files from earlier dates that remain unresolved, and the push hasn't happened yet since you didn't ask for it.

Now I'm looking into the chezmoi behavior to answer your question about whether pushing a new version automatically updates everything. The key points from the documentation: pushing to main alone doesn't trigger updates since `go install @latest` resolves to the latest tag, not the main branch—you need to tag and push the tag. Even after tagging, `chezmoi apply` and ansible only install missing binaries, they won't upgrade existing ones. To upgrade an already-installed binary, you'd need to run `just upgrade-go` which explicitly calls `go install @latest`. The go_tools pin in the defaults configuration is what controls the fresh install version.

So the answer is no—it doesn't automatically follow to the latest. You need both a tag (not just a main push) and an explicit upgrade command for existing installations. Let me verify this against the actual dotfiles configuration.
</details></think>

<!-- managed-by: chezmoi-dotfiles (chezmoi source: dot_agents/skills/chezmoi-dotfiles/SKILL.md.tmpl) — do not hand-edit the deployed copy; it re-renders on every `chezmoi apply`. -->

# chezmoi-dotfiles

This machine's `$HOME` is managed by a **chezmoi** (configs) + **ansible** (system
deps) dotfiles repo. This skill is the agent-facing map: how to drive it, and what
custom tooling exists that you wouldn't otherwise know about. It is rendered per-host
— the "What's enabled on THIS machine" section reflects what the user actually
selected at init.

## Orientation (read this first)

- **Repo root**: `chezmoi source-path` (no args) prints the source dir. Work there
  to edit: `cd "$(chezmoi source-path)"`.
- **Source path of one file**: `chezmoi source-path ~/.config/foo/bar` → the
  managed source file for that target.
- **`docs/**` is NOT deployed to `$HOME`** (it's chezmoi-ignored). Read detail via
  `"$(chezmoi source-path)/docs/..."`. A *subset* is deployed to `~/.config/docs/`
  (e.g. keybindings, cli-tools) for runtime helpers.
- **Read the contract before editing**: `"$(chezmoi source-path)/CLAUDE.md"` holds
  Hard invariants and cross-file maintenance rules. Honor them.
- This host: profile `macos_intel`, OS `darwin`/`amd64`, primary shell `zsh`.

## chezmoi essentials

```bash
chezmoi diff                 # preview changes (also: just chezmoi-diff)
chezmoi apply                # deploy + run scripts (also: cas, or just chezmoi-apply)
chezmoi status               # modified/added/removed targets
chezmoi edit ~/.zshrc        # edit the SOURCE of a target
chezmoi add  ~/.config/x     # start managing an existing file
chezmoi re-add               # pull live edits back into source
chezmoi managed | grep foo   # is X managed?
chezmoi cat ~/.config/x      # render a target without applying
chezmoi data | jq            # the template data (ground truth for the toggles below)
```

- **`cas`** = `chezmoi apply` + reload hint; **`cau`** = update from remote then apply
  (defined in `dot_config/shell/99_chezmoi_reload.sh`).
- **`dotcfg`** = reconfigure the init prompts on an already-set-up machine
  (`dotcfg` for the TUI, `dotcfg --set installLlmTools=true --yes` for one toggle,
  `dotcfg --dry-run` to preview). Shell twin: `czcfg`.
- **Never `chezmoi add` a `modify_`/`create_` target** — it overwrites the script.
- Full command cheat-sheet: `docs/this_repo/cheatsheet.md`. Install-vs-upgrade is
  split: `chezmoi apply`/ansible is **install-only**; upgrades go through
  `just upgrade-*`.

## What's enabled on THIS machine

(Rendered from this host's selections. For live truth: `chezmoi data | jq`.)
- **Coding agents** — Claude Code, OpenCode, Cursor, Codex, Gemini, Copilot (configs + CLIs). See `docs/tools/agent-overlays.md`, `docs/tools/agent-skills.md`.
- **Python uv tools** — mlflow, marimo, jupyter, etc. (powers the `mlf` CLI). See `docs/this_repo/uv-bootstrap.md`.
- **Extra runtimes** — rust, bun, ruby via mise (+ cargo/gem tools). Node is always present.
- **LLM tools** — Ollama / LiteLLM / local models.
- **JS CLI tools** — standalone npm CLIs (e.g. readability-cli).
- **.NET tools** — .NET SDK (mise) + dotnet global tools.
- **IaC** — Azure CLI, Terraform, OpenTofu.
- **Media** — ffmpeg, ImageMagick, exiftool, libvips.
- **Media control** — nowplaying-cli + switchaudio-osx (macOS) / playerctl (Linux); unlocks full `sysplay`/`sysnow` (built-in `sysvol`/`sysmute` work regardless).
- **Networking** — nmap, mtr, httpie, gping, trippy.
- **Tunnels** — ngrok, cloudflared.
- **Vim mode** — shell modal editing + tmux vim nav (NOT Neovim/editors — those are always vim).

## Custom in-house CLIs (always deployed to `~/.dotfiles/bin`, on PATH)

Each works from any cwd. Run with `--help`, or read `docs/tools/<name>.md`. Full list: `ls "$(chezmoi source-path)/dot_dotfiles/bin/"`.

- **`fleet`** — cross-host ops over your machines: `fleet chezmoi apply`, `fleet hosts`, `fleet tmux`, `fleet info`, `fleet pueue`, `fleet exec`. See `docs/this_repo/fleet-apply.md`, `docs/tools/fleet-exec.md`, `docs/tools/fleet-hosts.md`.
- **`mlf`** — MLflow umbrella CLI + TUI dashboard (needs Python uv tools). Picker twin: `tv mlflow`.
- **`pqsum`** — pueue queue summary (text / AI / JSON). See `docs/tools/pueue.md`.
- **`x`** — clipboard/file copy-paste-open with cross-platform fallbacks.
- **`view-office`** — open/preview Office docs (.docx/.xlsx/.pptx + legacy .doc/.xls/.ppt) in the terminal; also powers the yazi Office previewer. See `docs/tools/office-viewers.md`.
- **`mi-router`** / **`reyee`** / **`sms`** — read-only router CLIs (Xiaomi MiWiFi / Ruijie Reyee / Huawei HiLink SMS). See `docs/tools/{mi-router,reyee,sms}.md`.
- **`mi-dhcp-bind`** — opt-in **write** companion to `mi-router`: set/remove MiWiFi static DHCP reservations (`bind --self` autodetects this host). See `docs/tools/mi-dhcp-bind.md`.
- **`ping-monitor`** — record ping over time, flag latency spikes. See `docs/tools/ping-monitor.md`.
- **`wake`** — Wake-on-LAN sender: `wake <host>` powers a box on by magic packet (hosts in `~/.config/wake/hosts.toml`). Passive side = `installWakeOnLan` ansible role. See `docs/sysadmin/wake-on-lan.md`.
- **`yth`** — search your YouTube watch history (title/channel/description + captions; Takeout import + optional cookie sync). Picker twin: `tv yth`. See `docs/tools/yth.md`.
- **`dotcfg`** — reconfigure dotfiles prompts (above).
- **`sesh-preview`** — preview helper for the sesh picker.

## Television (`tv`) — fuzzy pickers with custom channels

There are ~40 **custom** channels (system state, fleet, services, infra, agents…)
that you won't discover otherwise. Browse them:

```bash
tv                      # default picker
tv list-channels        # list everything available
tv <channel>            # open one, e.g. tv fleet-hosts | tv mlflow | tv pueue
ls "$(chezmoi source-path)/dot_config/television/cable/"   # the channel definitions
```

Handy ones: `fleet-hosts`, `mlflow`, `pueue`, `ports`, `kill-process`, `services`,
`sessions`, `keybindings`, `aliases`, `git-ops`, `mac-apps`/`linux-apps`. Full
reference: `docs/tools/tv.md`.

## Keybindings & keymaps

- **`bindings`** (shell function) or **`tv keybindings`** — interactive reference.
- **tmux**: `prefix + Space` (or `prefix + e`) opens the action menu;
  `prefix + T` = sesh picker, `prefix + U` = tools. Deep ref: `docs/tools/tmux/keybindings.md`.
- **shell ZLE widgets**: `Alt+S` sesh sessions, `Alt+T` tools, `Ctrl+R` history (atuin).
- Master reference: `docs/shells/keybindings.md` (also deployed to `~/.config/docs/shells/keybindings.md`).

## Aliases & functions

`alias` to list shell aliases, or `tv aliases`. Catalog: `docs/shells/aliases.md`.
Helpers worth knowing: `chezmoi-cd` (jump to source), `run-for` (time-box a command),
`bindings` (above). App control: `applaunch`/`appquit`/`apprestart`/… (cross-platform).
System audio (cross-platform, built-in): `sysvol`/`sysmute` (+ `sysplay`/`sysnow` with `installMediaControl`); remote mute via `fleet exec --login -- sysmute on`.

## just recipes (run from the source dir)

```bash
cd "$(chezmoi source-path)" && just --list
```

Categories: `chezmoi-*`, `ansible-*`, `upgrade-*` (brew/mise/uv/npm/cargo/…),
`fleet`/`fleet-*`, `gen-prompts` (regenerate prompt scaffolding), `check`/`lint`,
`docker-*`. See `docs/this_repo/cheatsheet.md`.

## Editing rules (don't skip)

Before changing anything under the repo, read `CLAUDE.md` Hard invariants and the
"Cross-file maintenance rules" table — many surfaces have mandatory mirror updates
in the same commit (docs, completions, tool-managers index, this very skill, …).

# Go CLI tools installed via `go install`.
#
# The Go toolchain comes from mise and is gated on installExtraRuntimes
# (see dot_config/mise/config.toml.tmpl — `go = "latest"`). Binaries are
# installed into ~/.local/bin (via GOBIN), the blessed auto-install dir that
# is already on PATH (dot_config/shell/00_exports.sh.tmpl), matching where
# cargo/uv/npm/dotnet tools land.
#
# Add tools here. Pin the version so a fresh box installs a known-good build;
# `binary` is the resulting executable name, used for the `creates:`
# idempotency guard. Version bumps are handled by the upgrade path
# (`just upgrade-go` → scripts/upgrade_tools.sh cat_go), NOT by editing the pin
# — same install-vs-upgrade split as cargo. Example:
#   - name: github.com/owner/tool@v1.2.3
#     binary: tool

go_tools:
  - name: github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@v0.1.0
    binary: translate
