```text
=== go_tools tasks (idempotency guard = install-only?) ===
---
# Go CLI tools installation via `go install`.
# The Go toolchain is installed via mise (gated on installExtraRuntimes; see
# dot_config/mise/config.toml.tmpl). This role no-ops cleanly when Go is absent.

# =============================================================================
# Resolve the mise-managed Go bin dir (no-op the whole role if Go is absent)
# =============================================================================
# `mise which go` prints the absolute path to the managed go binary
# (~/.local/share/mise/installs/go/<ver>/bin/go); its dirname is the bin dir we
# prepend to PATH so `go install` runs. Empty/failed → Go isn't installed
# (installExtraRuntimes=false, or mise missing) and the install task below is
# skipped. Mirrors the herdr-plus build idiom in dot_ansible/roles/devtools.
- name: Resolve mise Go bin dir
  ansible.builtin.shell: |
    set -o pipefail
    gobin="$(mise which go 2>/dev/null)" || exit 0
    [ -n "$gobin" ] && dirname "$gobin"
  args:
    executable: /bin/bash
  environment:
    PATH: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin:/opt/homebrew/bin:/usr/local/bin:{{ ansible_facts['env']['PATH'] }}"
  register: go_bindir
  changed_when: false
  failed_when: false

# =============================================================================
# Install Go CLI tools (all platforms)
# =============================================================================
# GOBIN forces binaries into ~/.local/bin (the blessed dir already on PATH),
# not ~/go/bin (GOPATH/bin) or the version-pinned mise toolchain dir. GOPATH is
# pinned to ~/.local/share/go (XDG data) so the build-time module cache lands
# there instead of recreating ~/go — matches the interactive shell
# (dot_config/shell/02_legacy_tools.sh) and scripts/upgrade_tools.sh cat_go. The
# `creates:` guard makes this install-only + idempotent — version bumps go
# through `just upgrade-go` (scripts/upgrade_tools.sh cat_go), never a re-apply.
- name: Install Go CLI tools via go install
  when: (go_bindir.stdout | default('') | trim) != ""
  ansible.builtin.command: "go install {{ item.name }}"
  args:
    creates: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin/{{ item.binary }}"
  loop: "{{ go_tools }}"
  loop_control:
    label: "{{ item.name }}"
  environment:
    GOBIN: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin"
    GOPATH: "{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/share/go"
    PATH: "{{ (go_bindir.stdout | trim) }}:{{ ansible_facts['env']['HOME'] }}/.local/bin:{{ ansible_facts['env']['PATH'] }}"

=== upgrade path: how cat_go / upgrade-go resolves versions ===
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:262:  # install script or `go install`. If it was installed via a package manager
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:457:# Category: cargo — `cargo install-update -a` (bootstraps cargo-update if needed)
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:477:    info "Bootstrapping cargo-update (needed for 'cargo install-update -a')"

/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:491:    _run cargo install-update -a || any_fail=1
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:500:# Category: go — `go install <pkg>@latest` for each tool in

/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:539:  # toolchain dir. Upgrade = re-install at @latest regardless of the version
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:550:    # Strip any @version pin (module paths never contain '@') and go to @latest.
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:552:    info "Upgrading go tool: $pkg@latest"
/Users/david/.local/share/chezmoi/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh:553:    _run "$go_cmd" install "$pkg@latest" || any_fail=1

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:26:59Z)**_

Let me read the exact `cat_go` upgrade logic and locate the `just upgrade-go` recipe:

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:27:00Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read cat_go and find upgrade-go recipe

```bash
SRC="$(chezmoi source-path)"
echo "=== cat_go() lines 500-555 ==="
sed -n '500,555p' "$SRC/scripts/upgrade_tools.sh"
echo "=== just recipe file(s) ==="
ls "$SRC"/*ustfile* "$SRC"/justfile* 2>/dev/null
grep -rn "upgrade-go\|upgrade_tools.sh go\|cat_go" "$SRC/Justfile" 2>/dev/null | head
```

```text
=== cat_go() lines 500-555 ===
# Category: go — `go install <pkg>@latest` for each tool in
# dot_ansible/roles/go_tools/defaults/main.yml
# ============================================================================
cat_go() {
  # Resolve go via mise shim first, then system PATH.
  local go_cmd=""
  if [[ -x "$HOME/.local/share/mise/shims/go" ]]; then
    go_cmd="$HOME/.local/share/mise/shims/go"
  elif command -v go >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    go_cmd="go"
  else
    warn "go not found (Go not installed via mise?) — skipping"
    return $SKIP_RC
  fi

  # Parse the tools list from defaults YAML — lines `  - name: <pkg>@<ver>`.
  # Same hand-rolled awk parser as cat_dotnet.
  local defaults_file="$_REPO_ROOT/dot_ansible/roles/go_tools/defaults/main.yml"
  local tools=()
  if [[ -f "$defaults_file" ]]; then
    while IFS= read -r line; do
      tools+=("$line")
    done < <(awk '
            /^go_tools:/ { in_list=1; next }
            in_list && /^[^ ]/ { in_list=0 }
            in_list && /^[[:space:]]*-[[:space:]]*name:/ {
                sub(/^[[:space:]]*-[[:space:]]*name:[[:space:]]*/, "")
                gsub(/["'"'"']/, "")
                print
            }
        ' "$defaults_file")
  fi

  if [[ ${#tools[@]} -eq 0 ]]; then
    warn "no go tools to upgrade — skipping"
    return $SKIP_RC
  fi

  # Install into ~/.local/bin (the blessed dir), not ~/go/bin or the mise
  # toolchain dir. Upgrade = re-install at @latest regardless of the version
  # pinned in defaults (install-vs-upgrade split — defaults pin for fresh
  # installs, this moves them forward). See docs/this_repo/tool-managers.md.
  # GOPATH pinned to ~/.local/share/go (XDG data) so the build-time module cache
  # doesn't repopulate ~/go — mirrors dot_config/shell/02_legacy_tools.sh and the
  # go_tools ansible role.
  export GOBIN="$HOME/.local/bin"
  export GOPATH="$HOME/.local/share/go"

  local any_fail=0
  for t in "${tools[@]}"; do
    # Strip any @version pin (module paths never contain '@') and go to @latest.
    local pkg="${t%@*}"
    info "Upgrading go tool: $pkg@latest"
    _run "$go_cmd" install "$pkg@latest" || any_fail=1
  done
  return "$any_fail"

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:27:37Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm tracing through the chezmoi upgrade path in the dotfiles: the `just upgrade-go` command runs a shell script that iterates through Go tools, strips version pins, and installs the latest versions for each package.

The user's question is whether pushing a new version automatically updates chezmoi. The answer is no, for two key reasons. First, pushing to `main` alone isn't sufficient — Go's `@latest` resolver only recognizes published semver tags, not commits on the main branch, so you need to explicitly create and push a git tag for the new version. Second, `chezmoi apply` and the ansible tasks only install binaries when they don't already exist; once a binary is present, the idempotency guard prevents re-running the install step, so existing installations won't automatically upgrade even if you push new code.

To actually move an existing machine to a new version, you need to run `just upgrade-go`, which bypasses the version pin and installs `@latest`. So the complete workflow is: commit and push to main, create and push a git tag for the new version, then run `just upgrade-go` on each host (or use `fleet` for cross-host deployment). The version pin in defaults/main.yml only applies to fresh installations, not upgrades. I should also watch out for PATH shadowing to ensure the new binary takes precedence. So the pin stays as a floor—new installs get v0.1.0, then users run `just upgrade-go` to reach the latest. If they want fresh boxes to land on v0.2.0 directly, bumping the pin is optional, but the role's design keeps the pin stable and routes upgrades through the dedicated command. I should also check if there's a fleet mechanism for cross-host upgrades, maybe something like `fleet exec --login -- just upgrade-go`, though I'll keep it flexible since the exact fleet command might vary. Now I'll structure the response with the verified commit details, explain how chezmoi handles the dotfiles sync, and walk through the exact steps to push and tag the release.
</details></think>

## chezmoi 會自動追隨最新版嗎？→ **不會**,有兩道關卡

我看了你 dotfiles 裡的實際設定,`translate` 是透過 `go_tools` ansible role 裝的,pin 在 `dot_ansible/roles/go_tools/defaults/main.yml`:

```yaml
go_tools:
  - name: github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@v0.1.0
    binary: translate
```

**關卡 1 — push `main`沒用,要打 tag。**
role 和升級腳本都用 `go install …@latest`,而 `@latest` 解析到的是**最新的 git semver tag**,不是 `main`。所以我剛剛那個 commit `d64a45f` 在你 `git tag && push tag` 之前,對任何安裝完全隱形。(這跟你 `AGENTS.md` 的 release 章節一致。)

**關卡 2 — `chezmoi apply` / ansible 是 install-only,永遠不升級已裝的 binary。**
`go_tools/tasks/main.yml` 的安裝任務有 `creates: ~/.local/bin/translate` 這個 idempotency guard:

> The `creates:` guard makes this install-only + idempotent — version bumps go through `just upgrade-go`, never a re-apply.

也就是說,只要機器上已經有 `translate`,`chezmoi apply` 就**整個跳過**它 —— 即使你已經 tag 了新版。`defaults/main.yml` 的 `@v0.1.0` 只是**全新機器的安裝地板 (fresh-install floor)**。

**關卡 3 — 真正把既有機器推到新版的,只有 `just upgrade-go`。**
`Justfile:446` → `./scripts/upgrade_tools.sh go` → `cat_go()`:它會**剝掉 `@v0.1.0` pin**,對每個 go tool 跑 `go install <pkg>@latest`。這是唯一會把新 tag 拉到已安裝主機的動作。

## 所以要讓這次 bilingual 功能上到你的機器,流程是:

1. ✅ commit(`d64a45f`,已完成)

5. ⚠️ 注意 PATH 遮蔽:確認沒有更早的 `translate`(例如 `~/.dotfiles/bin`)蓋掉 `~/.local/bin` —— 這正是 `TODO.md` P2 + 那條 pitfall 講的。

**可選**:`defaults/main.yml` 的 floor 仍是 `@v0.1.0`。role 註解明說「升級不要動 pin」,所以正規做法是保持不動、靠 `just upgrade-go`。但若你想讓**全新的機器**一裝就直接拿到 bilingual,可以把 floor bump 到 `@v0.2.0`(那是 dotfiles repo 的另一個 commit)。

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./cmd/version.go`

```text
1	package cmd
2	
3	import (
4		"runtime/debug"
5		"strings"
6	)
7	
8	// buildVersion reports a version string derived from the Go build info, so
9	// `translate --version` distinguishes a freshly-built dev binary from a stale
10	// installed one. For `go install <path>@<ver>` it shows the module version; for
11	// a local `go build`/`go install .` off a working tree it shows the VCS revision,
12	// a "+dirty" marker when the tree had uncommitted changes, and the build time.
13	func buildVersion() string {
14		info, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
15		if !ok {
16			return "(unknown; no build info)"
17		}
18		ver := info.Main.Version // e.g. "v0.1.0" or "(devel)"
19		var rev, when string
20		dirty := false
21		for _, s := range info.Settings {
22			switch s.Key {
23			case "vcs.revision":
24				rev = s.Value
25			case "vcs.time":
26				when = s.Value
27			case "vcs.modified":
28				dirty = s.Value == "true"
29			}
30		}
31		var b strings.Builder
32		b.WriteString(ver)
33		if rev != "" {
34			if len(rev) > 12 {
35				rev = rev[:12]
36			}
37			b.WriteString(" (" + rev)
38			if dirty {
39				b.WriteString("+dirty")
40			}
41			b.WriteString(")")
42		}
43		if when != "" {
44			b.WriteString(" commit " + when)
45		}
46		return b.String()
47	}
48
```
</details></tool-use>

So when installed via `go install pkg@v0.3.0`, the version gets set correctly to `v0.3.0`, but when built from an extracted tarball without git history, `Main.Version` falls back to `(devel)` with no VCS metadata, which means `translate --version` would show that instead.

The Homebrew formula needs to inject the version somehow. The standard approach would be using linker flags to set a version variable, but translate doesn't expose one—it relies entirely on `ReadBuildInfo()`. Homebrew formulas typically download the tarball and build with `go build`, which can't set `Main.Version` without VCS data or a version variable to inject via ldflags. The challenge is finding a way to make the version available during the build without modifying translate's source code. The issue is that `ReadBuildInfo` shows `(devel)` for non-`go install` builds even with VCS info stamped, so both Homebrew tarballs and goreleaser builds would display `(devel)` rather than a clean version string — a poor user experience. Since translate explicitly chose to avoid a version constant and rely on ReadBuildInfo instead, the documentation should acknowledge this design decision and recommend adding a version variable as the pragmatic solution for Homebrew compatibility.

Installs today via 'go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest' into ~/.local/bin. Single maintainer, low star count.

- How the formula should set the version given the app uses ReadBuildInfo (Main.Version = "(devel)" from a tarball) — is there a supported way, or must the app add a ldflags-injectable var? Be specific.

```text
cmd/version.go:8:// buildVersion reports a version string derived from the Go build info, so
cmd/version.go:9:// `translate --version` distinguishes a freshly-built dev binary from a stale
cmd/version.go:10:// installed one. For `go install <path>@<ver>` it shows the module version; for
cmd/version.go:14:	info, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
cmd/version.go:18:	ver := info.Main.Version // e.g. "v0.1.0" or "(devel)"
internal/engine/llm.go:465:	httpReq.Header.Set("anthropic-version", "2023-06-01")
```

```text
=== backlog/release-binaries.md ===
# Ship prebuilt release binaries (goreleaser + GitHub Releases)

**Status**: P? — needs a spike
**Effort**: L
**Related**: `TODO.md` P? · `.goreleaser.yaml` (none yet) · chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl` in the dotfiles repo · [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md)

## Context

2026-07, surfaced while making translate a "resident binary" in the chezmoi
dotfiles. Chosen install path for now is **`go install
github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest`** into `~/.local/bin` (GOBIN-pinned).
That requires a Go toolchain on every host. The dotfiles fleet is multi-host /
multi-arch (this host is `macos_intel`/amd64; others differ). Prebuilt release
binaries would let hosts **without** Go install via chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal`
(archive-file, templated per OS/arch) — the same mechanism the dotfiles already
use for oh-my-zsh etc.

## Investigation

- Binary is **pure Go, no cgo** (`modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go), so
  `GOOS`/`GOARCH` cross-compilation is trivial — a single goreleaser run can emit
  darwin/{amd64,arm64} + linux/{amd64,arm64} archives.
- Current binary size ~22 MB (dictionary is NOT embedded — see dict-bundling).
- chezmoi external pattern (from the dotfiles `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl`):
  `type = "archive-file"` with a URL templated on `{{ .chezmoi.os }}` /
  `{{ .chezmoi.arch }}`, `refreshPeriod`, extract the single binary to
  `~/.local/bin`.

## Options considered

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| A. `go install @version` (current) | zero release infra; always builds for the host | needs Go toolchain per host; version pin is a git tag |
| B. goreleaser + Releases + `.chezmoiexternal` archive | no toolchain on target; multi-arch; can attach dict DB | release pipeline to maintain; must tag + run CI on each version |
| C. Homebrew tap | `brew`-native upgrade story | tap repo to maintain; macOS/Linuxbrew only |

## Current blocker / open questions

- Is `go install` "good enough"? Go is present on all current hosts (mise), so B
  is only needed if a Go-less host joins the fleet.
- If we do B, should the dictionary DB ship as a release asset (ties this doc to
  [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md))?

## Decision (if any)

`2026-07 deferred` — `go install` covers all current hosts. Revisit B if a
Go-less host needs translate, or once versioned releases are wanted for their own
sake.

## References

- goreleaser: https://goreleaser.com/
- chezmoi externals: https://www.chezmoi.io/reference/special-files-and-directories/chezmoiexternal-format/
=== find any version usage ===

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://docs.brew.sh/How-to-Create-and-Maintain-a-Tap`

```text
Rules (quoted excerpts ≤125 chars) and concise restatement:

- Repo name (GitHub): "we recommend that the repository’s name start with `homebrew-`"
  - Paraphrase: For GitHub-hosted taps, name the Git repo with a homebrew- prefix (recommended) so brew tap works easily.

- How to add/tap a GitHub repo: "`$ brew tap user/repository`"
  - Paraphrase: Running brew tap USER/REPO adds the GitHub repo as a tap (user = GitHub username, repository = repo name).

- How users install a formula from a tap: "Users can install any of your formulae directly with `brew install user/repository/formula`."
  - Paraphrase: Users may run brew install USER/REPO/FORMULA; Homebrew will auto-add (tap) the repository before installing.

- Where formula files belong: "can be added under either the `Formula` subdirectory, the `HomebrewFormula` subdirectory or the repository’s root." "The first available directory is used, other locations will be ignored."
  - Paraphrase: Place formula files in Formula/, or if absent use HomebrewFormula/, or else the repo root. Homebrew uses the first directory it finds and ignores other locations.

- Formula file naming/extension: "Make sure your formula file is in the correct location (`Formula/` subdirectory) and has the right file extension (`.rb`)."
  - Paraphrase: Formula files must be .rb and located in the correct directory (typically Formula/).

- Cask naming constraint (relevant when adding casks): "casks must have globally unique names to avoid clashes."
  - Paraphrase: If your tap provides casks, pick globally unique cask names (e.g., prefix with your username) to prevent name collisions.

(All quotes are taken verbatim from the supplied content and kept under 125 characters.)
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:36:33Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://docs.brew.sh/Adding-Software-to-Homebrew`

```text
Relevant excerpts (within 125 chars each):

- "you can use the [`brew create` command](/Manpage#create-options-url) to produce a basic version of your formula."
- "SHA-256 checksum of the file downloaded from `url`, calculated by the command `shasum -a 256 <file>`."
- "Next, you will want to go through the [Acceptable Formulae](/Acceptable-Formulae) ... to determine if the software is an appropriate addition to Homebrew."
- "Try installing your formula using `brew install --build-from-source <formula>`"
- "Run `brew audit --strict --new --online <formula>` with your formula."

Concise guidance for a Go CLI formula (paraphrased from above):

- URL: reference the GitHub tag tarball for the release in the url stanza, e.g. a URL that points to the tarball for the tag (GitHub releases tag tarballs are commonly used). Use the version in the formula so the url can interpolate it.

- SHA256: download the tarball locally and compute its checksum with shasum -a 256 <file>, then place that hex string into the sha256 stanza. (See quoted instruction above.)

- Build dependency: declare the build dependency on Go in the formula (example form: depends_on "go" => :build).

- Build invocation: use Homebrew’s Go helpers and build flags. Typical pattern is to call the formula's build stage using std_go_args and pass linker flags (ldflags) to embed version info. For example, in the install block you would invoke go build with std_go_args and set appropriate ldflags to include version and commit data.

- Version handling: set the formula version stanza and use it when interpolating the url; ensure livecheck/version handling follows Homebrew conventions and refer to Versions / Acceptable Formulae docs to confirm whether the package belongs in core or should live in a personal tap. (See quoted Acceptable Formulae guidance.)

- Testing and audit: after implementing, build-from-source locally with the quoted brew install command, add a meaningful test block and run brew test, then run the quoted brew audit command. Fix any audit issues before opening a PR.

- Where to start: use brew create to generate a template formula, then expand it (see the quoted brew create line).

Keep these checks in mind: ensure the software is acceptable per the Acceptable Formulae guidance and that you run local install/test/audit commands before submitting.
```
</details></tool-use>

What this page is about (short)
- The page documents GoReleaser’s publish support for Homebrew Casks (the homebrew_casks section), showing configuration options and examples for generating and pushing Cask files (Casks) into a tap repository.

Full YAML example for homebrew_casks (as shown)
homebrew_casks:
  -
    name: myproject
    alternative_names:
      - myproject@{{ .Version }}
      - myproject@{{ .Major }}
    ids:
      - foo
      - bar
    binaries:
      - myapp
      - myapp2
    # GoReleaser Pro
    app: Foo.app
    manpages:
      - man/myapp.1
      - man/myapp-subcmd.1
    completions:
      bash: completions/myapp.bash
      zsh: completions/myapp.zsh
      fish: completions/myapp.fish
    # Since v2.15
    generate_completions_from_executable:
      executable: "bin/myapp"
      args:
        - completions
      base_name: "myapp"
      shell_parameter_format: cobra
      shells:
        - bash
        - zsh
        - fish
        - pwsh
    url:
      template: "https://github.mycompany.com/foo/bar/releases/download/{{ .Tag }}/{{ .ArtifactName }}"
      verified: "github.com/owner/repo/"
      using: ":homebrew_curl"
      cookies:
        license: "accept-backup"
      referer: "https://example.com/download-page"
      headers:
        - "X-Version: {{ .Version }}"
      user_agent: "MyApp/1.0 (macOS)"
      data:
        format: "dmg"
        platform: "mac"
    commit_msg_template: "Brew cask update for {{ .ProjectName }} version {{ .Tag }}"
    directory: Casks
    caveats: "How to use this binary"
    homepage: "https://example.com/"
    description: "Software to create fast and easy drum rolls."
    skip_upload: true
    custom_block: |
      head "https://github.com/some/package.git"
      ...
    dependencies:
      - cask: some-cask
      - formula: some-formula
    conflicts:
      - cask: some-cask
    # Since v2.13
    hooks:
      pre:
        install: |
          system_command "/usr/bin/defaults", args: ["write", "com.example.app", "key", "value"]
        uninstall: |
          system_command "/usr/bin/defaults", args: ["delete", "com.example.app"]
      post:
        install: |
          system_command "/usr/bin/open", args: ["#{appdir}/MyApp.app"]
        uninstall: |
          system_command "/usr/bin/rm", args: ["-rf", "~/.myapp"]
    service: "myapp.service"
    zap:
      launchctl:
        - "my.fancy.package.service"
      quit:
        - "my.fancy.package"
      login_item:
        - "my.fancy.package"
      trash:
        - "~/.foo/bar"
        - "~/otherfile"
      delete:
        - "~/.foo/bar"
        - "~/otherfile"
    uninstall:
      launchctl:
        - "my.fancy.package.service"
      quit:
        - "my.fancy.package"
      login_item:
        - "my.fancy.package"
      trash:
        - "~/.foo/bar"
        - "~/otherfile"
      delete:
        - "~/.foo/bar"
        - "~/otherfile"
    repository:
      owner: caarlos0
      name: my-repo
      # Since v2.17
      branch: main
      token: "{{ .Env.GITHUB_PERSONAL_AUTH_TOKEN }}"
      token_type: "github"
      pull_request:
        enabled: true
        draft: true
        check_boxes: true
        body: |
          cc/ @foobar
        base:
          owner: goreleaser
          name: my-repo
          branch: main
      git:
        url: 'ssh://[email protected]:repo.git'
        private_key: '{{ .Env.PRIVATE_KEY_PATH }}'
        ssh_command: 'ssh -i {{ .Env.KEY }} -o SomeOption=yes'
    commit_author:
      name: goreleaserbot
      email: [email protected]
      signing:
        enabled: true
        key: "{{ .Env.GPG_SIGNING_KEY }}"
        program: gpg2
        format: openpgp

How the tap repository is targeted (exact config keys)
- The tap repository is specified under the cask’s repository: block. Relevant keys:
  - repository.owner
  - repository.name
  - repository.branch
  - repository.token
  - repository.token_type
  - repository.pull_request.enabled (and pull_request.base for cross-repo PRs)
  - repository.git.url (alternative git push via git.url)
  - repository.git.private_key and repository.git.ssh_command
- Commit author/signing keys are under commit_author.name, commit_author.email, commit_author.signing.key, etc.

Is this for CLI tools or GUI apps?
- The docs show options for both binaries and apps:
  - binary-focused keys: "binaries", "manpages", "completions", "generate_completions_from_executable" (used for CLI tools).
  - app-focused keys: "app: Foo.app", DMG-related url.data.format/platform, and macOS-specific hooks/zap/uninstall.
- In short: the cask config supports both CLI-based binaries and GUI apps; it is not limited to GUI apps.

Difference between homebrew_casks and brews (exact config keys)
- Config key for casks: homebrew_casks
- The older/deprecated formulas area is referenced as homebrew_formulas (the page lists "Homebrew (deprecated)" → customization/publish/homebrew_formulas/).
- So use homebrew_casks for Casks; formulas/brews are handled separately (homebrew_formulas / brews/formulas), not by homebrew_casks.

Two short direct quotes from the page (each <=125 chars)
- "After releasing to GitHub, GitLab, or Gitea, GoReleaser can generate and publish a _Homebrew Cask_ into a repository (_Tap_) that you have access to."
- "Casks are supposed to be signed, even if they are coming from a tap."

Limits and provenance
- All items above are derived only from the provided page content.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:36:19Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read current version.go implementation

`cat cmd/version.go`

```text
package cmd

import (
	"runtime/debug"
	"strings"
)

// buildVersion reports a version string derived from the Go build info, so
// `translate --version` distinguishes a freshly-built dev binary from a stale
// installed one. For `go install <path>@<ver>` it shows the module version; for
// a local `go build`/`go install .` off a working tree it shows the VCS revision,
// a "+dirty" marker when the tree had uncommitted changes, and the build time.
func buildVersion() string {
	info, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
	if !ok {
		return "(unknown; no build info)"
	}
	ver := info.Main.Version // e.g. "v0.1.0" or "(devel)"
	var rev, when string
	dirty := false
	for _, s := range info.Settings {
		switch s.Key {
		case "vcs.revision":
			rev = s.Value
		case "vcs.time":
			when = s.Value
		case "vcs.modified":
			dirty = s.Value == "true"
		}
	}
	var b strings.Builder
	b.WriteString(ver)
	if rev != "" {
		if len(rev) > 12 {
			rev = rev[:12]
		}
		b.WriteString(" (" + rev)
		if dirty {
			b.WriteString("+dirty")
		}
		b.WriteString(")")
	}
	if when != "" {
		b.WriteString(" commit " + when)
	}
	return b.String()
}
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:37:21Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://docs.brew.sh/Formula-Cookbook`

```text
Recommended Go formula pattern (concise, not verbatim from the doc):

- Add a build dependency on Go:
  depends_on "go" => :build

- In install, build with Homebrew helpers and pass ldflags as needed:
  def install
    system "go", "build", *std_go_args(ldflags: "-s -w -X main.version=#{version}")
  end

- Tests: use the formula's test do block to exercise basic functionality in the temp testpath (no interactive input).

Key facts quoted from the documentation (each quote ≤125 chars):

- About std_go_args defaults:
  " -trimpath"
  " -o=#{output}"

- About setting Go build flags:
  "It also provides a convenient way to set `-ldflags`, `-gcflags`, and `-tags`"

- About test block behavior and expectations:
  "The `test do` block automatically creates and changes to a temporary directory which is deleted after run."
  "We want tests that don’t require any user input and test the basic functionality of the application."

Notes (paraphrased):
- std_go_args supplies "-trimpath" and an "-o" that writes the binary to the expected output path; it does not implicitly set ldflags — pass ldflags via std_go_args(ldflags: "...") if you need them.
- Tests should run non-interactively, use testpath, and assert output or file creation (e.g., assert_match or assert_path_exists).
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:37:18Z)**_

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm noticing something odd about the version output — right after tagging v1.2.3, the build showed it as dirty with `vcs.modified = true`, which shouldn't happen immediately after a clean tag. I'm wondering if the `vt` binary itself might be causing this.

Actually, wait — the key insight here is that Go did pick up the tag `v1.2.3` and reported it in Main.Version, which suggests newer Go versions (like go1.26.4) are deriving the version directly from VCS tags during `go build`, rather than always defaulting to "(devel)" like older versions did.           - name: Set up Go

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:37:36Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Read version command implementation

`cat /Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-translate-cli-tui/cmd/version.go`

```text
package cmd

import (
	"runtime/debug"
	"strings"
)

// buildVersion reports a version string derived from the Go build info, so
// `translate --version` distinguishes a freshly-built dev binary from a stale
// installed one. For `go install <path>@<ver>` it shows the module version; for
// a local `go build`/`go install .` off a working tree it shows the VCS revision,
// a "+dirty" marker when the tree had uncommitted changes, and the build time.
func buildVersion() string {
	info, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
	if !ok {
		return "(unknown; no build info)"
	}
	ver := info.Main.Version // e.g. "v0.1.0" or "(devel)"
	var rev, when string
	dirty := false
	for _, s := range info.Settings {
		switch s.Key {
		case "vcs.revision":
			rev = s.Value
		case "vcs.time":
			when = s.Value
		case "vcs.modified":
			dirty = s.Value == "true"
		}
	}
	var b strings.Builder
	b.WriteString(ver)
	if rev != "" {
		if len(rev) > 12 {
			rev = rev[:12]
		}
		b.WriteString(" (" + rev)
		if dirty {
			b.WriteString("+dirty")
		}
		b.WriteString(")")
	}
	if when != "" {
		b.WriteString(" commit " + when)
	}
	return b.String()
}
```
</details></tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I need to check the default ldflags that goreleaser uses, since the project relies on `runtime/debug.ReadBuildInfo()` to access version information, and this is where the critical interaction happens during the build process.

The key insight is that when goreleaser runs `go build` from the source tree (not from a versioned module install), `info.Main.Version` will always be `(devel)` — Go only populates that field when building a module via `go install path@version`. Even with Go 1.18+ VCS stamping, the Main.Version stays `(devel)` for local builds; the VCS info gets embedded in `info.Settings` instead. So goreleaser's default ldflags, which inject values via `-X main.version=...`, would have no effect since the project doesn't define a `main.version` variable — it's reading directly from the build info struct.

The solution is to add a `var version string` in the main package and have the version logic check that first before falling back to ReadBuildInfo. Then goreleaser's default ldflags can stamp it properly. I should verify what goreleaser's actual default ldflags are to confirm they include `-X main.version={{.Version}}`.
</details></think>

Links: [{"title":"Using the `main.version` ldflag – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/resources/cookbooks/using-main.version/"},{"title":"Go – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/customization/builds/builders/go/"},{"title":"goreleaser_build.yml · GitHub","url":"https://gist.github.com/kkdai/d82791da7e1a4ea853231ad839b4154b"}]

I searched the web for your query. Summary of the relevant findings:

- Official GoReleaser docs / cookbook: by default GoReleaser injects three linker flags into Go builds: main.version (from the git tag / snapshot name), main.commit (git SHA), and main.date (RFC3339 timestamp). ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/resources/cookbooks/using-main.version/))

- The Go builder docs show you can customize the build ldflags in .goreleaser.yaml (and that the default main.date is the time goreleaser is run, which you can change for reproducible builds). ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/customization/builds/builders/go/))

- Many example configs and gists also include an extra builtBy field (commonly set to "goreleaser") in ldflags; some projects add -X main.builtBy=goreleaser in their templates or YAML. This is common in examples but not described as one of the three core defaults in the cookbook. ([gist.github.com](https://gist.github.com/kkdai/d82791da7e1a4ea853231ad839b4154b))

- Practical note: the default template used/seen in examples is typically something like:
  -s -w -X main.version={{.Version}} -X main.commit={{.Commit}} -X main.date={{.Date}} (and optionally -X main.builtBy=goreleaser). You can override or extend ldflags in your .goreleaser.yaml. ([gist.github.com](https://gist.github.com/kkdai/d82791da7e1a4ea853231ad839b4154b))

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://goreleaser.com/customization/archive/`

```text
Extracted archives section (key points):

- In GoReleaser v2 the singular "format" was replaced by the plural "formats" (use an array).  
  Quote: "formats: [\"zip\", \"tar.gz\"]"

- Default name_template for non-binary archives:
  Quote: "{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"

- To set only tar.gz use the formats key with a single-item array (tar.gz).

- To override Windows to produce zip use format_overrides with goos: windows and formats: ["zip"].

Minimal working archives config (example):

archives:
  - id: my-archive
    ids:
      - default
    formats: ["tar.gz"]          # use tar.gz for this archive
    name_template: "{{ .ProjectName }}_{{ .Version }}_{{ .Os }}_{{ .Arch }}"
    wrap_in_directory: true
    files:
      - LICENSE
      - README.md
    format_overrides:
      - goos: windows
        formats: ["zip"]        # override Windows to produce zip

Notes (concise):
- Use "formats" (array) in v2 instead of the deprecated singular "format".  
  Quote: "format: \"zip\" # Singular form, single format, deprecated."
- For direct binary publishing set formats to ["binary"].
- If you want only the built binary in tar.gz/gz, provide an empty-matching files glob (e.g. none*) so README/LICENSE aren’t implicitly added.
```
</details></tool-use>

`cd /Users/david/src/tries/2026-07-09-translate-cli-tui; sed -n '54,80p' cmd/root.go`

```text
func NewRootCmd() *cobra.Command {
	root := &cobra.Command{
		Use:   "translate [text...]",
		Short: "Fast terminal translation (CLI + TUI)",
		Long: "translate is a fast CLI/TUI translator.\n\n" +
			"  translate \"hola mundo\" --to en    one-shot\n" +
			"  echo hola | translate --to en       pipe\n" +
			"  translate                           interactive TUI",
		Version:       buildVersion(),
		SilenceUsage:  true,
		SilenceErrors: true,
		// ArbitraryArgs lets bare text fall through to translation while exact
		// subcommands (config, lang, …) still route. Without it, cobra rejects
		// `translate "hola"` as an unknown command once subcommands exist.
		Args: cobra.ArbitraryArgs,
		RunE: runRoot,
	}
	f := root.PersistentFlags()
	f.StringVarP(&flagTo, "to", "t", "", "target language (fuzzy; e.g. en, chinese)")
	f.StringVarP(&flagFrom, "from", "f", "", "source language (\"auto\" to detect)")
	f.StringVar(&flagModel, "model", "", "LLM model id override")
	f.StringVar(&flagProvider, "provider", "", "provider name (e.g. copilot, ollama)")
	f.StringVar(&flagEngine, "engine", "", "engine: auto|<provider>|google|dict")
	f.StringVar(&flagTier, "tier", "", "model tier: default|fast|max")
	f.StringVar(&flagPreset, "preset", "", "LLM prompt style: concise|contextual|dictionary")
	f.StringVar(&flagInstructions, "instructions", "", "extra system-prompt guidance (domain focus, etc.)")
	f.BoolVar(&flagPair, "pair", false, "bidirectional mode: home-language input → --pair-with, else → --to")
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:39:34Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://goreleaser.com/resources/cookbooks/using-main.version/`

```text
Recommended consumption method: use package-level variables in main and have GoReleaser stamp them via ldflags (not runtime/debug.ReadBuildInfo).

Exact default ldflags GoReleaser sets (names quoted verbatim):
- "main.version" — current Git tag (the `v` prefix is stripped) or snapshot name
- "main.commit" — current git commit SHA
- "main.date" — date in the RFC3339 format

The page does not mention using runtime/debug.ReadBuildInfo nor mention the "(devel)" ReadBuildInfo behavior or a workaround.

Recommended Go code snippet (each source line shown verbatim):
"package main"
""
"import \"fmt\""
""
"var ("
"\tversion = \"dev\""
"\tcommit  = \"none\""
"\tdate    = \"unknown\""
")"
""
"func main() {"
"\tfmt.Printf(\"my app %s, commit %s, built at %s\", version, commit, date)"
"}"
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:39:37Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://goreleaser.com/customization/publish/homebrew_casks/`

```text
Short answer: No. GoReleaser does not automatically add a quarantine‑removal postflight hook — you must provide it or sign/notarize.

Evidence (quotes <=125 chars):
- "GoReleaser can sign and notarize both binaries and apps"
- "you still have the option to tell macOS to remove the quarantine bit from the binary on a post install hook:"
- system_command "/usr/bin/xattr", args: ["-dr", "com.apple.quarantine", "#{staged_path}/foo"]
- "App Name is damaged and cannot be opened"
- "zap:"

What’s required for an unsigned CLI binary in a cask to run on macOS (from the page):
- Either have the app/binary properly signed and notarized (recommended), or
- Remove the quarantine bit at install time (example: a post-install hook that runs xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine), or
- Instruct users to run the xattr command themselves (via caveats) — otherwise users may see the "App Name is damaged and cannot be opened" alert.

Note: The page also warns that using xattr bypasses macOS security and may be disabled in future macOS versions.
```
</details></tool-use>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
So when I set `Version:` in Cobra, calling `translate --version` will output something like `translate version v0.3.0`. The key is that `Version:` evaluates `buildVersion()` at command construction time, which reads the ldflags variable I'd inject, so the test would pick up that injected version.

To make ldflags injection work, I need to modify `buildVersion()` to check a package-level `var version string` that gets set via ldflags. The ldflags path would be `-X github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/cmd.version=...` with the value. Since Homebrew's `#{version}` evaluates to `0.3.0` (stripping the leading `v` from the tag), I'd inject either `v#{version}` to match the tag format or just `#{version}` for the plain version number.

I should use `std_go_args(ldflags:)` in the Ruby formula, which is the modern shorthand when a local `ldflags` variable exists. However, there's an important limitation: `runtime/debug.BuildInfo.Main.Version` can't be set via ldflags because it's synthesized by the Go toolchain during module-aware builds, not a regular package variable. When building from an extracted tarball without VCS metadata, `Main.Version` always shows as `(devel)` with no way to override it through build flags.

The idiomatic solution is to add an injectable `var version` in the source code and have the Homebrew formula pass the version via `-X` ldflags — this is what essentially all Go formulae do rather than relying on `Main.Version`. There's also a workaround where some formulae skip extracting the tarball entirely and build directly from the repository, though that has its own constraints.

For computing the SHA256, I can mention using `brew fetch --force` or piping the URL through `curl` and `shasum`. On the LICENSE front, since this is a personal tap, the `license` stanza is optional (though strict audits would flag it), so I'd recommend either adding a LICENSE file and setting it to MIT, or simply omitting the stanza altogether.

The Go version constraint is worth noting—go.mod specifies 1.26.4, so Homebrew's Go formula needs to match or exceed that version. There's also the vendoring consideration: building from a tarball normally requires network access to fetch Go module dependencies, but Homebrew's sandbox blocks that during installation for core formulae, so either the dependencies need to be vendored or fetched as resources. ules to be vendored or use `go mod` resources because the build sandbox blocks network. For a PERSONAL tap, `brew install` still runs in a sandbox that blocks network by default? Let me recall: Homebrew's sandbox does block network during the install phase. So `go build` fetching modules from proxy.golang.org would FAIL in the sandbox.

This is a critical gotcha. How do core Go formulae handle it? They either:
1. Commit a `vendor/` directory in the source tarball (so `go build` uses vendored deps, no network). If `vendor/` exists, `go build` uses it automatically and needs no network.
2. Or declare each module as a `go_resource`/`resource` (old style, deprecated) — modern approach is vendoring.

So for building-from-source from a GitHub tag tarball without network in the sandbox, the tarball must include a `vendor/` directory. The project currently doesn't vendor, so I'd need to either run `go mod vendor` and commit it before tagging so the tarball includes it, then use `go build -mod=vendor` to work offline, or find a way to disable the sandbox network restriction — though Homebrew always sandboxes by default and there's no straightforward per-formula override. Let me actually look at a real Homebrew Go formula to understand how this works in practice — something like `gh` or `fzf` — to see whether the sandbox truly blocks network access during the build phase or if there's some exception I'm missing. I need to verify whether Homebrew's sandbox actually blocks network access during Go builds, and check if this project includes vendored dependencies.
</details></think>

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'm verifying whether recent versions of goreleaser automatically add quarantine removal to macOS casks, since I recall there was a change in v2.x. The documentation seems to indicate it doesn't happen automatically, but I want to confirm this carefully before finalizing the configuration. The key distinction is that GoReleaser deprecated `brews:` in favor of `homebrew_casks:`, but Homebrew itself still supports formulas in personal taps just fine. For a cask distributing an unsigned binary, I need to add a post-install hook to strip the quarantine attribute, otherwise users hit the "damaged and cannot be opened" error. I'm wondering whether formula-installed binaries also get quarantined or if that's cask-specific. Casks intentionally apply the quarantine attribute as policy, which triggers Gatekeeper to block unsigned binaries unless the attribute is stripped in a post-install hook. For a single-maintainer unsigned CLI, formulas are actually more ergonomic since they skip quarantine entirely, but GoReleaser deprecated formula support. I need to weigh using the recommended `homebrew_casks` approach with a quarantine-stripping hook against keeping the simpler but deprecated `brews` option, and I'm leaning toward the future-proof cask route.

The postflight hook needs to call xattr to remove the quarantine attribute from the staged binary path, and the cask definition should include a `binaries` key to properly symlink the installed binary.

For chezmoi integration, I can use `.chezmoiexternal.toml` with `type = "archive-file"` and template the URL using `.chezmoi.os` and `.chezmoi.arch`, which conveniently align with GoReleaser's default output naming scheme of `darwin`/`linux` and `amd64`/`arm64`. Writing config example... The key insight is that goreleaser strips the `v` prefix from version tags when creating archive filenames, so chezmoi needs to handle both the prefixed tag in the URL and the unprefixed version in the filename. For the version reporting issue, I'll set up a hybrid approach where a hardcoded version variable takes precedence when it's been stamped by ldflags during the build, otherwise falling back to ReadBuildInfo — this way `go install @latest` still reports the correct version, goreleaser builds get the stamped value, and local builds show "(devel)".

I need to write out the Go code snippet that implements this hybrid version detection, then verify that goreleaser's configuration uses the `builds:` key and that the binary output name defaults correctly to the project name. Let me run a quick search to verify this detail and finalize my findings.

<tool-use data-tool-type="search" data-tool-name="WebSearch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebSearch**</summary>
 `goreleaser v2 homebrew_casks automatically adds quarantine removal postflight default 2025 2026`

```text
Web search results for query: "goreleaser v2 homebrew_casks automatically adds quarantine removal postflight default 2025 2026"

Links: [{"title":"Homebrew Casks – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/customization/publish/homebrew_casks/"},{"title":"Announcing GoReleaser v2.10 – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/blog/goreleaser-v2.10/"},{"title":"DeepSeek-Reasonix/.goreleaser.yaml at main-v2 · esengine ... - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/esengine/DeepSeek-Reasonix/blob/main-v2/.goreleaser.yaml"},{"title":"Deprecation of --no-quarantine flag · Homebrew · Discussion #6537","url":"https://github.com/orgs/Homebrew/discussions/6537"},{"title":"GitHub - huangyxi/brew-no-quarantine: Homebrew wrapper to remove the ...","url":"https://github.com/huangyxi/brew-no-quarantine"}]

Short answer — No. GoReleaser v2’s homebrew_casks feature does not silently add a quarantine-stripping postflight by default; it supports adding a post-install hook (which many projects use to run xattr -d com.apple.quarantine), but that hook must be configured (or produced by your release workflow), it is not forced automatically.

Evidence / sources
- GoReleaser docs explain the homebrew_casks publisher and explicitly note you have the option to remove the quarantine bit via a post‑install hook (i.e., it’s an option, not an automatic default). ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/customization/publish/homebrew_casks/))
- homebrew_casks was introduced as the proper way to publish prebuilt macOS artifacts in GoReleaser v2.x (homebrew_casks added in the v2.10+ era). ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/blog/goreleaser-v2.10/))
- Example .goreleaser.yml files (many public projects) show adding a post-install hook that runs /usr/bin/xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine to generate the cask’s postflight block — i.e., you (or your config) add that hook. ([github.com](https://github.com/esengine/DeepSeek-Reasonix/blob/main-v2/.goreleaser.yaml))
- Homebrew itself removed the old --no-quarantine bypass and now expects post‑processing (e.g., manual xattr removal) for unsigned/notarized apps — this change was discussed/answered on Nov 12, 2025 (post‑processing required). That explains why many maintainers add a postflight hook. ([github.com](https://github.com/orgs/Homebrew/discussions/6537))
- Third‑party tools and scripts (and some cask helper projects) insert the postflight xattr removal for you — but these are external helpers, not GoReleaser doing it silently. ([github.com](https://github.com/huangyxi/brew-no-quarantine))

If you want, I can:
- Look at your .goreleaser.yml and show exactly how to add a post-install hook that strips com.apple.quarantine (example snippet).  
- Or search for specific public projects released with goreleaser that do (or do not) include that postflight, to show real examples in 2025–2026. Which would you prefer?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.
```
</details></tool-use>

<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
Test multiple tag values clean-on-HEAD with cache cleared

```bash
test_tag() {
  local tag=$1; local d=/tmp/tt_$2
  rm -rf "$d" && mkdir "$d" && cd "$d"
  printf 'module example.com/tt%s\n\ngo 1.26.4\n' "$2" > go.mod
  cp /tmp/vtest/main.go .
  git init -q && git config user.email t@t.com && git config user.name t && git add -A && git commit -qm init
  git tag "$tag"
  go clean -cache 2>/dev/null
  local out; out=$(go build -o /tmp/o . && /tmp/o | head -1)
  printf '%-10s -> %s\n' "$tag" "$out"
}
test_tag v1.0.0 a
test_tag v3.1.0 b
test_tag v0.3.0 c
test_tag v2.0.0 d
test_tag v0.1.0 e
test_tag v1.2.3 f
```

```text
v1.0.0     -> Main.Version: v1.0.0
v3.1.0     -> Main.Version: v0.0.0-20260720064057-c4740984ff4b
v0.3.0     -> Main.Version: v0.3.0
v2.0.0     -> Main.Version: v0.0.0-20260720064109-828aeacd9e86

The key insight is Go's semantic import versioning rule: for a module path without a major version suffix (like `example.com/tt`), only v0.x.x and v1.x.x tags are valid. Any v2+ tags are incompatible with that module path and get rejected, forcing Go to fall back to pseudo-versions instead. That's why v2.0.0 and v3.1.0 are being ignored while the v1.x.x tags work as expected.

For the real project with `github.com/daviddwlee84/translate` (also no suffix) and only v0.x.x tags, those are all valid, so a clean build will correctly stamp v0.3.0. I'm also realizing that `go install pkg@version` pulls from the module cache or proxy, which means vcs.* settings won't be present since VCS stamping only happens when building from an actual VCS working tree.

Now I'm thinking through the two main scenarios: when you run `go install m@v`, you get Main.Version set to that version but no vcs metadata. But when you do `go build` or `go install .` in a git checkout with the default buildvcs=auto, if HEAD is on a valid semver tag, you get the tag as Main.Version plus the full vcs.revision, time, and modified status.

If HEAD isn't on a tag (or the tag doesn't match the module's major version), you get a pseudo-version instead.  `goreleaser default ldflags -s -w -X main.version main.commit main.date main.builtBy`

```text
Web search results for query: "goreleaser default ldflags -s -w -X main.version main.commit main.date main.builtBy"

Links: [{"title":"Using the `main.version` ldflag – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/resources/cookbooks/using-main.version/"},{"title":"goreleaser/.goreleaser.yaml at main - GitHub","url":"https://github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser/blob/main/.goreleaser.yaml"},{"title":"Go – GoReleaser","url":"https://goreleaser.com/customization/builds/builders/go/"},{"title":"ldflags default includes Commit instead of ShortCommit #1264","url":"https://github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser/issues/1264"},{"title":"Using ldflags to Set Version Information for Go Applications","url":"https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/using-ldflags-to-set-version-information-for-go-applications"}]

Summary of what I found

- GoReleaser injects link-time flags by default including -s -w and variables for version/commit/date. The project docs describe main.version, main.commit and main.date being set (version comes from the git tag/snapshot, commit is the git SHA, date in RFC3339). ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/resources/cookbooks/using-main.version/))

- The GoReleaser repository’s own .goreleaser.yaml shows the default ldflags string used for builds: -s -w -X main.version={{.Version}} -X main.commit={{.Commit}} -X main.date={{.CommitDate}} -X main.builtBy=goreleaser -X main.treeState={{.IsGitDirty}}. (So in practice GoReleaser adds main.builtBy and main.treeState in addition to the version/commit/date variables.) ([github.com](https://github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser/blob/main/.goreleaser.yaml))

- You can override these ldflags in your .goreleaser.yaml build section if you want different names or values. The build docs explain how to customize ldflags and the date/mod_timestamp behavior for reproducible builds. ([goreleaser.com](https://goreleaser.com/customization/builds/builders/go/))

- Note: historically there was an issue where the default used the full commit SHA (Commit) rather than a short commit; if you need a short commit you may need to set the template to ShortCommit explicitly. ([github.com](https://github.com/goreleaser/goreleaser/issues/1264))

- The -s -w flags are the standard go linker flags that strip the symbol table and DWARF to reduce binary size. If you want symbol/debug info in your binaries, remove those flags. ([digitalocean.com](https://www.digitalocean.com/community/tutorials/using-ldflags-to-set-version-information-for-go-applications))

Do you want:
- the exact snippet you can paste into your .goreleaser.yaml to customize ldflags, or
- me to check a specific repo/config for what ldflags it will actually use?

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
Looking at the `std_go_args` function, it takes an output path and optional parameters for ldflags, gcflags, and tags. By default it returns just the trimpath and output flags, only injecting ldflags when explicitly passed—so there's no automatic version injection happening.

GoReleaser's documented defaults inject three ldflags: `main.version` (the tag with `v` stripped), `main.commit`, and `main.date`. Their own repo config adds two more (`main.builtBy` and `main.treeState`), but those aren't part of the standard defaults.

When using `go install pkg@version`, the resulting binary gets the version set but has no VCS metadata because the build happens from the module cache rather than a live repository checkout. Running `go version -m` on such a binary shows the module info and build settings without any `vcs.*` keys—that's the expected behavior.

For this project, the key issue is that the version variable needs to live in package `cmd` (where `version.go` already exists), not in `main`, which means the ldflags path must be `-X github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/cmd.version=...` instead of the typical `main.version`. I'll go with option (a)—adding the var to `cmd` and adjusting the ldflags accordingly, since that's the minimal change.

The pattern is straightforward: declare `var version string` at the package level, then add an early return in `buildVersion()` that checks if version was injected at build time; if it's empty, fall back to reading the Go build info as before.

For the Homebrew formula, I'm setting the ldflags to inject the version with the `v` prefix since Homebrew's version variable doesn't include it.

For goreleaser, I'm overriding the default build configuration to use `{{.Tag}}` instead of `{{.Version}}` to preserve the leading `v` in the version string, since the version variable is in the cmd package rather than main.

The key insight is that without a package variable, there's no way for either Homebrew or goreleaser to make `go build` report the actual git tag at build time. For Homebrew's standard tarball builds, ldflags are the practical solution since there's no git history available. GoReleaser, on the other hand, runs `go build` directly in the repository at the tagged commit, so even without explicit ldflags, the version could theoretically be stamped by buildvcs if the module variable is set up correctly.

However, relying on buildvcs stamping in GoReleaser is fragile—dirty working trees, major version suffixes, or shallow clones in CI can break it. The robust approach is to use ldflags with a version variable, which is what GoReleaser is designed to do anyway. There's a caveat with shallow clones in CI environments where the tag might not be visible to buildvcs, even though GoReleaser builds in the actual repository with `-trimpath` enabled. I need to run a quick test to verify whether `-trimpath` actually strips the vcs stamp, since that's a detail that could shift my understanding of why Homebrew builds show "(devel)".
</details></think>

_**Agent - sidechain (2026-07-20 06:46:04Z)**_

You are a pragmatic release engineer. A single-maintainer, low-star pure-Go CLI (already installable via 'go install @latest' and via a chezmoi go_tools ansible role) wants Homebrew installability.

Research found:
[1] Distribute translate via a personal source-building tap. Create repo github.com/daviddwlee84/homebrew-tap (the "homebrew-" prefix is required and stripped): `brew tap daviddwlee84/tap` → that repo; users then run `brew install daviddwlee84/tap/translate`. Put Formula/translate.rb pointing url at the GitHub tag tarball (…/archive/refs/tags/v0.3.0.tar.gz), with sha256, `depends_on "go" => :build`, and an install block using `system "go","build",*std_go_args(ldflags:)`. std_go_args auto-adds -trimpath and -o bin/translate, so no bin.install needed. Homebrew's sandbox is filesystem-only (network allowed), so no vendoring is required — go build fetches modules at install time (confirmed by fzf/gh). CRITICAL version caveat: `debug.BuildInfo.Main.Version` is synthesized by the Go toolchain only for `go install pkg@ver`; it is NOT linker-settable, so a tarball build reports "(devel)". There is no supported way to inject it — you MUST add a `var version` to package cmd and override buildVersion(), then have the formula pass `-X …/cmd.version=v#{version}` (exactly how fzf/gh/lazygit do it).
Gotchas: Main.Version is NOT injectable. `runtime/debug.BuildInfo.Main.Version` is populated by the Go toolchain ONLY during module-aware `go install path@version` (resolved via proxy/VCS). It is a synthesized value, not a linker symbol, so no `-ldflags -X`, `-buildvcs`, or any `go build` flag can set it. Building from an extracted tag tarball always yields "(devel)", and since the tarball has no .git there are no vcs.revision/vcs.time settings either — you get a bare "(devel)". The ONLY fix is a source change: add `var version` and inject it. This is exactly why fzf/gh/lazygit all use `-X main.version=#{version}` rather than relying on Main.Version. | The -X path must be the full import path of the package holding the var: `github.com/daviddwlee84/translate/cmd.version` (var lives in package cmd, not main). A wrong path silently no-ops (no build error, version stays empty). | Homebrew's `version` strips the leading v: for url `.../v0.3.0.tar.gz`, `#{version}` == `0.3.0`. Inject `v#{version}` if you want the binary to print `v0.3.0` (matching the old Main.Version formatting); otherwise it prints `0.3.0`. Keep the test assertion consistent with whichever you pick. | Repo naming: `brew tap USER/NAME` maps to `github.com/USER/homebrew-NAME`. Name the repo `homebrew-tap` → tap name is `daviddwlee84/tap` → install is `brew install daviddwlee84/tap/translate`. Docs phrase the prefix as 'recommended', but it is required for the shorthand; without it you must tap via an explicit git URL. | No vendoring needed: Homebrew's build sandbox (macOS sandbox-exec) restricts filesystem writes, NOT network, so `go build` downloads modules from the Go proxy at install time. Confirmed because homebrew-core fzf and gh build from source with no vendor/ and no resources. (lazygit uses `-mod=vendor` only because it commits a vendor/ dir.) The project currently has no vendor/ and the v0.3.0 tag doesn't include one — that's fine. If you ever want hermetic/offline builds, run `go mod vendor`, commit it before tagging, and add `-mod=vendor` to the build args. | std_go_args already supplies `-trimpath` and `-o <prefix>/bin/translate` (output defaults to the formula name), so you do NOT need a manual `bin.install`. Just call `system "go", "build", *std_go_args(ldflags:)`. | go.mod declares `go 1.26.4`, so Homebrew's `go` build dependency must be >= 1.26.4 (fine as of mid-2026, but note it if a user is on an old brew go). | License: the repo currently has NO LICENSE file (README only cites ECDICT's MIT for bundled data). `license "MIT"` in the formula is optional for a personal tap and won't block install, but `brew audit --strict` will complain if the stanza doesn't match an actual LICENSE file. Add a real LICENSE to the repo, or omit the `license` line. | GitHub auto-generated archive tarballs (…/archive/refs/tags/…) are what homebrew-core itself uses and are stable per tag, but they are regenerated on the fly — always pin the sha256 you computed; never assume it. | `brew install --HEAD` (if you add a `head` block) builds from a git checkout where `version` is 'HEAD' and Main.Version is still '(devel)' but VCS settings ARE present. If you add head support, guard the ldflags so you don't inject a literal `vHEAD`.

[2] Empirically verified on go1.26.4 (the project's toolchain). info.Main.Version is a clean "vX.Y.Z" in exactly two cases: (1) `go install path@vX.Y.Z` (proxy resolves the version — no vcs.* settings are recorded), and (2) `go build`/`go install .` in a CLEAN git checkout whose HEAD sits exactly on a semver tag that is VALID for the module's major version (v0.x/v1.x for an unsuffixed path; v2+ needs a /vN suffix). Surprising confirmed result: for module github.com/daviddwlee84/translate, tags v0.1.0–v0.3.0 are valid, so a clean `git clone && checkout vX && go build` DOES stamp "v0.3.0". The "(devel)" problem is specific to building from an extracted tarball (no .git → no VCS → "(devel)", no vcs.* keys) — which is exactly what a standard Homebrew stable build does. `-trimpath` (always added by std_go_args) does NOT strip the VCS stamp. Homebrew's std_go_args injects nothing version-related; goreleaser injects `-s -w -X main.version -X main.commit -X main.date` by default. Robust fix: add `var version string` in package cmd and inject via -ldflags, preferring it over ReadBuildInfo.
Gotchas: Extracted-tarball build (Homebrew stable url, `go build`) has no .git → buildvcs finds nothing → Main.Version="(devel)" and NO vcs.revision/vcs.time/vcs.modified keys exist at all. This is the root cause, not -trimpath. | MAJOR-VERSION TRAP (verified): a tag whose major version is >=2 (e.g. v2.0.0, v3.1.0) is INVALID for a module path without a matching /vN suffix, so `go build` on a clean checkout at that tag silently falls back to a pseudo-version (v0.0.0-<time>-<hash>) instead of the tag. v0.x and v1.x tags stamp correctly. The project is safe today (v0.x) but this bites at v2. | `go install path@vX.Y.Z` sets Main.Version=vX.Y.Z but records NO vcs.* settings (source comes from the module cache/proxy, not a working tree). `go version -m` on such a binary shows the `mod ... vX.Y.Z` line but no vcs lines. Your current buildVersion() therefore prints just the bare version for @version installs and adds the (rev+dirty) suffix only for local checkouts — that's expected. | Clean checkout on tag → "v0.3.0"; DIRTY tree on that tag → "v0.3.0+dirty" with vcs.modified=true; commits AFTER the tag → pseudo "v0.3.1-0.<time>-<hash>"; no tag as ancestor → "v0.0.0-<time>-<hash>". Untracked files (even a build artifact written into the repo dir) count as dirty and flip the +dirty/vcs.modified marker. | Homebrew's `std_go_args(output: bin/name, ldflags: nil, gcflags: nil, tags: nil)` returns only `["-trimpath", "-o=<output>"]` by default and adds `-ldflags=...` ONLY when you pass ldflags:. It injects zero version info — you must supply the -X yourself. | goreleaser's default ldflags set `main.version` to the tag WITH the leading v stripped ({{.Version}} = "0.3.0", {{.Tag}} = "v0.3.0"). If you want the binary to report "v0.3.0", use {{.Tag}} or re-add the v. | goreleaser builds inside the real git repo, so buildvcs could in principle stamp the tag even without a var — but this is fragile (dirty tree, shallow clones missing tags, v2+ suffix rule); goreleaser's ldflags injection exists precisely to make it deterministic. Don't rely on buildvcs for releases. | cgo/CGO_ENABLED: modernc.org/sqlite is pure Go, so set CGO_ENABLED=0 in goreleaser for reproducible static builds; Homebrew's Go builds default to whatever the toolchain uses but no cgo is needed here.

[3] Pure-Go cross-compile is trivial (CGO_ENABLED=0, goos darwin/linux × goarch amd64/arm64). For Homebrew: GoReleaser DEPRECATED `brews:`/homebrew_formulas in v2.10 — the current, non-deprecated key is `homebrew_casks:`. There is no CLI-vs-GUI split anymore; a CLI ships as a cask via the `binaries:` stanza. Big gotcha: Homebrew removed the `--no-quarantine` bypass (Nov 2025), so an UNSIGNED cask binary triggers "translate is damaged and cannot be opened" unless you add a post-install `xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine` hook. The tap is targeted via `repository.{owner,name,branch,token}`; token must be a PAT with repo scope on the tap repo (convention: HOMEBREW_TAP_GITHUB_TOKEN) because the default GITHUB_TOKEN cannot push to a second repo. Default ldflags stamp `main.version/commit/date` — but this project reads runtime/debug.ReadBuildInfo, so nothing gets stamped; add `var version` and prefer it, falling back to ReadBuildInfo. GoReleaser's default archive names (darwin/linux + amd64/arm64) line up exactly with chezmoi `.chezmoi.os`/`.chezmoi.arch`, so `.chezmoiexternal` archive-file consumption needs no arch mapping.
Gotchas: `brews:` is DEPRECATED as of GoReleaser v2.10 (message: 'Deprecated in v2.10. Homebrew Casks should be used instead.'). It still works today but is on borrowed time — use `homebrew_casks:`. There is no separate maintained 'formula' publisher anymore. | CASK QUARANTINE: Homebrew casks intentionally set com.apple.quarantine, and Homebrew removed the `--no-quarantine` escape hatch (~Nov 2025). An unsigned/un-notarized binary → macOS shows 'translate is damaged and cannot be opened'. You MUST add a post-install hook running `xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine` (Snippet #1) or sign+notarize (needs a paid Apple Developer ID — overkill for a single maintainer). | Trade-off note: classic Homebrew *formulas* do NOT apply the quarantine bit, so an unsigned formula binary runs with no xattr hack. That's the one ergonomic reason some still use the deprecated `brews:`. If you value zero-Gatekeeper-friction over future-proofing, `brews:` is the pragmatic short-term choice; otherwise `homebrew_casks:` + the hook is the 2026 answer. | ReadBuildInfo interaction: GoReleaser's default ldflags target `main.version/main.commit/main.date`. This project has none of those and reads info.Main.Version instead, so goreleaser builds would report '(devel)'. `-X` on a nonexistent symbol is silently ignored. Fix = add `var version` and prefer it (Snippet #3). | Version string mismatch in URLs: the git TAG is `v0.3.0` (used in the release download PATH) but goreleaser strips the leading `v` from `.Version`, so the archive FILENAME is `translate_0.3.0_...`. chezmoi/curl consumers need `v{{version}}` in the path and `{{version}}` (no v) in the filename. | Token: `repository.token` must reference a PAT (`{{ .Env.HOMEBREW_TAP_GITHUB_TOKEN }}`), NOT the workflow's GITHUB_TOKEN — the auto token is scoped to the translate repo only and will 403 when pushing the cask to homebrew-tap. | v2 archive key change: use `formats: [tar.gz]` (list), not the deprecated singular `format:`. Default `wrap_in_directory` is false, so the binary sits at the archive root → chezmoi `path = "translate"`. | Arch/OS mapping is a non-issue here: goreleaser defaults emit `darwin`/`linux` and `amd64`/`arm64`, which are byte-for-byte what chezmoi's `.chezmoi.os` and `.chezmoi.arch` return. Do NOT retitle Os to `Darwin`/`macOS` or you'll break the chezmoi template. | workflow permissions: `contents: write` is required for the goreleaser-action step to create the Release. | setup-go: prefer `go-version-file: go.mod` over `go-version: stable` since go.mod pins go 1.26.4 and `stable` could lag/lead the toolchain.

Adversarially assess the RECOMMENDED path. Compare three options for THIS project: (A) manual build-from-source personal tap (hand-bump formula url+sha256 per tag), (B) goreleaser + prebuilt binaries + auto-formula-push to tap on tag, (C) homebrew-core submission. Consider: the notability/maintenance bar of homebrew-core; whether prebuilt bottles are worth it vs build-from-source given Go is a build dep either way; how each interacts with the version-stamping issue (does the app NEED a version var?); ongoing maintenance cost; how it coexists with the existing go install + chezmoi paths; and whether a GH Action makes A obsolete. Give a clear recommendation (likely A to start, B when releases get frequent, skip C), with the single most important prerequisite. 'summary' = your final recommendation.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-opus-4-8 2026-07-20 06:46:18Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./cmd/version.go`

```text
1	package cmd
2	
3	import (
4		"runtime/debug"
5		"strings"
6	)
7	
8	// buildVersion reports a version string derived from the Go build info, so
9	// `translate --version` distinguishes a freshly-built dev binary from a stale
10	// installed one. For `go install <path>@<ver>` it shows the module version; for
11	// a local `go build`/`go install .` off a working tree it shows the VCS revision,
12	// a "+dirty" marker when the tree had uncommitted changes, and the build time.
13	func buildVersion() string {
14		info, ok := debug.ReadBuildInfo()
15		if !ok {
16			return "(unknown; no build info)"
17		}
18		ver := info.Main.Version // e.g. "v0.1.0" or "(devel)"
19		var rev, when string
20		dirty := false
21		for _, s := range info.Settings {
22			switch s.Key {
23			case "vcs.revision":
24				rev = s.Value
25			case "vcs.time":
26				when = s.Value
27			case "vcs.modified":
28				dirty = s.Value == "true"
29			}
30		}
31		var b strings.Builder
32		b.WriteString(ver)
33		if rev != "" {
34			if len(rev) > 12 {
35				rev = rev[:12]
36			}
37			b.WriteString(" (" + rev)
38			if dirty {
39				b.WriteString("+dirty")
40			}
41			b.WriteString(")")
42		}
43		if when != "" {
44			b.WriteString(" commit " + when)
45		}
46		return b.String()
47	}
48
```
</details></tool-use>

<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `./backlog/release-binaries.md`

```text
1	# Ship prebuilt release binaries (goreleaser + GitHub Releases)
2	
3	**Status**: P? — needs a spike
4	**Effort**: L
5	**Related**: `TODO.md` P? · `.goreleaser.yaml` (none yet) · chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl` in the dotfiles repo · [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md)
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	2026-07, surfaced while making translate a "resident binary" in the chezmoi
10	dotfiles. Chosen install path for now is **`go install
11	github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@latest`** into `~/.local/bin` (GOBIN-pinned).
12	That requires a Go toolchain on every host. The dotfiles fleet is multi-host /
13	multi-arch (this host is `macos_intel`/amd64; others differ). Prebuilt release
14	binaries would let hosts **without** Go install via chezmoi `.chezmoiexternal`
15	(archive-file, templated per OS/arch) — the same mechanism the dotfiles already
16	use for oh-my-zsh etc.
17	
18	## Investigation
19	
20	- Binary is **pure Go, no cgo** (`modernc.org/sqlite` is pure Go), so
21	  `GOOS`/`GOARCH` cross-compilation is trivial — a single goreleaser run can emit
22	  darwin/{amd64,arm64} + linux/{amd64,arm64} archives.
23	- Current binary size ~22 MB (dictionary is NOT embedded — see dict-bundling).
24	- chezmoi external pattern (from the dotfiles `.chezmoiexternal.toml.tmpl`):
25	  `type = "archive-file"` with a URL templated on `{{ .chezmoi.os }}` /
26	  `{{ .chezmoi.arch }}`, `refreshPeriod`, extract the single binary to
27	  `~/.local/bin`.
28	
29	## Options considered
30	
31	| Option | Pros | Cons |
32	|---|---|---|
33	| A. `go install @version` (current) | zero release infra; always builds for the host | needs Go toolchain per host; version pin is a git tag |
34	| B. goreleaser + Releases + `.chezmoiexternal` archive | no toolchain on target; multi-arch; can attach dict DB | release pipeline to maintain; must tag + run CI on each version |
35	| C. Homebrew tap | `brew`-native upgrade story | tap repo to maintain; macOS/Linuxbrew only |
36	
37	## Current blocker / open questions
38	
39	- Is `go install` "good enough"? Go is present on all current hosts (mise), so B
40	  is only needed if a Go-less host joins the fleet.
41	- If we do B, should the dictionary DB ship as a release asset (ties this doc to
42	  [dict-bundling.md](dict-bundling.md))?
43	
44	## Decision (if any)
45	
46	`2026-07 deferred` — `go install` covers all current hosts. Revisit B if a
47	Go-less host needs translate, or once versioned releases are wanted for their own
48	sake.
49	
50	## References
51	
52	- goreleaser: https://goreleaser.com/
53	- chezmoi externals: https://www.chezmoi.io/reference/special-files-and-directories/chezmoiexternal-format/
54
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
===AGENTS.md (release/version section)===
2:## Releasing & versioning
3-
4:`translate --version` is derived from Go build info (`cmd/version.go` →
5:`debug.ReadBuildInfo`), so **the version *is* the git tag** — there is no version
6-constant to edit. `go install …@latest` and the chezmoi/ansible pin both resolve
7:to a published **tag**, not to `main`; a commit on `main` does **not** reach an
8:installed binary until it is pushed **and** tagged.
9-
10-SemVer, pre-1.0 (`0.y.z`):
11-
12-- backward-compatible feature (new mode/flag, e.g. `--learn`) → bump **minor** (`0.1.0 → 0.2.0`)
13-- bug fix only → bump **patch** (`0.2.0 → 0.2.1`)
14:- breaking CLI/flag/config change → still a minor bump while `0.x`; call it out in the tag message
15-- cut **`v1.0.0`** only once the CLI/flags/config surface is considered stable
16-
17-To ship the current `main` to this machine via chezmoi:
18-
19-1. `go test ./...` green, working tree clean.
20-2. `git push origin main`
21:3. `git tag -a vX.Y.Z -m "<highlights>"` && `git push origin vX.Y.Z`
22:4. verify: `go install github.com/daviddwlee84/translate@vX.Y.Z && translate --version` → `vX.Y.Z`
23-5. install on this machine (dotfiles repo): run `just upgrade-go` — it installs each go
24:   tool at `@latest` (= the new tag) into `~/.local/bin`. The `go_tools` pin in
25-   `dot_ansible/roles/go_tools/defaults/main.yml` is only the *fresh-install floor*
26-   (its header says **don't** bump it for upgrades); `chezmoi apply`/ansible is
27-   install-only and won't move an already-installed binary. Make sure no stale copy
28-   shadows `~/.local/bin` earlier on `PATH` (e.g. a hand-built one in `~/.dotfiles/bin`).
29-
30:There is no CHANGELOG; the annotated tag message is the release note. Keep commit
31:subjects in `feat(scope): …` / `fix(scope): …` form so `git log <prev-tag>..<tag>`
32-reads as a coherent changelog.
33-
34-<!-- project-knowledge-harness:agent-guidance -->
35-<!-- Snippet for the project's agent contract file (AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md /
36-     similar). The bundled scripts/init.sh appends this between sentinel
37-     markers; safe to re-run. -->
38-
39-### Long-term backlog → `TODO.md` + `backlog/`
40-
41-When the user surfaces an idea explicitly **not** being implemented this
42-session (signals: "maybe later", "nice to have", "if I'm interested",
43-"工程量太大需要再評估", "先記下來"), add an entry to [`TODO.md`](TODO.md) using
44:the priority + effort tag schema. Do **not** create new `ROADMAP.md` /
45-`IDEAS.md` / `BACKLOG.md` files — `TODO.md` is the single index.
46-
47-The bundled `scripts/todo-kanban.sh` validates the format. Run it
48-(`scripts/todo-kanban.sh --validate-only TODO.md`) after editing so syntax
49-drift is caught immediately.
50-
51-#### Three ways to add a TODO entry (preferred order)
52-
53-1. **Structured CLI — `scripts/add-todo.sh`** (default):
54-
55-   ```
56-   scripts/add-todo.sh --priority P3 --effort M \
57-     --title "Title" --description "Description"
58-   ```
59-
60-   Inserts a canonically-formatted line into the right `## P*` lane and
61-   re-runs the validator. Add `--backlog` to also scaffold
62-   `backlog/<slug>.md` from the bundled template.
63-
64-2. **Quick capture — `backlog/inbox.md`** (when priority/effort unclear):
65-
66-   ```
67:   echo "- maybe add docs versioning with mike" >> backlog/inbox.md
68-   ```
69-
70-   When the user asks "sweep the inbox", run
71-   `scripts/sweep-inbox.sh`. It prompts for the missing fields per loose
72-   line and calls `add-todo.sh`. Use `--batch` for non-interactive runs
73-   that only formalize lines with parseable `key=value` pairs.
74-
75-3. **Direct edit of `TODO.md`** — fine if the format is fresh; run
76-   `scripts/todo-kanban.sh --validate-only` afterwards.
77-
